questions.stem: Which of the following costs should be capitalized as part of the cost of a self-constructed asset?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Interest on debt incurred to finance construction only after the asset is placed in service

choices.label: B
choices.text: Direct materials, direct labor, and an allocation of overhead including capitalized interest during construction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Only direct materials and direct labor

choices.label: D
choices.text: All costs including general and administrative overhead

questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-30-1 and ASC 835-20, the cost of a self-constructed asset includes direct materials, direct labor, applicable overhead, and interest cost incurred during the construction period (capitalized interest per ASC 835-20). G&A costs are generally expensed unless clearly attributable to construction.

questions.stem: Under ASC 360, what is the first step in testing a long-lived asset (held for use) for impairment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Compare the asset's carrying amount to its fair value

choices.label: B
choices.text: Compare the asset's carrying amount to the sum of undiscounted future cash flows

choices.label: C
choices.text: Calculate the present value of future cash flows

choices.label: D
choices.text: Obtain an independent appraisal

questions.explanation: ASC 360-10-35-17 uses a two-step approach: Step 1 (recoverability test) compares the carrying amount to the sum of undiscounted expected future cash flows. If the carrying amount exceeds undiscounted cash flows, the asset is impaired. Step 2 measures the impairment loss as the excess of carrying amount over fair value.

questions.stem: Equipment costing $120,000 with a salvage value of $20,000 and useful life of 5 years is depreciated using double-declining balance. What is the depreciation expense in Year 2?
questions.explanation: DDB rate = 2 Г— (1/5) = 40%. Year 1 depreciation: $120,000 Г— 40% = $48,000. Book value end of Year 1: $120,000 - $48,000 = $72,000. Year 2 depreciation: $72,000 Г— 40% = $28,800. Note: Under DDB, salvage value is not subtracted from cost to compute the depreciation base, but the asset cannot be depreciated below salvage value.

questions.stem: Under the current ASC 350 guidance (post-ASU 2017-04), how is goodwill impairment measured?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Compare implied fair value of goodwill to its carrying amount (Step 2)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Compare fair value of the reporting unit to its carrying amount; impairment equals the excess of carrying amount over fair value, limited to the goodwill balance

choices.label: C
choices.text: Compare undiscounted cash flows to the carrying amount of the reporting unit

choices.label: D
choices.text: Compare fair value of identifiable net assets to goodwill carrying amount

questions.explanation: ASU 2017-04 simplified goodwill impairment testing by eliminating Step 2. Under the current guidance (ASC 350-20-35-3C), if the carrying amount of a reporting unit exceeds its fair value, an impairment loss is recognized equal to the excess, not to exceed the total goodwill allocated to that reporting unit.

questions.stem: An entity acquires a trademark with no legal, regulatory, or competitive factors that limit its useful life. How should this intangible asset be accounted for?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Amortized over 40 years

choices.label: B
choices.text: Amortized over 15 years

choices.label: C
choices.text: Not amortized, but tested for impairment at least annually

choices.label: D
choices.text: Expensed immediately

questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-30-35-4, an intangible asset with an indefinite useful life is not amortized. Instead, it is tested for impairment at least annually (or more frequently if events indicate potential impairment). An indefinite life means there is no foreseeable limit to the period over which the asset is expected to contribute to cash flows.

questions.stem: Under ASC 410-20, when should an asset retirement obligation (ARO) be recognized?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Only when the asset is retired

choices.label: B
choices.text: When the liability is incurred (typically when the asset is placed in service), at fair value

choices.label: C
choices.text: At the midpoint of the asset's useful life

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only if the retirement cost exceeds 10% of the asset's cost

questions.explanation: ASC 410-20-25-4 requires recognition of an ARO at fair value in the period in which it is incurred, which is typically when the asset is acquired or constructed. The fair value is added to the carrying amount of the related long-lived asset and recognized as a liability. The liability is subsequently accreted to its settlement value over time.

questions.stem: Under ASC 845, when a nonmonetary exchange has commercial substance, at what amount should the acquired asset be recorded?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Book value of the asset given up

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fair value of the asset given up (or fair value of the asset received if more clearly evident)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Average of the book values of both assets

choices.label: D
choices.text: The lower of cost or fair value

questions.explanation: Under ASC 845-10-30-1, if a nonmonetary exchange has commercial substance (the entity's future cash flows are expected to change significantly), the cost of the acquired asset is measured at the fair value of the asset given up plus any cash paid. If the fair value of the asset received is more clearly evident, that value is used instead. A gain or loss is recognized.

questions.stem: Under ASC 730, research and development costs are generally:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Capitalized as intangible assets

choices.label: B
choices.text: Expensed as incurred

choices.label: C
choices.text: Capitalized during the development phase only

choices.label: D
choices.text: Deferred and amortized over the expected benefit period

questions.explanation: ASC 730-10-25-1 requires that research and development costs be expensed as incurred under U.S. GAAP. This includes materials, equipment used only for R&D, personnel costs, and contract services. This differs from IFRS, which permits capitalization of development costs meeting specific criteria.

questions.stem: When a long-lived asset is classified as held for sale under ASC 360-10-45, it should be measured at:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fair value

choices.label: B
choices.text: Net realizable value

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lower of carrying amount or fair value less cost to sell

choices.label: D
choices.text: Carrying amount without further depreciation

questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-35-43, a long-lived asset classified as held for sale is measured at the lower of its carrying amount or fair value less cost to sell. Depreciation ceases once the asset is classified as held for sale. If fair value less cost to sell is lower, an impairment loss is recognized.

questions.stem: Under ASC 350-40, costs incurred during which phase of internal-use software development should be capitalized?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Preliminary project stage

choices.label: B
choices.text: Application development stage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Post-implementation/operation stage

choices.label: D
choices.text: All phases should be capitalized

questions.explanation: ASC 350-40-25-2 requires that costs incurred during the application development stage be capitalized. Costs in the preliminary project stage (conceptual formulation, evaluation of alternatives) and post-implementation stage (training, maintenance) are expensed as incurred. The application development stage begins when management commits to funding and it is probable the project will be completed.

questions.stem: Entity G is constructing a building. Average accumulated expenditures during the year are $600,000. The company has a specific construction loan of $400,000 at 8% and general borrowings with a weighted-average rate of 10%. Actual total interest incurred is $80,000. What amount of interest should be capitalized?
questions.explanation: Avoidable interest = specific borrowing portion ($400,000 Г— 8% = $32,000) + excess expenditures at general rate (($600,000 - $400,000) Г— 10% = $20,000) = $52,000. Capitalized interest = lesser of avoidable interest ($52,000) and actual interest ($80,000) = $52,000.

questions.stem: A machine costs $240,000 with a salvage value of $40,000 and a 5-year useful life. Using the sum-of-the-years'-digits method, what is the depreciation expense in Year 3?

Entity H has equipment with a carrying value of $500,000. Due to a significant decline in market demand, management performs an impairment test. Undiscounted future cash flows are estimated at $420,000. Fair value is $350,000. What impairment loss should be recognized?
questions.explanation: Step 1: Recoverability test вҖ” carrying value $500,000 > undiscounted CF $420,000, so the asset is impaired. Step 2: Impairment loss = carrying value - fair value = $500,000 - $350,000 = $150,000. The undiscounted CFs are only used for the trigger test, not to measure the loss.

questions.stem: Entity J recognizes an ARO of $50,000 (fair value) when a plant is placed in service. The credit-adjusted risk-free rate is 6%. What is the carrying amount of the ARO liability after 3 years?
questions.explanation: The ARO liability is accreted using the credit-adjusted risk-free rate. After 3 years: $50,000 Г— (1.06)^3 = $50,000 Г— 1.191016 = $59,551. Each year's accretion expense: Y1 = $3,000, Y2 = $3,180, Y3 = $3,371.

questions.stem: Entity K exchanges equipment (cost $100,000, accumulated depreciation $60,000, fair value $55,000) for a different machine plus $5,000 cash. The exchange has commercial substance. What gain or loss should Entity K recognize?

choices.label: C
choices.text: $5,000 gain

choices.label: D
choices.text: $0 (deferred)

questions.explanation: Book value of asset given = $100,000 - $60,000 = $40,000. Fair value of asset given = $55,000. Since the exchange has commercial substance, the full gain is recognized. Gain = FV given ($55,000) - BV ($40,000) = $15,000. The new asset is recorded at FV received = $55,000 - $5,000 cash = $50,000.

questions.stem: A reporting unit has a carrying amount of $2,000,000 (including goodwill of $300,000). Its fair value is determined to be $1,850,000. What is the goodwill impairment loss?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-20-35-3C (post-ASU 2017-04), impairment = excess of carrying amount over fair value, limited to goodwill balance. Excess = $2,000,000 - $1,850,000 = $150,000. Since $150,000 < goodwill balance of $300,000, the impairment loss is $150,000.

questions.stem: Entity L acquires a building for $1,000,000 with the following components: Structure 70% (useful life 30 years), Roof 15% (15 years), HVAC system 15% (10 years). All components have zero salvage value. What is total depreciation expense in Year 1 using component depreciation?

Entity M is constructing a warehouse. Expenditures: Jan 1 $200,000; Apr 1 $300,000; Oct 1 $100,000. The company's only borrowing is a 12-month construction loan at 8% annual interest. What is the amount of interest to be capitalized?

Entity N acquired a patent for $600,000 with a remaining legal life of 12 years and estimated useful life of 8 years. After 3 years of amortization, the remaining useful life is revised to 3 additional years. What is the amortization expense in Year 4?
questions.explanation: Initial amortization = $600,000 / 8 = $75,000/year. After 3 years: carrying value = $600,000 - (3 Г— $75,000) = $375,000. Revised remaining life = 3 years. New amortization = $375,000 / 3 = $125,000/year. Change in estimate is applied prospectively.

questions.stem: Entity P sells equipment on July 1. The equipment cost $180,000 with accumulated depreciation of $120,000 as of January 1. Annual straight-line depreciation is $36,000. The equipment is sold for $35,000. What is the gain or loss on disposal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: $7,000 loss

choices.label: D
choices.text: $3,000 gain

questions.explanation: Depreciation Jan-Jun (half year) = $36,000 Г— 6/12 = $18,000. Accumulated depreciation at disposal = $120,000 + $18,000 = $138,000. Book value at disposal = $180,000 - $138,000 = $42,000. Loss = $42,000 - $35,000 = $7,000.

questions.stem: A company purchases land for $500,000 and makes the following expenditures: grading and leveling $30,000; parking lot paving $80,000; permanent landscaping $20,000; fencing $15,000. Which amount should be classified as depreciable land improvements?
questions.explanation: Land improvements with limited useful lives are depreciable: parking lot ($80,000) and fencing ($15,000) = $95,000. Grading and leveling ($30,000) is a cost of preparing the land and is part of the land cost (non-depreciable). Permanent landscaping ($20,000) is also considered part of land (non-depreciable) as it has an indefinite life.

questions.stem: A company acquires land, building, and equipment for a lump sum of $1,200,000. Appraised values are: land $500,000, building $400,000, equipment $100,000. What amount should be recorded for the building?
questions.explanation: In a lump-sum purchase, the total cost is allocated based on relative fair values. Total appraised value = $500,000 + $400,000 + $100,000 = $1,000,000. Building allocation = ($400,000 / $1,000,000) Г— $1,200,000 = $480,000.

questions.stem: A company purchases land with an old building for $900,000. The intention is to demolish the building and construct a new one. Demolition costs are $60,000 and salvage from demolition yields $10,000. What is the cost of the land?
questions.explanation: When land is purchased with the intent to demolish an existing structure, the entire purchase price plus demolition costs less any salvage are charged to land. Land cost = $900,000 + $60,000 вҲ’ $10,000 = $950,000. No amount is allocated to the demolished building.

questions.stem: A machine with a carrying value of $200,000 and remaining useful life of 5 years undergoes a major overhaul costing $75,000 that extends its useful life by 3 years. How should the overhaul cost be accounted for?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Expense immediately as repairs and maintenance

choices.label: B
choices.text: Capitalize and depreciate over the new remaining life of 8 years

choices.label: C
choices.text: Capitalize and depreciate over 3 years only

choices.label: D
choices.text: Record as a deferred charge

questions.explanation: A major overhaul that extends the useful life of an asset is capitalized because it provides future economic benefits beyond the original estimate. The cost is added to the asset and the total carrying value ($200,000 + $75,000 = $275,000) is depreciated over the new total remaining useful life of 8 years (5 original + 3 extension).

questions.stem: Equipment has a carrying value of $600,000, expected undiscounted future cash flows of $550,000, and fair value of $450,000. What impairment loss should be recognized?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 360, the impairment test for long-lived assets is a two-step process. Step 1 (recoverability test): Compare carrying value ($600,000) to undiscounted future cash flows ($550,000). Since $600,000 > $550,000, the asset is impaired. Step 2 (measurement): Impairment loss = Carrying value вҲ’ Fair value = $600,000 вҲ’ $450,000 = $150,000.

questions.stem: Under ASC 350, a company elects to perform a qualitative assessment (Step 0) for goodwill impairment. Which of the following would most likely lead the company to conclude that it is NOT necessary to perform the quantitative impairment test?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The reporting unit's stock price has declined significantly

choices.label: B
choices.text: A key customer contract was recently lost

choices.label: C
choices.text: The reporting unit's fair value substantially exceeded carrying value in the prior year with no subsequent adverse changes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Industry regulatory changes have increased operating costs

questions.explanation: The qualitative assessment (Step 0) allows an entity to skip the quantitative test if, based on qualitative factors, it is more likely than not that the fair value of the reporting unit exceeds its carrying amount. If fair value substantially exceeded carrying value in the most recent test and no adverse changes have occurred, no quantitative test is needed.

questions.stem: In a business combination, a company identifies a customer relationship intangible asset with a fair value of $800,000 and estimated useful life of 10 years. How should this asset be accounted for?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Include in goodwill and test annually for impairment

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recognize separately and amortize over 10 years

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recognize separately but do not amortize since customer relationships have indefinite lives

choices.label: D
choices.text: Expense immediately as an intangible asset cannot arise from customer relationships

questions.explanation: Customer relationship intangible assets meet the contractual-legal or separability criterion for recognition apart from goodwill under ASC 805. Since the asset has a finite useful life (10 years), it is amortized over that period. Only intangible assets with indefinite useful lives are not amortized.

questions.stem: A company pays $200,000 to acquire a franchise with an initial term of 15 years and a renewal option for an additional 15 years at minimal cost. The company intends to renew. Over what period should the franchise right be amortized?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 15 years

choices.label: B
choices.text: 30 years

choices.label: C
choices.text: 40 years (maximum under GAAP)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Indefinite life; do not amortize

questions.explanation: Under ASC 350, the useful life of an intangible asset should include renewal periods if renewal is reasonably certain and can be accomplished at little or no cost. Since the company intends to renew and renewal is at minimal cost, the useful life is 15 + 15 = 30 years.

questions.stem: Under ASC 350-50, costs incurred during which stage of website development should be capitalized?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Planning stage only

choices.label: B
choices.text: Application and infrastructure development stage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Operating stage only

choices.label: D
choices.text: All stages should be capitalized

questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-50, costs incurred during the application and infrastructure development stage are capitalized. This is similar to the treatment for internal-use software (ASC 350-40). Planning stage costs are expensed as incurred. Operating stage costs are generally expensed, except for certain upgrades or enhancements.

questions.stem: A company incurs $300,000 in implementation costs for a cloud computing arrangement (hosting arrangement) that is a service contract. Under ASC 350-40, how should these costs be accounted for?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Expense immediately

choices.label: B
choices.text: Capitalize as an intangible asset and amortize over the hosting arrangement term

choices.label: C
choices.text: Capitalize as a prepaid asset and amortize over the hosting arrangement term

choices.label: D
choices.text: Capitalize as PPE

questions.explanation: Under ASU 2018-15, implementation costs for a cloud computing arrangement that is a service contract (not a software license) are capitalized as a prepaid asset (not an intangible asset) and amortized over the term of the hosting arrangement. The costs follow the same capitalization criteria as internal-use software under ASC 350-40.

questions.stem: A company receives a donation of land from a local government. The land has a fair value of $250,000 on the date of donation. How should the company record this transaction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Debit Land $250,000; Credit Contribution Revenue $250,000

choices.label: B
choices.text: Debit Land $0; no entry needed since there is no cost

choices.label: C
choices.text: Debit Land $250,000; Credit Paid-in Capital - Donations $250,000

choices.label: D
choices.text: Debit Land $250,000; Credit Deferred Revenue $250,000

questions.explanation: Under ASC 958-605 (contributions), a for-profit entity receiving a nonreciprocal transfer (donation) of a nonfinancial asset records the asset at fair value with a credit to contribution revenue (or gain). Government grants to for-profit entities are generally accounted for by analogy to contribution guidance.

questions.stem: A company acquires equipment on April 1, Year 1 for $480,000. Salvage value is $0 and useful life is 10 years. The company uses the half-year convention. What is Year 1 depreciation expense under straight-line?
questions.explanation: Under the half-year convention, assets placed in service during the year receive one-half year of depreciation regardless of the actual date acquired. Annual depreciation = $480,000 / 10 = $48,000. Year 1 depreciation = $48,000 Г— 1/2 = $24,000.

questions.stem: Company A exchanges a machine (cost $100,000, accumulated depreciation $60,000, fair value $55,000) for Company B's machine (fair value $50,000) plus $5,000 cash. The exchange has commercial substance. What gain or loss does Company A recognize?

choices.label: B
choices.text: $5,000 gain

choices.label: D
choices.text: $0 (defer any gain)

questions.explanation: When an exchange has commercial substance, gains and losses are recognized in full. Book value of machine given up = $100,000 вҲ’ $60,000 = $40,000. Fair value of consideration received = $50,000 (machine) + $5,000 (cash) = $55,000. Gain = $55,000 вҲ’ $40,000 = $15,000.

questions.stem: A company recognized a $200,000 impairment loss on equipment in Year 1. In Year 2, conditions improve and the equipment's fair value exceeds its post-impairment carrying value by $120,000. Under U.S. GAAP, what amount of impairment reversal may be recognized?
questions.explanation: Under U.S. GAAP (ASC 360-10-35-20), once an impairment loss is recognized for a long-lived asset to be held and used, the reduced carrying amount becomes the new cost basis and may not be restored. Reversal of impairment losses is prohibited. This differs from IFRS (IAS 36), which permits reversal up to the original carrying amount.

questions.stem: Under ASC 350-40, during which phase of internal-use software development should costs be capitalized?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Preliminary project stage

choices.label: B
choices.text: Application development stage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Post-implementation/operation stage

choices.label: D
choices.text: All three stages

questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-40, costs incurred during the application development stage are capitalized. This includes coding, software configuration, testing, and installation. Costs during the preliminary project stage (conceptual formulation, evaluating alternatives) and post-implementation stage (training, maintenance) are expensed as incurred.

questions.subtopic: Patent Amortization - Legal vs Economic Life
questions.stem: A company acquires a patent for $340,000. The remaining legal life is 17 years, but the patent's economic useful life is estimated at 8 years. What is the annual amortization expense?
questions.explanation: Intangible assets with finite lives are amortized over the shorter of their legal or economic useful life. Since the economic life (8 years) is shorter than the legal life (17 years), amortization = $340,000 / 8 = $42,500 per year.

questions.stem: A mining company acquires mineral rights for $5,000,000. Estimated recoverable reserves are 2,000,000 tons. During Year 1, 350,000 tons are extracted and 300,000 tons are sold. What is the depletion expense recognized in Year 1?
questions.explanation: Depletion is calculated based on units extracted, not units sold. Depletion rate = $5,000,000 / 2,000,000 = $2.50 per ton. Year 1 depletion = 350,000 tons Г— $2.50 = $875,000. The 50,000 unsold tons remain in inventory (with depletion allocated to their cost). The depletion expense of $875,000 is recognized because all extracted units bear their share of depletion cost.

questions.stem: A lessee makes leasehold improvements costing $180,000 at the beginning of a 10-year lease with two 5-year renewal options that are not reasonably certain to be exercised. The improvements have an estimated useful life of 15 years. Over what period should the improvements be depreciated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 10 years

choices.label: B
choices.text: 15 years

choices.label: C
choices.text: 20 years

choices.label: D
choices.text: 5 years

questions.explanation: Leasehold improvements are depreciated over the shorter of their useful life (15 years) or the remaining lease term (10 years, since renewal options are not reasonably certain). Therefore, the improvements should be depreciated over 10 years. Annual depreciation = $180,000 / 10 = $18,000.

questions.stem: A company is constructing a building for its own use. Average accumulated expenditures during the period are $2,000,000. The company has a specific construction loan of $1,500,000 at 8% and general borrowings of $3,000,000 at 6%. What amount of interest should be capitalized?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 835-20, interest capitalization uses the avoidance concept. First, apply the specific borrowing rate: $1,500,000 Г— 8% = $120,000. Remaining expenditures requiring capitalization = $2,000,000 вҲ’ $1,500,000 = $500,000. Apply the weighted average rate of general borrowings: $500,000 Г— 6% = $30,000. Total capitalizable interest = $120,000 + $30,000 = $150,000. Actual total interest = ($1,500,000 Г— 8%) + ($3,000,000 Г— 6%) = $300,000. Since capitalizable amount ($150,000) does not exceed actual interest ($300,000), capitalize $150,000.

questions.stem: Under ASC 932 (Extractive Activities - Oil and Gas), which of the following is true about the successful efforts method?

choices.label: A
choices.text: All exploration costs are capitalized regardless of outcome

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only the costs of successful exploratory wells are capitalized; dry hole costs are expensed

choices.label: C
choices.text: All exploration and development costs are expensed as incurred

choices.label: D
choices.text: Exploration costs are deferred until production begins

questions.explanation: Under the successful efforts method (ASC 932), costs of drilling exploratory wells are initially capitalized as pending determination of whether proved reserves have been found. If the well is dry (unsuccessful), the costs are expensed. If successful, costs remain capitalized. Development costs for proved reserves are always capitalized. The full cost method, by contrast, capitalizes all costs within a cost center.

questions.stem: A machine is purchased for $100,000 with a salvage value of $10,000 and a useful life of 5 years. Using the double-declining balance (DDB) method, what is the depreciation expense in Year 2?
questions.explanation: DDB rate = 2 / 5 years = 40%. Year 1 depreciation = $100,000 Г— 40% = $40,000. Year 1 ending book value = $60,000. Year 2 depreciation = $60,000 Г— 40% = $24,000. Note that salvage value is not deducted from the initial cost under DDB, but total depreciation cannot reduce the book value below salvage value ($10,000).

questions.stem: Equipment costing $50,000 with a salvage value of $5,000 has a useful life of 4 years. Using the sum-of-years-digits (SYD) method, what is the depreciation expense in Year 3?
questions.explanation: SYD denominator = 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 10. Depreciable base = $50,000 - $5,000 = $45,000. Year 3 fraction = 2/10 (remaining life / SYD). Year 3 depreciation = $45,000 Г— 2/10 = $9,000. The SYD method is an accelerated method that allocates more depreciation to earlier years.

questions.stem: A company acquires a trademark in a business combination for $500,000. The trademark has been in use for 50 years and the company expects to renew it indefinitely at minimal cost. How should this trademark be accounted for after acquisition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Amortize over 50 years

choices.label: B
choices.text: Amortize over 15 years (maximum under GAAP)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Not amortize; test for impairment at least annually

choices.label: D
choices.text: Expense immediately

questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-30-35-4, an intangible asset with an indefinite useful life is not amortized. Instead, it is tested for impairment at least annually or more frequently when indicators of impairment exist. A trademark that is expected to be renewed indefinitely at minimal cost qualifies as an indefinite-life intangible. There is no maximum amortization period under U.S. GAAP.

questions.stem: Mining equipment costs $800,000 with a salvage value of $50,000. Total estimated production is 500,000 tons. In Year 1, 75,000 tons are extracted. What is the Year 1 depreciation expense?
questions.explanation: Depreciable base = $800,000 - $50,000 = $750,000. Rate per unit = $750,000 / 500,000 tons = $1.50 per ton. Year 1 depreciation = 75,000 tons Г— $1.50 = $112,500. The units-of-production method matches depreciation expense to actual usage, making it appropriate for assets whose wear is primarily a function of use rather than time.

questions.stem: A company acquires a noncompete agreement as part of a business combination. The agreement has a contractual term of 5 years. Under U.S. GAAP, how should this asset be amortized?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Not amortized вҖ” tested for impairment annually

choices.label: B
choices.text: Amortized over 5 years on a straight-line basis (unless another method better reflects the pattern of benefit)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Expensed immediately as an acquisition cost

choices.label: D
choices.text: Amortized over 15 years regardless of contractual term

questions.explanation: A noncompete agreement is a finite-life intangible asset under ASC 350-30-35-6. It is amortized over its useful life, which in this case is the contractual term of 5 years. The straight-line method is used unless another systematic method better reflects the pattern in which the economic benefits are consumed or otherwise used up.

questions.stem: Under ASC 350, at what level is goodwill tested for impairment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Individual asset level

choices.label: B
choices.text: Operating segment or one level below an operating segment (reporting unit)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Entity-wide level

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cash-generating unit level

questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-20-35-33, goodwill is tested for impairment at the reporting unit level. A reporting unit is an operating segment or one level below an operating segment (a component). Components with similar economic characteristics may be aggregated into a single reporting unit. Cash-generating unit (D) is an IFRS concept, not U.S. GAAP.

questions.stem: An asset group has a carrying amount of $1,000,000 and an undiscounted future cash flow of $800,000. The fair value of the asset group is $700,000. How much impairment loss is recognized?
questions.explanation: Step 1 (Recoverability test): Compare carrying amount ($1,000,000) to undiscounted cash flows ($800,000). Since $1,000,000 > $800,000, the asset group fails the recoverability test. Step 2 (Measurement): Impairment loss = Carrying amount - Fair value = $1,000,000 - $700,000 = $300,000. The loss is allocated to the long-lived assets in the group on a pro rata basis based on carrying amounts.

questions.stem: A company decides to abandon equipment with a carrying value of $45,000 (cost $120,000, accumulated depreciation $75,000). The equipment has no salvage value. What is the accounting treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continue depreciating over remaining useful life

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recognize a $45,000 loss and remove the asset from the books

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reclassify as held for sale at fair value

choices.label: D
choices.text: Write down to $1 and disclose in notes

questions.explanation: When an asset is abandoned, it is derecognized and a loss equal to its carrying amount is recognized if there is no expected recovery. The entry is: Debit Loss on Abandonment $45,000, Debit Accumulated Depreciation $75,000, Credit Equipment $120,000. Abandoned assets do not qualify for held-for-sale classification because they are not being marketed for sale.

questions.stem: Equipment originally costing $200,000 with a salvage value of $20,000 and a 10-year life has been depreciated for 4 years using straight-line. At the beginning of Year 5, the remaining useful life is revised to 3 more years (total 7 years). What is the Year 5 depreciation?
questions.explanation: Original annual depreciation = ($200,000 - $20,000) / 10 = $18,000. After 4 years, accumulated depreciation = $18,000 Г— 4 = $72,000. Book value at start of Year 5 = $200,000 - $72,000 = $128,000. Revised remaining life = 7 total years вҲ’ 4 years elapsed = 3 years. New annual depreciation = ($128,000 - $20,000) / 3 = $108,000 / 3 = $36,000. Changes in estimate are applied prospectively (no retroactive adjustment). Answer: $36,000.

questions.stem: A company completes a business combination on September 1. During the measurement period, the company discovers that an acquired intangible asset (customer list) has a fair value of $200,000, not the preliminary estimate of $150,000. What is the accounting effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recognize a $50,000 gain in current period income

choices.label: B
choices.text: Adjust goodwill downward by $50,000 retroactively as of the acquisition date

choices.label: C
choices.text: Expense the $50,000 difference

choices.label: D
choices.text: No adjustment вҖ” the preliminary estimate is final

questions.explanation: Under ASC 805-10-25-13, during the measurement period (up to one year from acquisition date), the acquirer may adjust provisional amounts recognized at the acquisition date. An increase in the fair value of an identifiable intangible asset ($50,000) reduces goodwill by the same amount. These adjustments are made as if the accounting had been completed at the acquisition date.

questions.stem: Company A exchanges a truck (cost $60,000, accumulated depreciation $40,000, fair value $25,000) for Company B's truck (fair value $22,000) plus $3,000 cash. The exchange lacks commercial substance. What value does Company A record for the new truck?
questions.explanation: When an exchange lacks commercial substance, the new asset is recorded at the book value of the asset given up minus any cash received (or plus cash paid). Book value of old truck = $60,000 - $40,000 = $20,000. Cash received = $3,000. New truck value = $20,000 - $3,000 = $17,000. No gain is recognized because the exchange lacks commercial substance. A loss would be recognized if the fair value were below carrying amount.

questions.stem: In a business combination, a company acquires in-process research and development (IPR&D) with a fair value of $3,000,000. The project has not yet reached technological feasibility. How should the acquired IPR&D be accounted for?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Expense immediately

choices.label: B
choices.text: Capitalize as an indefinite-life intangible asset until the project is completed or abandoned

choices.label: C
choices.text: Capitalize and amortize over the expected development period

choices.label: D
choices.text: Include in goodwill

questions.explanation: Under ASC 805-20-35-6, IPR&D acquired in a business combination is recognized as an indefinite-life intangible asset and not amortized until the project is completed or abandoned. It is tested for impairment at least annually. When the project is completed, the asset is reclassified as a finite-life intangible and amortized. If abandoned, the carrying amount is written off.

questions.stem: Which of the following assets qualifies for interest capitalization under ASC 835-20?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inventory routinely manufactured in large quantities on a repetitive basis

choices.label: B
choices.text: A building being constructed for the company's own use

choices.label: C
choices.text: Land purchased and held for future development with no current construction activity

choices.label: D
choices.text: Investment securities purchased on margin

questions.explanation: Under ASC 835-20-15-5, interest capitalization applies to assets that are constructed or otherwise produced for an entity's own use, assets produced as discrete projects for sale or lease, and investments accounted for by the equity method while the investee has activities in progress to begin operations. Routine inventory (A), idle land (C), and investment securities (D) do not qualify.

questions.stem: A company incurs $500,000 in implementation costs for a cloud computing arrangement that is a service contract (not a software license). Under current U.S. GAAP, how should these costs be accounted for?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Capitalize as an intangible asset

choices.label: B
choices.text: Capitalize as a prepaid asset and amortize over the service contract term

choices.label: C
choices.text: Expense as incurred

choices.label: D
choices.text: Capitalize as PP&E

questions.explanation: Under ASU 2018-15 (codified in ASC 350-40), implementation costs incurred in a hosting arrangement that is a service contract are capitalized if they meet the same criteria as internal-use software. However, the capitalized costs are presented as a prepaid asset (not an intangible), amortized over the term of the service contract, and included in the same line item as the service fees.

questions.stem: A reporting unit has a carrying amount of $5,000,000 (including goodwill of $1,200,000) and a fair value of $4,400,000. Under the current one-step goodwill impairment test (ASU 2017-04), what is the goodwill impairment loss?
questions.explanation: Under ASU 2017-04 (ASC 350-20-35-3C), goodwill impairment is measured as the excess of the reporting unit's carrying amount over its fair value, limited to the carrying amount of goodwill. Excess = $5,000,000 - $4,400,000 = $600,000. Since $600,000 < goodwill of $1,200,000, the impairment loss is $600,000. The implied fair value of goodwill is no longer calculated.

questions.stem: When measuring the fair value of a long-lived asset for impairment testing, ASC 820 permits three valuation approaches. Which approach uses discounted expected future cash flows specific to the asset?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Market approach

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cost approach

choices.label: C
choices.text: Income approach

choices.label: D
choices.text: Residual approach

questions.explanation: The income approach (ASC 820-10-35-32) converts future amounts (cash flows or earnings) to a single present value using a discount rate that reflects current market expectations. The market approach uses prices from comparable market transactions. The cost approach estimates the amount required to replace the asset's service capacity. There is no 'residual approach' in ASC 820.

questions.stem: In a business combination, which of the following intangible assets must be recognized separately from goodwill?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An assembled workforce

choices.label: B
choices.text: A patented technology

choices.label: C
choices.text: General synergies expected from the combination

choices.label: D
choices.text: Going concern value

questions.explanation: Under ASC 805-20-25-10, intangible assets acquired in a business combination must be recognized separately from goodwill if they meet either the contractual-legal criterion or the separability criterion. A patent meets the contractual-legal criterion. Assembled workforce (A), synergies (C), and going concern value (D) do not meet either criterion and remain part of goodwill.

questions.stem: Under U.S. GAAP, can a company revalue its PP&E to fair value after initial recognition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, revaluation to fair value is permitted as an accounting policy election

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, but only for investment property

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, PP&E must be carried at cost less accumulated depreciation and impairment

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only when fair value exceeds cost

questions.explanation: Under U.S. GAAP (ASC 360-10-35), PP&E is carried at historical cost less accumulated depreciation and any impairment losses. Upward revaluation to fair value is prohibited. This differs from IFRS (IAS 16), which permits the revaluation model as an accounting policy choice. Under U.S. GAAP, impairment write-downs are also not reversible for assets held and used.

questions.stem: A company borrows $2,000,000 at 8% specifically for construction of a building. During the year, average accumulated expenditures are $1,500,000. The company also has general debt of $5,000,000 at 6%. What is the amount of capitalizable interest?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 835-20-30, the capitalizable interest is calculated by applying the specific borrowing rate to the average accumulated expenditures up to the specific borrowing amount. Since average accumulated expenditures ($1,500,000) are less than the specific borrowing ($2,000,000), use the specific rate: $1,500,000 Г— 8% = $120,000. No general borrowing rate is needed because expenditures do not exceed the specific debt.

questions.stem: A company reclassifies PP&E (carrying amount $500,000) as held for sale. The fair value less costs to sell is $420,000. After reclassification, the fair value increases to $470,000. What carrying amount is reported?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-35-40, assets held for sale are measured at the lower of carrying amount or fair value less costs to sell. Initially: lower of $500,000 or $420,000 = $420,000 (impairment loss of $80,000). Subsequently, ASC 360-10-35-43 permits reversal of the loss up to the cumulative loss previously recognized. Fair value less costs to sell = $470,000. Gain recovery = $470,000 - $420,000 = $50,000, but limited to $80,000 originally recognized. Carrying amount = $470,000.

questions.stem: Redwood Corp. incurred the following costs related to its manufacturing equipment: (1) $5,000 for routine maintenance to keep the equipment running, (2) $12,000 to replace a major component that extends the equipment's useful life by 3 years, and (3) $3,500 for repainting the equipment for aesthetic purposes. What total amount should Redwood capitalize?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-25-1, subsequent costs are capitalized only when they extend the useful life, increase capacity, or improve efficiency. Routine maintenance ($5,000) and repainting ($3,500) are period expenses. The $12,000 major component replacement extends useful life and qualifies for capitalization. Total capitalized = $12,000.

questions.stem: Under the GAAP cost model, which of the following best describes the subsequent measurement of property, plant, and equipment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Carried at fair value with changes recognized in other comprehensive income

choices.label: B
choices.text: Carried at historical cost less accumulated depreciation and any accumulated impairment losses

choices.label: C
choices.text: Carried at replacement cost adjusted for physical deterioration

choices.label: D
choices.text: Carried at the lower of cost or net realizable value

questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-35-4, PP&E is subsequently measured at historical cost less accumulated depreciation and any accumulated impairment losses (the cost model). U.S. GAAP does not permit the revaluation model for PP&E. Fair value with OCI adjustments is the IFRS revaluation model. Lower of cost or NRV applies to inventory, not PP&E.

questions.subtopic: Asset Acquisition vs Business Combination Distinction
questions.stem: Maple Inc. paid $2,000,000 to acquire a group of assets consisting of land, a building, and equipment from an unrelated party. No employees or processes were acquired. Under ASC 805, how should Maple account for this transaction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: As a business combination, recognizing goodwill for any excess purchase price

choices.label: B
choices.text: As an asset acquisition, allocating the cost to individual assets based on their relative fair values

choices.label: C
choices.text: As an asset acquisition, recording each asset at its individual fair value and recognizing any excess as a gain

choices.label: D
choices.text: As a business combination, recording assets at fair value with no goodwill

questions.explanation: Under ASC 805-10-25-1, a transaction is a business combination only if the acquired set constitutes a business, which generally requires inputs, processes, and outputs. Acquiring only assets without employees or processes typically fails the business definition. Under ASC 805-50, an asset acquisition requires allocating the total cost to individual assets based on their relative fair values, with no goodwill recognized.

questions.stem: Parker Co. has an indefinite-life trademark with a carrying amount of $400,000. At year-end, the fair value is determined to be $350,000. Parker applies the quantitative impairment test. What is the impairment loss to be recognized?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-30-35-18, indefinite-life intangible assets must be tested for impairment at least annually by comparing the carrying amount to fair value. If carrying amount ($400,000) exceeds fair value ($350,000), an impairment loss of $50,000 ($400,000 - $350,000) is recognized. No recoverability test using undiscounted cash flows is required for indefinite-life intangibles.

questions.subtopic: Goodwill - Reporting Unit with Zero or Negative Carrying Amount
questions.stem: A reporting unit has a carrying amount of negative $100,000 (net liabilities exceed assets including goodwill of $200,000). The fair value of the reporting unit is determined to be $50,000. Under the current ASU 2017-04 one-step test, what impairment loss, if any, should be recognized?

choices.label: A
choices.text: $0 because the reporting unit has negative equity

questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-20-35-8C (as amended by ASU 2017-04), the impairment loss is the excess of the carrying amount of the reporting unit over its fair value, limited to the total goodwill allocated. When the carrying amount of the reporting unit is negative ($-100,000), it does not exceed the fair value ($50,000), so no impairment loss is recognized. A negative carrying amount inherently satisfies the test because the carrying amount is already below fair value.

questions.stem: Which of the following internally generated intangible assets may be recognized on the balance sheet under U.S. GAAP?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An internally developed brand name

choices.label: B
choices.text: Costs of internally developed software for sale after technological feasibility is established

choices.label: C
choices.text: An internally generated customer list

choices.label: D
choices.text: Internally generated goodwill

questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-20-25-3, costs of internally developing, maintaining, or restoring intangible assets that are not specifically identifiable or have indeterminate lives shall not be recognized as assets. However, ASC 985-20-25-1 provides an exception: software development costs for external sale are capitalized after technological feasibility is established. Internally generated brand names, customer lists, and goodwill cannot be recognized.

questions.stem: Network TV Co. acquires broadcasting rights for a popular series for $6,000,000. The contract covers 3 seasons, and the expected revenue pattern is: Season 1 = 50%, Season 2 = 30%, Season 3 = 20%. Under ASC 920-350, what is the amortization expense for Season 2?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 920-350-35 (broadcasters), program license agreements are amortized based on the expected pattern of revenue generation. The total cost of $6,000,000 is allocated by revenue percentage. Season 2 amortization = $6,000,000 x 30% = $1,800,000.

questions.stem: Studio Corp. incurs $10,000,000 in production costs for a film. In Year 1, the film generates $4,000,000 in revenue. Total estimated remaining revenue is $6,000,000. Under ASC 926-20-35 (individual-film-forecast method), what amount of film cost should be amortized in Year 1?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 926-20-35-4, film costs are amortized using the individual-film-forecast method: (Current period revenue / Total estimated revenue) x Unamortized cost. Year 1 revenue = $4,000,000. Total estimated revenue = $4,000,000 + $6,000,000 = $10,000,000. Amortization = ($4,000,000 / $10,000,000) x $10,000,000 = $4,000,000.

questions.stem: MineralEx Corp. acquires mining rights for $8,000,000. Estimated recoverable units are 2,000,000 tons. During Year 1, 300,000 tons are extracted and 250,000 tons are sold. What is the depletion expense charged to cost of goods sold in Year 1?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 930-360-35, depletion of mineral rights uses the units-of-production method. Depletion rate = $8,000,000 / 2,000,000 tons = $4 per ton. Total depletion for extraction = 300,000 x $4 = $1,200,000. However, depletion charged to cost of goods sold is based on units sold: 250,000 x $4 = $1,000,000. The remaining $200,000 is included in inventory.

questions.stem: Under U.S. GAAP, when is componentization (separating a single asset into components with different useful lives) required for depreciation purposes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is required for all assets with components that have different useful lives

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is required only when the components are physically separable

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is permitted but not required; entities may use composite or group depreciation as an alternative

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is prohibited under U.S. GAAP; only IFRS allows componentization

questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-35-4 and ASC 360-10-35-8, U.S. GAAP permits but does not require component depreciation. Entities may depreciate a single asset as a whole or separate it into significant components with different useful lives. Composite or group depreciation methods are acceptable alternatives under U.S. GAAP. IFRS (IAS 16.43) requires component depreciation when cost is significant relative to total.

questions.stem: Delta Corp. exchanges equipment (cost $100,000, accumulated depreciation $40,000, fair value $75,000) for a delivery truck with a fair value of $90,000, paying $15,000 cash. The transaction has commercial substance. What amount should Delta record as the cost of the delivery truck?
questions.explanation: When an exchange has commercial substance, the new asset is recorded at fair value regardless of the boot percentage. Fair value of asset received = $90,000 (which equals fair value of asset given up $75,000 + cash paid $15,000). Delta also recognizes a gain of $15,000 ($75,000 FV - $60,000 book value). The boot percentage is $15,000 / $90,000 = 16.7%, which is below 25%, but this threshold analysis is only relevant for exchanges lacking commercial substance. Cost of truck = $90,000.

questions.stem: NewVenture Inc. incurred the following costs prior to commencing operations: $50,000 in employee training, $30,000 in legal fees for incorporation, $20,000 in travel costs related to opening a new branch, and $15,000 for testing equipment before it is ready for use. Which costs must be expensed as incurred under ASC 720-15?

choices.label: A
choices.text: $50,000 training and $30,000 legal fees only

choices.label: B
choices.text: $50,000 training, $30,000 legal fees, and $20,000 travel costs

choices.label: C
choices.text: All four costs totaling $115,000

choices.label: D
choices.text: $50,000 training and $20,000 travel costs only

questions.explanation: Under ASC 720-15-25-1, start-up costs (including organization costs) must be expensed as incurred. Start-up costs include training, legal fees for incorporation, and pre-opening travel costs. However, the $15,000 for testing equipment is part of the cost to get the equipment ready for its intended use and is capitalized as part of the equipment's cost under ASC 360-10-30-1. Total expensed = $50,000 + $30,000 + $20,000 = $100,000.

questions.stem: Under ASC 720-35, which of the following advertising costs, if any, may be capitalized?

choices.label: A
choices.text: All advertising costs must be expensed as incurred or when advertising first takes place

choices.label: B
choices.text: Direct-response advertising costs may be capitalized if certain conditions are met

choices.label: C
choices.text: Brand awareness campaigns may be capitalized over their expected benefit period

choices.label: D
choices.text: Advertising production costs may always be capitalized and amortized

questions.explanation: Under ASC 720-35-25-1, advertising costs are generally expensed as incurred or the first time the advertising takes place. However, ASC 720-35-25-4 provides an exception for direct-response advertising: these costs may be capitalized if (1) the primary purpose is to elicit direct customer response, and (2) the entity can demonstrate that the future revenues obtained exceed the amount capitalized. Brand awareness and production costs do not qualify for capitalization.

questions.subtopic: Pre-Production Costs for Long-Term Supply Contracts
questions.stem: AutoParts Co. incurs $800,000 in design and development costs for molds and dies used to produce parts under a long-term supply contract with a major automaker. The automaker will own the molds. Under ASC 340-10, how should AutoParts account for these costs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Expense all $800,000 immediately as research and development

choices.label: B
choices.text: Capitalize $800,000 and amortize over the contract term if the costs are recoverable

choices.label: C
choices.text: Capitalize $800,000 as property, plant, and equipment

choices.label: D
choices.text: Defer $800,000 and recognize as revenue when the molds are delivered

questions.explanation: Under ASC 340-10-25-4, pre-production costs related to long-term supply arrangements (such as design, development, and tooling costs) may be capitalized and amortized over the contract term if the costs are specifically reimbursable or recoverable through the pricing of the parts under the contract. Since the automaker owns the molds, these are not PP&E of AutoParts but rather deferred costs to be amortized as a cost of fulfilling the contract.

questions.stem: Franklin Manufacturing has a machine with a cost of $500,000, a salvage value of $50,000, and a 10-year useful life (straight-line). The machine was idled for 6 months during the year due to lack of demand. What depreciation expense should Franklin recognize for the full year?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-35-4, depreciation continues to be recognized on assets temporarily idled. An asset that is idle does not cease to be depreciated unless it is classified as held for sale. Annual depreciation = ($500,000 - $50,000) / 10 = $45,000. The full $45,000 is recognized even though the machine was idle for 6 months.

questions.subtopic: Fully Depreciated Asset Still in Use
questions.stem: A company continues to use equipment that is fully depreciated (cost $200,000, accumulated depreciation $200,000). Which of the following statements is correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The equipment must be removed from the books since it has been fully depreciated

choices.label: B
choices.text: The equipment should be written up to its current fair value

choices.label: C
choices.text: The equipment remains on the books at zero net book value with no further depreciation recognized

choices.label: D
choices.text: Additional depreciation must be recognized to reflect continued use

questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-35-4, when an asset is fully depreciated, no additional depreciation is recognized. The asset and its accumulated depreciation remain on the balance sheet at their gross amounts until the asset is disposed of or retired. Writing up to fair value is not permitted under the U.S. GAAP cost model. The asset is not removed until actual disposition occurs.

questions.stem: Terra Corp. classifies a disposal group as held for sale. The group includes assets with a carrying amount of $800,000, liabilities of $200,000, and a fair value (less costs to sell) of $550,000 for the assets. What carrying amount should the disposal group be reported at?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Net assets of $350,000

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assets at $550,000 and liabilities at $200,000, reported separately

choices.label: C
choices.text: Net assets of $600,000

choices.label: D
choices.text: Assets at $800,000 and liabilities at $200,000, reported separately

questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-45-14, the assets and liabilities of a disposal group classified as held for sale are presented separately on the balance sheet (not netted). Under ASC 360-10-35-40, the long-lived assets in the group are measured at the lower of carrying amount ($800,000) or fair value less costs to sell ($550,000). The assets are written down to $550,000 (impairment loss of $250,000). Liabilities remain at $200,000. Both are reported separately.

questions.stem: On January 1, Year 1, Birch Co. reclassified a building from held for sale back to held and used. At reclassification, the building had a carrying amount under held-for-sale measurement of $300,000. If the building had never been classified as held for sale, its depreciated carrying amount would have been $340,000. The fair value less costs to sell at reclassification date is $320,000. At what amount should Birch record the building upon reclassification?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-35-44, when an asset is reclassified from held for sale back to held and used, it is measured at the lower of (a) its carrying amount before it was classified as held for sale, adjusted for depreciation that would have been recognized (=$340,000), and (b) its fair value at the date of reclassification ($320,000). The lower amount is $320,000.

questions.stem: During the construction of a new warehouse, BuildRight Inc. incurred the following costs: $25,000 in property taxes on the land during construction, $18,000 in insurance premiums on the construction project, and $7,000 in general liability insurance for the company. Which of these costs should be capitalized as part of the warehouse cost?

choices.label: A
choices.text: $25,000 property taxes only

choices.label: B
choices.text: $43,000 property taxes and construction insurance

choices.label: C
choices.text: $50,000 all costs

choices.label: D
choices.text: $18,000 construction insurance only

questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-30-1, costs necessary to bring the asset to the condition and location for its intended use are capitalizable. Property taxes on the construction site during the construction period ($25,000) and insurance directly related to the construction project ($18,000) are capitalizable as part of the asset's cost. General company liability insurance ($7,000) is a period cost and should be expensed. Total capitalized = $25,000 + $18,000 = $43,000.

questions.stem: GreenTech Corp. purchased land with known environmental contamination for $500,000. GreenTech incurred $120,000 in environmental remediation costs to clean up the land before constructing a factory. An additional $30,000 in ongoing monitoring costs was incurred after operations commenced. How should these costs be treated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Capitalize $120,000 as part of the land cost; expense $30,000

choices.label: B
choices.text: Expense both $120,000 and $30,000 as environmental costs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Capitalize both $120,000 and $30,000 as part of the land cost

choices.label: D
choices.text: Capitalize $120,000 as part of the factory cost; expense $30,000

questions.explanation: Under ASC 410-30-25-9 and ASC 360-10-30-1, environmental remediation costs incurred to prepare land for its intended use (before construction begins) are capitalized as part of the cost of the land. The $120,000 cleanup cost is part of the land's initial preparation. The $30,000 in ongoing monitoring costs incurred after operations begin are period expenses as they do not add future economic benefit to the asset.

questions.stem: Under the private company alternative (ASU 2014-02), how is goodwill accounted for by an entity that elects this option?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Goodwill is amortized over 10 years on a straight-line basis and tested for impairment only upon a triggering event

choices.label: B
choices.text: Goodwill is amortized over a useful life not to exceed 10 years and tested for impairment only upon a triggering event at the entity level

choices.label: C
choices.text: Goodwill is not amortized but tested for impairment annually at the entity level

choices.label: D
choices.text: Goodwill is expensed immediately upon acquisition

questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-20-35-62 (the private company alternative per ASU 2014-02), a private company may elect to amortize goodwill on a straight-line basis over a useful life not to exceed 10 years (management may choose a shorter period). Goodwill is tested for impairment only when a triggering event occurs (not annually), and the test is performed at the entity level or reporting unit level (entity-wide option available). This reduces cost and complexity for private companies.

questions.stem: PharmaCo acquires an exclusive 8-year license to manufacture and sell a patented drug for $2,400,000. The license has no renewal option. What is the annual amortization expense?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-30-35-6, finite-life intangible assets are amortized over their useful life. The license has a definite contractual term of 8 years with no renewal option, making 8 years the useful life. Annual amortization = $2,400,000 / 8 = $300,000 per year (straight-line method, unless another method better reflects the pattern of economic benefit consumption).

questions.subtopic: Intangible - Trade Name vs Trademark Distinction
questions.stem: Which of the following is true regarding the accounting for trade names and trademarks under U.S. GAAP?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Trade names are always amortized over 15 years while trademarks are indefinite-life assets

choices.label: B
choices.text: Both trade names and trademarks may have either finite or indefinite useful lives depending on whether they can be renewed at minimal cost

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both must be amortized over their legal life regardless of renewal options

choices.label: D
choices.text: Trade names are expensed when acquired while trademarks are capitalized

questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-30-35-3 and ASC 350-30-35-4, the determination of whether an intangible asset has a finite or indefinite useful life depends on factors including legal, contractual, and economic considerations. Both trade names and trademarks may have indefinite useful lives if there is no foreseeable limit on the period over which they are expected to generate cash flows, including consideration of renewal at minimal cost. If indefinite, they are not amortized but tested for impairment annually.

questions.stem: TechBuy Corp. pays $5,000,000 to acquire assets from a seller in a transaction determined to be an asset acquisition (not a business combination). The individual fair values are: equipment $2,500,000, customer contracts $1,500,000, and a patent $1,500,000. Transaction costs are $250,000. What amount should be allocated to the patent?
questions.explanation: In an asset acquisition under ASC 805-50-30-1, the total cost (including transaction costs) is allocated to individual assets based on their relative fair values. Total cost = $5,000,000 + $250,000 = $5,250,000. Total fair value = $2,500,000 + $1,500,000 + $1,500,000 = $5,500,000. Patent's relative share = $1,500,000 / $5,500,000 = 3/11. Patent allocation = (3/11) x $5,250,000 = $1,431,818. Unlike a business combination, transaction costs in an asset acquisition are included in the total cost to be allocated.

questions.stem: Lakeside Corp. spent $80,000 to replace the roof on its warehouse. The old roof had a remaining book value of $15,000. The new roof is expected to extend the building's useful life by 10 years. What journal entry should Lakeside record?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Debit Building $80,000; Credit Cash $80,000

choices.label: B
choices.text: Debit Repair Expense $80,000; Credit Cash $80,000

choices.label: C
choices.text: Debit Building $80,000, Debit Loss on Disposal $15,000; Credit Accumulated Depreciation $15,000, Credit Cash $80,000

choices.label: D
choices.text: Debit Building $65,000, Debit Loss on Disposal $15,000; Credit Cash $80,000

questions.explanation: When a major component is replaced and the replacement extends the asset's useful life, the cost is capitalized. The old component must be derecognized. The old roof has a remaining book value of $15,000 (cost minus accumulated depreciation already recognized on the roof portion). The journal entry: Debit Building $80,000 (capitalize new roof), Debit Loss on Disposal $15,000 (remove old roof net book value), Credit Accumulated Depreciation $15,000 (remove old roof's accumulated depreciation from the building account), Credit Cash $80,000.
questions.explanationJa: дё»иҰҒж§ӢжҲҗиҰҒзҙ гҒҢдәӨжҸӣгҒ•гӮҢиҖҗз”Ёе№ҙж•°гҒҢе»¶й•·гҒ•гӮҢгӮӢе ҙеҗҲгҖҒиІ»з”ЁгҒҜиіҮз”ЈиЁҲдёҠгҖӮж—§ж§ӢжҲҗиҰҒзҙ гҒҜйҷӨеҚҙгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮж—§еұӢж №гҒ®еёіз°ҝж®Ӣй«ҳгҒҜ$15,000гҖӮд»•иЁіпјҡеҖҹж–№ е»әзү©$80,000пјҲж–°еұӢж №гӮ’иіҮз”ЈиЁҲдёҠпјүгҖҒеҖҹж–№ йҷӨеҚҙжҗҚ$15,000пјҲж—§еұӢж №гҒ®з°ҝдҫЎгӮ’йҷӨеҚҙпјүгҖҒиІёж–№ жёӣдҫЎе„ҹеҚҙзҙҜиЁҲйЎҚ$15,000пјҲж—§еұӢж №гҒ®зҙҜиЁҲйЎҚгӮ’йҷӨеҚҙпјүгҖҒиІёж–№ зҸҫйҮ‘$80,000гҖӮ

questions.stem: Apex Corp. uses the composite depreciation method for its fleet of delivery vehicles. The fleet has a total cost of $600,000, total salvage value of $60,000, and a composite useful life of 6 years. What is the annual composite depreciation expense?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-35-8, the composite depreciation method groups similar assets together. The composite depreciation rate is calculated on the total depreciable amount. Total depreciable amount = $600,000 - $60,000 = $540,000. Annual composite depreciation = $540,000 / 6 years = $90,000.

questions.stem: Hillcrest Corp. uses the group (composite) depreciation method for office furniture. The group has a cost of $300,000 and accumulated depreciation of $180,000. One desk with a cost of $5,000 is retired and sold for $3,000 cash. What entry should Hillcrest record?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Debit Cash $3,000, Debit Loss $2,000; Credit Furniture $5,000

choices.label: B
choices.text: Debit Cash $3,000, Debit Accumulated Depreciation $2,000; Credit Furniture $5,000

choices.label: C
choices.text: Debit Cash $3,000, Debit Gain $2,000; Credit Furniture $5,000

choices.label: D
choices.text: Debit Cash $3,000; Credit Furniture $3,000

questions.explanation: Under the group (composite) depreciation method per ASC 360-10-35-8, no gain or loss is recognized on the retirement of individual assets within the group. The difference between the proceeds and the asset cost is debited (or credited) to accumulated depreciation. Entry: Debit Cash $3,000, Debit Accumulated Depreciation $2,000 (plug = $5,000 - $3,000); Credit Furniture $5,000.

questions.stem: A company has a broadcasting license carried at $500,000 as an indefinite-life intangible asset. Due to new regulations, the license will not be renewed and has a remaining legal life of 5 years. What accounting change is required?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recognize an immediate impairment loss of $500,000

choices.label: B
choices.text: Begin amortizing the license over 5 years and test for impairment using the recoverability test for finite-life assets

choices.label: C
choices.text: Continue to test for impairment annually without amortization until expiration

choices.label: D
choices.text: Retroactively restate prior periods to reflect amortization from the acquisition date

questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-30-35-17, when an indefinite-life intangible asset is subsequently determined to have a finite life, the asset is tested for impairment under the indefinite-life framework at the time of reclassification. Then, the carrying amount (after any impairment) is amortized prospectively over the remaining useful life. Going forward, impairment testing follows the finite-life asset approach (recoverability test per ASC 360-10-35-17). This is treated as a change in accounting estimate, applied prospectively.

questions.stem: Timber Corp. owns a 100-acre tract of timberland with a total carrying amount of $1,000,000. Timber sells 30 acres for $400,000. What gain or loss should Timber recognize on the partial disposal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gain of $100,000

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gain of $400,000

choices.label: C
choices.text: Loss of $300,000

choices.label: D
choices.text: No gain or loss is recognized on partial disposals

questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-40-3, when a portion of a long-lived asset is disposed of, the carrying amount of the disposed portion is removed and any difference between proceeds and carrying amount is recognized as a gain or loss. Carrying amount of 30 acres = (30/100) x $1,000,000 = $300,000. Proceeds = $400,000. Gain = $400,000 - $300,000 = $100,000.

questions.stem: Construct Corp. has two qualifying assets under construction during the year. Asset A had weighted-average accumulated expenditures of $1,200,000 and Asset B had $800,000. Construct Corp. had the following debt: a $1,000,000 construction loan at 8% specifically for Asset A, and general borrowings of $3,000,000 at 6%. What is the total capitalized interest?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 835-20-30, interest capitalization is computed for each qualifying asset. Asset A: specific borrowing of $1,000,000 at 8% covers $1,000,000 of the $1,200,000 weighted-average accumulated expenditures. Interest on specific debt = $1,000,000 x 8% = $80,000. Excess expenditures for A = $1,200,000 - $1,000,000 = $200,000, which uses the weighted-average rate on general borrowings (6%). Interest on excess = $200,000 x 6% = $12,000. Asset B: no specific borrowing, so the entire $800,000 uses the general rate. Interest = $800,000 x 6% = $48,000. Total avoidable interest = $80,000 + $12,000 + $48,000 = $140,000. Actual interest = ($1,000,000 x 8%) + ($3,000,000 x 6%) = $260,000. Since avoidable interest ($140,000) is less than actual interest ($260,000), capitalized interest = $140,000.

questions.stem: CloudFirst Corp. pays $240,000 for a 3-year cloud computing arrangement that is determined to be a hosting arrangement (service contract), not a software license. Under ASC 350-40 (as updated by ASU 2018-15), how should CloudFirst account for this arrangement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Capitalize $240,000 as an intangible asset and amortize over 3 years

choices.label: B
choices.text: Expense $240,000 immediately in the first year

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recognize $80,000 per year as a prepaid expense and expense ratably over the hosting term

choices.label: D
choices.text: Capitalize $240,000 as property, plant, and equipment

questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-40-15-4A (as updated by ASU 2018-15), a cloud computing arrangement that is a service contract (hosting arrangement) is not a software license and the fees are not capitalized as an intangible asset. The arrangement is accounted for as a service contract. If paid upfront, the $240,000 is recorded as a prepaid expense and expensed ratably over the 3-year hosting period ($80,000 per year). Implementation costs may be capitalized separately under ASC 350-40-25-16 if they meet certain criteria.

questions.stem: On January 1, Year 1, PetroEnergy recorded an asset retirement obligation of $500,000 (present value) using a credit-adjusted risk-free rate of 5%. At the end of Year 2, PetroEnergy revises the estimated future retirement cost upward, increasing the ARO liability by $100,000 (present value). What is the accretion expense for Year 2 (before recording the revision)?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 410-20-35-3, accretion expense is calculated by multiplying the beginning ARO balance by the credit-adjusted risk-free rate. Year 1: Accretion = $500,000 x 5% = $25,000. Ending ARO balance Year 1 = $500,000 + $25,000 = $525,000. Year 2: Accretion expense (before revision) = $525,000 x 5% = $26,250. The $100,000 revision is recorded at the end of Year 2 and will affect Year 3 accretion.

questions.subtopic: Deferred Payment Purchase - Cash Price Equivalent
questions.stem: Cedar Corp. acquires equipment by signing a non-interest-bearing note requiring a single payment of $50,000 in 3 years. No established cash price for the equipment is available, but the prevailing market interest rate for similar notes is 8%. The present value of $1 at 8% for 3 periods is 0.7938. At what amount should Cedar initially record the equipment?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 835-30-25-8, when equipment is acquired with a non-interest-bearing note and no cash price is available, the asset is recorded at the present value of the note using the prevailing market rate. PV = $50,000 x 0.7938 = $39,690. The $10,310 difference between the face amount ($50,000) and present value ($39,690) is a discount that is recognized as interest expense over the 3-year note term. The asset is recorded at $39,690, not its face amount. ($46,296 uses a 1-period factor in error; $38,000 applies a flat 24% to the face amount in error.)

questions.stem: Birchwood Inc. purchases machinery with an invoice price of $200,000 under terms 2/10, n/30. Birchwood pays within the discount period. It also incurs $6,000 freight-in and $4,000 installation costs. Under the net method, at what amount should the machinery be capitalized?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-30-1, the cost of machinery includes the net purchase price plus all costs necessary to bring it to working condition. Cash discounts available are treated as a reduction of cost (whether taken or not, under the net/preferred method). Net invoice price = $200,000 x (1 - 0.02) = $196,000. Add freight-in $6,000 and installation $4,000. Capitalized cost = $196,000 + $6,000 + $4,000 = $206,000.

questions.stem: Aspen Corp. acquires a specialized machine by issuing 5,000 shares of its $10 par common stock. On the acquisition date, the stock has a readily determinable fair value of $32 per share, and an independent appraisal values the machine at $170,000. At what amount should Aspen record the machine?
questions.explanation: When PP&E is acquired by issuing equity securities that have a readily determinable fair value, the asset is recorded at the fair value of the securities issued, as this is the more objective and reliable measure of the transaction's value. Fair value of stock issued = 5,000 shares x $32 = $160,000. The machine is recorded at $160,000. The $50,000 par value (5,000 x $10) and the appraisal value are not used when an active market price for the shares exists.
questions.explanationJa: еёӮе ҙдҫЎж јгҒҢе®№жҳ“гҒ«жұәе®ҡгҒ§гҒҚгӮӢж ӘејҸгӮ’зҷәиЎҢгҒ—гҒҰPP&EгӮ’еҸ–еҫ—гҒҷгӮӢе ҙеҗҲгҖҒеҸ–еј•дҫЎеҖӨгҒ®гӮҲгӮҠе®ўиҰізҡ„гҒ§дҝЎй јжҖ§гҒ®й«ҳгҒ„жҢҮжЁҷгҒ§гҒӮгӮӢзҷәиЎҢж ӘејҸгҒ®е…¬жӯЈдҫЎеҖӨгҒ§иіҮз”ЈгӮ’иЁҲдёҠгҒҷгӮӢгҖӮзҷәиЎҢж ӘејҸгҒ®е…¬жӯЈдҫЎеҖӨ = 5,000ж Ә x $32 = $160,000гҖӮж©ҹжў°гҒҜ$160,000гҒ§иЁҲдёҠгҖӮйЎҚйқў$50,000пјҲ5,000 x $10пјүгӮ„й‘‘е®ҡи©•дҫЎйЎҚгҒҜгҖҒж ӘејҸгҒ®жҙ»зҷәгҒӘеёӮе ҙдҫЎж јгҒҢеӯҳеңЁгҒҷгӮӢе ҙеҗҲгҒҜдҪҝз”ЁгҒ—гҒӘгҒ„гҖӮ

questions.stem: Granite Co. self-constructs a machine for its own use. Direct materials cost $80,000 and direct labor cost $60,000. The machine required 2,000 direct labor hours. Granite normally applies variable overhead at $5 per direct labor hour to production. If Granite had instead purchased the machine, it would have paid $165,000. At what amount should the self-constructed machine be capitalized (incremental/full cost concept, but not exceeding purchase price)?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-30-1, a self-constructed asset is capitalized at direct materials, direct labor, and an allocable portion of overhead. Direct materials $80,000 + direct labor $60,000 + applied overhead (2,000 hrs x $5 = $10,000) = $150,000. Because the computed cost ($150,000) is below the external purchase price ($165,000), no write-down to a lower amount is required and no loss is recognized. The asset is capitalized at $150,000. (If accumulated construction cost had exceeded fair value/purchase price, the excess would be expensed as a loss.)

questions.subtopic: Capitalized Interest - Interest Earned on Borrowed Funds
questions.stem: Summit Corp. borrows $1,000,000 at 9% specifically to construct a building. Before the funds are needed for construction, Summit temporarily invests $400,000 of the loan proceeds and earns $12,000 in interest income during the period. Weighted-average accumulated expenditures equal $1,000,000. What amount of interest should Summit capitalize?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 835-20-30-10, interest earned on the temporary investment of borrowed construction funds does NOT reduce the amount of interest cost eligible for capitalization (for general construction projects under U.S. GAAP). Avoidable interest = specific borrowing $1,000,000 x 9% = $90,000. The $12,000 of investment income is recognized separately as interest income in the income statement and does not offset capitalized interest. Capitalized interest = $90,000.

questions.subtopic: Capitalization Period - When Interest Capitalization Stops
questions.stem: Under ASC 835-20, the capitalization of interest on a qualifying asset should cease when:

choices.label: A
choices.text: The asset is substantially complete and ready for its intended use

choices.label: B
choices.text: Construction expenditures are fully paid in cash

choices.label: C
choices.text: The construction loan is fully repaid

choices.label: D
choices.text: The asset begins generating revenue

questions.explanation: Under ASC 835-20-25-5, the interest capitalization period ends when the asset is substantially complete and ready for its intended use. This is independent of when the loan is repaid, when expenditures are paid, or when revenue is first generated. If an asset is completed in parts and each part can be used independently, capitalization ceases for each part as it is completed. After the capitalization period ends, interest is expensed.

questions.stem: Equipment costing $90,000 with no salvage value and a useful life of 6 years is depreciated using the 150% declining balance method. What is the depreciation expense in Year 2?
questions.explanation: The 150% declining balance rate = 1.5 x (1/6) = 0.25 (25%). Year 1 depreciation = $90,000 x 25% = $22,500. Book value end of Year 1 = $90,000 - $22,500 = $67,500. Year 2 depreciation = $67,500 x 25% = $16,875. Under declining balance methods, salvage value is not subtracted from the base, but the asset is not depreciated below salvage value (here $0).
