questions.stem: An asset costs $64,000 with a $4,000 salvage value and a 4-year life. The company uses double-declining balance but switches to straight-line in the year that straight-line yields a higher (or equal) expense. What is the depreciation expense in Year 3?
questions.explanation: DDB rate = 2 x (1/4) = 50%. Year 1: $64,000 x 50% = $32,000; BV = $32,000. Year 2: $32,000 x 50% = $16,000; BV = $16,000. Year 3 DDB amount would be $16,000 x 50% = $8,000. Year 3 straight-line on remaining depreciable base over remaining life = ($16,000 - $4,000) / 2 years = $6,000. The company switches when straight-line exceeds DDB; here DDB ($8,000) still exceeds straight-line ($6,000), so DDB applies. Year 3 depreciation = $8,000.

questions.stem: A building costing $1,200,000 with no salvage value and a 40-year straight-line life is placed in service on October 1, Year 1. The company depreciates real property using the full-month (actual months in service) convention. What is the Year 1 depreciation expense?
questions.explanation: Annual straight-line depreciation = $1,200,000 / 40 = $30,000. Under the full-month convention, the asset is depreciated for the actual number of months in service during the year. From October 1 to December 31 = 3 months. Year 1 depreciation = $30,000 x 3/12 = $7,500.

questions.subtopic: Impairment Loss Allocation Within Asset Group
questions.stem: An asset group consists of equipment (carrying amount $300,000) and a building (carrying amount $700,000); total carrying amount $1,000,000. The group fails the recoverability test, and the fair value of the group is $760,000. A measured impairment loss of $240,000 is allocated pro rata based on relative carrying amounts. How much of the loss is allocated to the building?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-35-28, an impairment loss for an asset group is allocated to the long-lived assets of the group on a pro rata basis using the relative carrying amounts (but no individual asset is reduced below its own fair value). Total impairment = $1,000,000 - $760,000 = $240,000. Building's share = ($700,000 / $1,000,000) x $240,000 = 0.70 x $240,000 = $168,000.

questions.stem: Under ASC 360-10-35-21, which of the following is NOT, by itself, an indicator that a long-lived asset (held and used) should be tested for impairment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A routine annual increase in the asset's market value

choices.label: B
choices.text: A significant adverse change in the business climate that could affect the asset's value

choices.label: C
choices.text: A current-period operating loss combined with a history of losses associated with the asset

choices.label: D
choices.text: A significant decrease in the market price of the asset

questions.explanation: ASC 360-10-35-21 lists events that indicate the carrying amount of a long-lived asset may not be recoverable, including a significant decrease in market price, a significant adverse change in business climate, and a current-period operating loss combined with a history of losses. An increase in market value is favorable and is NOT an impairment indicator; in fact, U.S. GAAP prohibits writing assets up. Only declines or adverse changes trigger impairment testing.

questions.stem: Under ASC 360-10-45-9, which of the following conditions must be met for a long-lived asset to be classified as held for sale?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The sale must be completed within the current fiscal year regardless of circumstances

choices.label: B
choices.text: The asset is available for immediate sale in its present condition and the sale is probable within one year

choices.label: C
choices.text: Management has only begun evaluating whether to sell the asset

choices.label: D
choices.text: The asset must already have a signed, binding sale contract

questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-45-9, held-for-sale classification requires that: (1) management commits to a plan to sell, (2) the asset is available for immediate sale in its present condition, (3) an active program to locate a buyer has begun, (4) the sale is probable within one year, (5) the asset is actively marketed at a reasonable price, and (6) it is unlikely the plan will change. A signed contract is not required, the sale need not close in the current fiscal year, and merely evaluating a sale is insufficient.

questions.subtopic: ARO - Capitalized Asset Retirement Cost Depreciation
questions.stem: On January 1, Year 1, Quarry Co. places an asset in service and recognizes an asset retirement obligation with a present value (fair value) of $80,000. The related plant asset has a cost of $500,000 (excluding the ARO) and a 10-year useful life with no salvage value (straight-line). What is the total Year 1 depreciation expense, including the capitalized asset retirement cost?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 410-20-25-5, the fair value of the ARO ($80,000) is capitalized as part of the carrying amount of the related long-lived asset (asset retirement cost). Total depreciable cost = $500,000 + $80,000 = $580,000. Straight-line depreciation over 10 years = $580,000 / 10 = $58,000 per year. (Accretion expense on the ARO liability is a separate charge and is not part of depreciation.)

questions.stem: At the end of an asset's life, Delta Mining's asset retirement obligation has accreted to a carrying amount of $250,000. Delta settles the obligation by paying a third party $235,000 to perform the remediation. What gain or loss does Delta recognize on settlement?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 410-20-40-1, when an asset retirement obligation is settled for an amount different from its carrying amount, the entity recognizes a gain or loss on settlement. The ARO liability carrying amount is $250,000 and the actual settlement cost is $235,000. Since the company pays less than the recorded liability, it recognizes a gain = $250,000 - $235,000 = $15,000.

questions.stem: Acquirer Co. purchases 100% of Target Co. for $9,000,000 cash. The fair values of Target's identifiable assets total $11,000,000 and the fair values of its liabilities total $3,500,000. What amount of goodwill should Acquirer recognize?

choices.label: D
choices.text: $0; recognize a bargain purchase gain

questions.explanation: Under ASC 805-30-30-1, goodwill = consideration transferred - fair value of identifiable net assets acquired. Fair value of identifiable net assets = identifiable assets $11,000,000 - liabilities $3,500,000 = $7,500,000. Goodwill = $9,000,000 - $7,500,000 = $1,500,000. Because consideration exceeds the net assets acquired, goodwill (not a bargain purchase gain) is recognized.

questions.stem: Buyer Corp. acquires all of Seller Corp.'s net assets for $4,200,000 cash. After a reassessment confirming all identifiable assets and liabilities are correctly measured, the fair value of identifiable assets acquired is $6,000,000 and liabilities assumed is $1,400,000. How should Buyer account for the difference?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recognize a $400,000 bargain purchase gain in earnings on the acquisition date

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recognize $400,000 of negative goodwill amortized over 40 years

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reduce the recorded value of acquired non-current assets by $400,000

choices.label: D
choices.text: Record $400,000 of goodwill

questions.explanation: Under ASC 805-30-25-2 and 805-30-25-4, a bargain purchase occurs when the fair value of identifiable net assets acquired exceeds the consideration transferred. Net assets = $6,000,000 - $1,400,000 = $4,600,000. Consideration = $4,200,000. Bargain purchase amount = $4,600,000 - $4,200,000 = $400,000. After reassessing the measurement, the acquirer recognizes the excess as a gain in earnings on the acquisition date. Negative goodwill amortization is not permitted under current GAAP.

questions.stem: After a business combination, an acquirer must assign the $3,000,000 of acquired goodwill to its reporting units. Under ASC 350-20-35-41, the goodwill is assigned to reporting units based on:

choices.label: A
choices.text: The relative book values of the reporting units

choices.label: B
choices.text: The reporting units expected to benefit from the synergies of the combination, even if no other acquired assets are assigned to them

choices.label: C
choices.text: An equal split among all reporting units of the entity

choices.label: D
choices.text: The reporting unit with the highest revenue

questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-20-35-41 and 35-42, goodwill acquired in a business combination is assigned to one or more reporting units expected to benefit from the synergies of the combination. This is done as of the acquisition date using a methodology consistent with the objective. A reporting unit may be assigned goodwill even if no other assets or liabilities of the acquired entity are assigned to it. Allocation is not based on relative book values, equal splits, or revenue alone.

questions.stem: In a business combination, the acquirer identifies an order or production backlog arising from purchase orders that are cancelable by customers. Under ASC 805-20, how should this backlog be treated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It cannot be recognized separately because the orders are cancelable, so it is subsumed into goodwill

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is recognized separately from goodwill if it arises from contractual or other legal rights, even if cancelable

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is recognized only if the backlog exceeds 10% of annual revenue

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is expensed immediately as a marketing-related cost

questions.explanation: Under ASC 805-20-55-23, an order or production backlog acquired in a business combination meets the contractual-legal criterion and is recognized as an intangible asset separately from goodwill, even if the purchase or sales orders are cancelable. Meeting the contractual-legal criterion does not depend on whether the contracts are cancelable. There is no quantitative threshold for recognition.

questions.stem: Under ASC 350-30-35-6, the method of amortizing a finite-life intangible asset should reflect the pattern in which the asset's economic benefits are consumed. If that pattern cannot be reliably determined, which method is used?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Straight-line method

choices.label: B
choices.text: Double-declining balance method

choices.label: C
choices.text: The asset is not amortized

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sum-of-the-years'-digits method

questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-30-35-6, the amortization method for a finite-life intangible asset should reflect the pattern in which the economic benefits of the asset are consumed or used up. If that pattern cannot be reliably determined, the straight-line method is used as the default. An intangible with a finite life must be amortized; not amortizing applies only to indefinite-life intangibles.

questions.stem: Vertex Co. acquires a finite-life intangible asset for $500,000 with a 10-year useful life. A third party has committed to purchase the intangible at the end of its useful life for $80,000. What is the annual amortization expense?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-30-35-8, the amount of a finite-life intangible asset to be amortized is its cost less its residual value. Residual value is assumed to be zero unless a third party has committed to purchase the asset at the end of its useful life or it can be determined from an active market. Here a third-party commitment exists for $80,000. Amortizable base = $500,000 - $80,000 = $420,000. Annual amortization = $420,000 / 10 = $42,000.

questions.stem: Vista Corp. holds a finite-life customer-list intangible with a carrying amount of $360,000. Triggered by a major customer loss, Vista estimates undiscounted future cash flows of $330,000 and a fair value of $290,000. What impairment loss should Vista recognize?
questions.explanation: Finite-life intangible assets are tested for impairment using the same two-step model as other long-lived assets under ASC 360-10-35. Step 1 (recoverability): carrying amount $360,000 > undiscounted cash flows $330,000, so the asset is impaired. Step 2 (measurement): impairment loss = carrying amount - fair value = $360,000 - $290,000 = $70,000.

questions.stem: Under ASC 985-20, costs of developing software to be sold, leased, or otherwise marketed are capitalized:

choices.label: A
choices.text: From the point technological feasibility is established until the product is available for general release to customers

choices.label: B
choices.text: From the beginning of the project until completion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Only after the product is released to customers

choices.label: D
choices.text: Never; all such costs are expensed as research and development

questions.explanation: Under ASC 985-20-25-1 through 25-3, costs incurred to develop software for sale are charged to research and development expense until technological feasibility is established. From the point technological feasibility is established until the product is available for general release to customers, production costs are capitalized. After general release, capitalized costs are amortized, and subsequent maintenance and customer support costs are expensed.

questions.subtopic: Software for Sale - Amortization Greater of Two Methods
questions.stem: TechSoft capitalized $900,000 of software development costs for a product to be sold. In Year 1, the product generates $600,000 of current revenue, and total estimated revenue (current and future) is $3,000,000. The remaining economic life is 4 years (straight-line). Under ASC 985-20-35, what is the Year 1 amortization expense?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 985-20-35-1, annual amortization of capitalized software-for-sale costs is the GREATER of: (1) the revenue method: (current revenue / total estimated revenue) x capitalized cost = ($600,000 / $3,000,000) x $900,000 = $180,000; or (2) the straight-line method over remaining economic life = $900,000 / 4 = $225,000. The greater amount is $225,000.

questions.subtopic: Internal-Use Software - Capitalizable vs Expensed Costs
questions.stem: During the application development stage of an internal-use software project, NovaCorp incurs: $200,000 for coding and configuration, $40,000 for data conversion from the old system, and $25,000 for training employees on the new system. Under ASC 350-40, what amount should be capitalized?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-40-25-2 and 350-40-25-3, only certain application development stage costs are capitalized: coding, software configuration, interface development, testing, and installation. Data conversion costs ($40,000) and training costs ($25,000) are expensed as incurred, even if they occur during the application development stage. Therefore, only the $200,000 coding and configuration is capitalized.

questions.subtopic: Long-Lived Asset Disposal - Discontinued Operations Distinction
questions.stem: Riverside Corp. sells a single piece of manufacturing equipment that does not represent a strategic shift in operations. The equipment is sold at a loss. Under ASC 360-10 and ASC 205-20, how should the loss be presented?

choices.label: A
choices.text: As a component of income from continuing operations

choices.label: B
choices.text: As a discontinued operation, net of tax, below continuing operations

choices.label: C
choices.text: As a direct adjustment to retained earnings

choices.label: D
choices.text: As an item of other comprehensive income

questions.explanation: Under ASC 205-20-45-1B, a disposal is reported as a discontinued operation only if it represents a strategic shift that has (or will have) a major effect on the entity's operations and financial results. Selling a single piece of equipment that is not a strategic shift does not qualify as a discontinued operation. The gain or loss on disposal is reported within income from continuing operations, not net of tax below the line.

questions.stem: A fire destroys Maple Corp.'s warehouse, which had a cost of $700,000 and accumulated depreciation of $420,000. Maple receives $350,000 from its insurance company. What gain or loss should Maple recognize on the involuntary conversion?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 610-30 and ASC 360-10-40, an involuntary conversion (e.g., destruction by fire with insurance proceeds) is accounted for by removing the asset's carrying amount and recognizing a gain or loss for the difference between the insurance proceeds and the carrying amount. Carrying amount = $700,000 - $420,000 = $280,000. Insurance proceeds = $350,000. Gain = $350,000 - $280,000 = $70,000.

questions.subtopic: Land Held for Investment vs Operations
questions.stem: Horizon Corp. owns two parcels of land: Parcel 1 ($300,000) is the site of its operating headquarters, and Parcel 2 ($450,000) is held for long-term capital appreciation with no current use. How should each parcel be classified and is either depreciated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Parcel 1 as PP&E and Parcel 2 as a long-term investment; neither is depreciated

choices.label: B
choices.text: Both as PP&E; both depreciated over 40 years

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both as long-term investments; neither depreciated

choices.label: D
choices.text: Parcel 1 as PP&E (depreciated) and Parcel 2 as an investment (not depreciated)

questions.explanation: Land used in operations (Parcel 1) is classified as PP&E. Land held for long-term appreciation and not used in operations (Parcel 2) is classified as a long-term investment, not PP&E. Land is not depreciated regardless of classification because it has an indefinite useful life. Therefore, neither parcel is depreciated; only the classification differs based on intended use.

questions.stem: Pinnacle Corp. purchases land for $2,000,000 and immediately begins constructing a building on it. During the construction period, weighted-average accumulated expenditures (including the land) are $2,500,000. The applicable capitalization rate is 7%. Regarding interest on the land cost, which statement is correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Interest capitalized on the land expenditures is added to the cost of the building (not the land), because the land is being developed for the building

choices.label: B
choices.text: No interest may be capitalized on land expenditures under any circumstances

choices.label: C
choices.text: Interest on land is always capitalized to the land account

choices.label: D
choices.text: Interest on land must be expensed because land is not a depreciable asset

questions.explanation: Under ASC 835-20-30-9, if land is purchased and developed for a particular use (such as constructing a building), the land qualifies as an asset under development and is included in the qualifying asset base. The interest cost capitalized on expenditures for the land is added to the cost of the structure (building) being constructed, not to the land account, because the land is being developed for the structure. If land is developed for sale as lots, the interest would be capitalized as part of the land.

questions.subtopic: Spare Parts and Standby Equipment Classification
questions.stem: Industrial Co. purchases a major spare component (a backup turbine) for $400,000 to be used only if its primary turbine fails. The backup turbine has a useful life of 12 years. How should Industrial account for this major spare part?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Capitalize as PP&E and depreciate over its useful life

choices.label: B
choices.text: Record as inventory and expense when placed into service

choices.label: C
choices.text: Expense immediately as a repairs and maintenance cost

choices.label: D
choices.text: Record as a prepaid expense and amortize over 12 months

questions.explanation: Major spare parts and standby (backup) equipment that an entity expects to use for more than one period qualify as property, plant, and equipment under ASC 360-10. The backup turbine has a 12-year useful life and provides standby service capacity, so it is capitalized as PP&E and depreciated over its useful life. Minor spare parts that are consumed quickly are typically carried as inventory, but a major standby component is a long-lived asset.

questions.stem: Under ASC 835-20-15-5, an investor accounting for an investment under the equity method may capitalize interest on the investment when:

choices.label: A
choices.text: The investee has activities in progress necessary to commence its planned principal operations and is using funds to acquire qualifying assets for those operations

choices.label: B
choices.text: The investee pays regular dividends

choices.label: C
choices.text: The investment is classified as available-for-sale

choices.label: D
choices.text: The investor owns less than 20% of the investee

questions.explanation: Under ASC 835-20-15-5(c), investments accounted for by the equity method qualify for interest capitalization while the investee has activities in progress necessary to commence its planned principal operations, provided the investee's activities include the use of funds to acquire qualifying assets for its operations. Once the investee begins its principal operations, the qualifying status ends. Dividend-paying status, AFS classification, and ownership below 20% (which generally precludes equity-method accounting) are not the criteria.

questions.subtopic: Depreciation - Fractional Year SYD Across Calendar Years
questions.stem: An asset costing $110,000 with a $10,000 salvage value and a 5-year life is purchased on July 1, Year 1. The company uses sum-of-the-years'-digits depreciation and allocates the first year over the partial period. What is the depreciation expense for the calendar year ended December 31, Year 2?
questions.explanation: Depreciable base = $110,000 - $10,000 = $100,000. SYD denominator = 5+4+3+2+1 = 15. First full SYD year (5/15) = $100,000 x 5/15 = $33,333; second full SYD year (4/15) = $100,000 x 4/15 = $26,667. Because the asset was acquired July 1, each SYD 'year' straddles two calendar years. Calendar Year 2 = last half of SYD-Year-1 + first half of SYD-Year-2 = ($33,333 x 1/2) + ($26,667 x 1/2) = $16,667 + $13,333 = $30,000.
questions.explanationJa: е„ҹеҚҙеҹәзӨҺ = $110,000 - $10,000 = $100,000гҖӮSYDеҲҶжҜҚ = 5+4+3+2+1 = 15гҖӮSYDз¬¬1е№ҙеәҰпјҲ5/15пјү= $100,000 x 5/15 = $33,333гҖҒSYDз¬¬2е№ҙеәҰпјҲ4/15пјү= $100,000 x 4/15 = $26,667гҖӮ7жңҲ1ж—ҘеҸ–еҫ—гҒ®гҒҹгӮҒеҗ„SYDгҖҢе№ҙеәҰгҖҚгҒҜ2жҡҰе№ҙгҒ«гҒҫгҒҹгҒҢгӮӢгҖӮжҡҰе№ҙYear 2 = SYDз¬¬1е№ҙеәҰгҒ®еҫҢеҚҠ + SYDз¬¬2е№ҙеәҰгҒ®еүҚеҚҠ = ($33,333 x 1/2) + ($26,667 x 1/2) = $16,667 + $13,333 = $30,000гҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Asset Exchange - Loss Recognition When Lacking Commercial Substance
questions.stem: Coastal Co. exchanges a machine (cost $90,000, accumulated depreciation $50,000, fair value $32,000) for a similar machine. The exchange lacks commercial substance and no cash is exchanged. What gain or loss, if any, should Coastal recognize, and at what amount is the new machine recorded?

choices.label: A
choices.text: $8,000 loss; new machine recorded at $32,000

choices.label: B
choices.text: No loss; new machine recorded at $40,000

choices.label: C
choices.text: $8,000 gain; new machine recorded at $40,000

choices.label: D
choices.text: $8,000 loss; new machine recorded at $40,000

questions.explanation: When an exchange lacks commercial substance, gains are deferred but losses are recognized immediately if the fair value of the asset given up is less than its book value (an indicated impairment). Book value of machine given = $90,000 - $50,000 = $40,000. Fair value = $32,000, which is below book value, so an $8,000 loss ($40,000 - $32,000) is recognized. The new machine is recorded at the fair value of the asset given up = $32,000 (book value less the recognized loss).

questions.subtopic: Government Grant Related to Asset (Deferred Income Approach)
questions.stem: Solaris Inc. receives a $300,000 government grant to help fund the purchase of equipment costing $1,000,000 with a 10-year life. Solaris elects the deferred income method, recognizing the grant as deferred income and amortizing it over the equipment's life. What is the net effect on Year 1 pre-tax income from depreciation and grant amortization combined?

choices.label: A
choices.text: $70,000 net expense

choices.label: B
choices.text: $100,000 net expense

choices.label: C
choices.text: $30,000 net income

choices.label: D
choices.text: $130,000 net expense

questions.explanation: For-profit entities commonly account for asset-related government grants by analogy to IAS 20. Under the deferred income method, the grant is recorded as deferred income and amortized to income over the asset's life, while the asset is depreciated on its gross cost. Annual depreciation = $1,000,000 / 10 = $100,000 (expense). Annual grant amortization = $300,000 / 10 = $30,000 (income). Net effect on pre-tax income = $100,000 expense - $30,000 income = $70,000 net expense.

questions.stem: Vantage Manufacturing pays $60,000 to relocate and rearrange its existing production machinery to improve workflow efficiency. The rearrangement is expected to provide benefits over future periods. Under U.S. GAAP, how should these rearrangement costs be treated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Capitalize and amortize over the period expected to benefit if future benefits are probable and measurable; otherwise expense

choices.label: B
choices.text: Always expense immediately as a period cost with no exceptions

choices.label: C
choices.text: Add to the cost of the land on which the factory sits

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recognize as a reduction of accumulated depreciation

questions.explanation: Rearrangement, reinstallation, and relocation costs that provide probable future economic benefits beyond the current period may be capitalized as a deferred cost and amortized over the periods expected to benefit. If the future benefit is not probable or cannot be reliably measured, the costs are expensed as incurred. They are not added to land, and they are not netted against accumulated depreciation.

questions.stem: Apex Group performs its annual goodwill impairment test on December 31. On August 15, a significant adverse legal ruling causes the fair value of one reporting unit to drop sharply. Under ASC 350-20, what is Apex required to do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Test that reporting unit's goodwill for impairment between annual tests because a triggering event occurred

choices.label: B
choices.text: Take no interim action and rely solely on the next scheduled annual test on December 31

choices.label: C
choices.text: Immediately write off all goodwill of the entity

choices.label: D
choices.text: Amortize the goodwill prospectively starting August 15

questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-20-35-30, goodwill of a reporting unit must be tested for impairment between annual tests if an event occurs or circumstances change that would more likely than not reduce the fair value of the reporting unit below its carrying amount. A significant adverse legal ruling is such a triggering event, so Apex must perform an interim impairment test for the affected reporting unit instead of relying only on the scheduled annual test. Goodwill is not amortized (except under the private company alternative), and a triggering event does not automatically result in writing off all goodwill.

questions.stem: Harlow Corp. acquired a tract of land for $400,000 to be used as a future plant site. Harlow also paid $12,000 in closing costs, assumed $8,000 of delinquent property taxes owed by the seller, paid $15,000 to raze an old shed on the property (receiving $3,000 in salvage proceeds), and paid a $10,000 special assessment to the city for sewer connections. At what amount should Harlow record the land?
questions.explanation: The cost of land includes all expenditures necessary to acquire it and prepare it for its intended use: purchase price $400,000 + closing costs $12,000 + delinquent taxes assumed $8,000 + razing costs $15,000 вҲ’ salvage proceeds $3,000 + special assessment $10,000 = $442,000. Demolition costs (net of salvage) are part of land when the land was acquired with the intent to remove the existing structure, and special assessments for permanent improvements maintained by the government are also charged to land. $432,000 results from omitting the net razing cost of $12,000; $445,000 results from failing to offset the $3,000 salvage proceeds; $430,000 results from excluding both the razing cost and the salvage offset.
questions.explanationJa: еңҹең°гҒ®еҸ–еҫ—еҺҹдҫЎгҒ«гҒҜгҖҒеҸ–еҫ—гҒЁдҪҝз”Ёжә–еӮҷгҒ«зӣҙжҺҘеҝ…иҰҒгҒӘгҒҷгҒ№гҒҰгҒ®ж”ҜеҮәгӮ’еҗ«гӮҒгҒҫгҒҷпјҲASC 360-10-30пјүгҖӮиіје…ҘдҫЎж ј$400,000 + жұәжёҲиІ»з”Ё$12,000 + еЈІдё»гҒ®ж»һзҙҚеӣәе®ҡиіҮз”ЈзЁҺгҒ®еј•еҸ—$8,000 + ж—§е»әзү©гҒ®еҸ–еЈҠиІ»з”Ё$15,000 вҲ’ е»ғжқҗеЈІеҚҙеҸҺе…Ҙ$3,000 + дёӢж°ҙйҒ“зү№еҲҘиіҰиӘІйҮ‘$10,000 = $442,000гҖӮеңҹең°гӮ’е»әзү©ж’ӨеҺ»зӣ®зҡ„гҒ§еҸ–еҫ—гҒ—гҒҹе ҙеҗҲгҖҒеҸ–еЈҠиІ»з”ЁпјҲзҙ”йЎҚпјүгҒҜеңҹең°еҺҹдҫЎгҒ«з®—е…ҘгҒ•гӮҢгҒҫгҒҷгҖӮгҒҫгҒҹж”ҝеәңгҒҢз¶ӯжҢҒгҒҷгӮӢжҒ’д№…зҡ„ж”№иүҜгҒ«еҜҫгҒҷгӮӢзү№еҲҘиіҰиӘІйҮ‘гӮӮеңҹең°еҺҹдҫЎгҒ§гҒҷгҖӮ$432,000гҒҜеҸ–еЈҠзҙ”иІ»з”Ё$12,000гҒ®и„ұжјҸгҖҒ$445,000гҒҜе»ғжқҗеҸҺе…Ҙ$3,000гҒ®жҺ§йҷӨжјҸгӮҢгҖҒ$430,000гҒҜдёЎж–№гӮ’йҷӨеӨ–гҒ—гҒҹиӘӨгӮҠгҒ§гҒҷгҖӮ

questions.stem: Kestrel Inc. purchased an existing office building for $850,000 and paid $5,000 for title insurance. Before occupying the building, Kestrel spent $95,000 on renovations necessary to prepare the building for its intended use. During the renovation period, vandals broke windows, and Kestrel paid $10,000 to repair the vandalism damage. At what amount should Kestrel capitalize the building?
questions.explanation: The cost of a purchased building includes the purchase price plus all costs necessary to bring it to the condition for its intended use: $850,000 + $5,000 title insurance + $95,000 renovation = $950,000. Repairs of vandalism damage are not a necessary cost of readying the assetвҖ”they are neither normal nor necessary, so the $10,000 is expensed as incurred. $960,000 incorrectly capitalizes the vandalism repair; $945,000 incorrectly omits the title insurance; $860,000 incorrectly excludes the renovation costs that were necessary for the intended use.

questions.stem: Pinewood Manufacturing self-constructs a piece of production equipment for its own use. Which of the following costs should be excluded from the capitalized cost of the self-constructed equipment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Direct labor of employees working on the construction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Variable manufacturing overhead directly attributable to the construction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Interest cost incurred during the construction period on borrowings, up to avoidable interest

choices.label: D
choices.text: Abnormal amounts of wasted material and labor caused by construction inefficiencies

questions.explanation: The cost of a self-constructed asset includes direct materials, direct labor, and overhead directly attributable to the construction, plus capitalized interest during the construction period under ASC 835-20. However, abnormal amounts of wasted material, labor, or other resources resulting from inefficiencies do not add value to the asset and are charged to expense in the period incurred. Direct labor, directly attributable variable overhead, and avoidable interest during construction are all properly includable in the asset's cost.

questions.subtopic: Capitalized Interest - Specific and General Borrowings
questions.stem: Tidewater Corp. is constructing a new distribution center. Weighted-average accumulated expenditures for the year were $1,500,000. Tidewater has a construction loan of $900,000 at 6% specifically obtained for the project, and other general debt outstanding all year of $3,000,000 at 9% (actual interest of $270,000). What amount of interest should Tidewater capitalize for the year?
questions.explanation: Avoidable interest is computed by applying the specific borrowing rate to expenditures up to the specific borrowing, and the weighted-average rate on other borrowings to the excess: ($900,000 Г— 6%) + (($1,500,000 вҲ’ $900,000) Г— 9%) = $54,000 + $54,000 = $108,000. Total actual interest ($54,000 + $270,000 = $324,000) exceeds avoidable interest, so $108,000 is capitalized. $135,000 results from applying 9% to the full $1,500,000; $90,000 results from applying 6% to the full $1,500,000; $54,000 results from capitalizing only the interest on the specific construction loan and ignoring the excess expenditures.

questions.subtopic: Basket Purchase - Relative Fair Value Allocation
questions.stem: Sycamore Co. acquired land, a building, and machinery from a bankrupt competitor for a single lump-sum payment of $2,700,000. Independent appraisals indicate fair values of $1,000,000 for the land, $1,800,000 for the building, and $200,000 for the machinery. At what amount should Sycamore record the building?
questions.explanation: In a lump-sum (basket) purchase, the total cost is allocated to the individual assets based on their relative fair values. Total appraised value = $1,000,000 + $1,800,000 + $200,000 = $3,000,000. The building receives $2,700,000 Г— ($1,800,000 / $3,000,000) = $1,620,000. $1,800,000 incorrectly records the building at its full appraised value, which would overstate total assets above the actual cost paid; $1,500,000 results from an even allocation error; $900,000 applies the building's ratio to only a portion of the price.

questions.subtopic: Exchange with Commercial Substance - Boot Received
questions.stem: Ridgeline Corp. exchanged a machine (original cost $80,000, accumulated depreciation $30,000, fair value $62,000) for newer equipment with a fair value of $52,000 plus $10,000 in cash. The exchange has commercial substance. What amount of gain should Ridgeline recognize on the exchange?
questions.explanation: When a nonmonetary exchange has commercial substance, the transaction is measured at fair value and the full gain or loss is recognized immediately. The gain equals the fair value of the asset given up minus its carrying amount: $62,000 вҲ’ ($80,000 вҲ’ $30,000) = $62,000 вҲ’ $50,000 = $12,000. The new equipment is recorded at its fair value of $52,000. $0 would apply only if the exchange lacked commercial substance and no boot were received; $10,000 confuses the cash received with the gain; $1,935 results from incorrectly applying the proportional gain recognition formula ($12,000 Г— $10,000/$62,000), which applies only when the exchange lacks commercial substance.
questions.explanationJa: е•ҶжҘӯзҡ„е®ҹиіӘпјҲcommercial substanceпјүгӮ’жңүгҒҷгӮӢйқһиІЁе№ЈжҖ§иіҮз”ЈгҒ®дәӨжҸӣгҒ§гҒҜгҖҒеҸ–еј•гӮ’е…¬жӯЈдҫЎеҖӨгҒ§жё¬е®ҡгҒ—гҖҒжҗҚзӣҠгҒ®е…ЁйЎҚгӮ’еҚіжҷӮиӘҚиӯҳгҒ—гҒҫгҒҷпјҲASC 845-10-30пјүгҖӮеҲ©еҫ— = еј•жёЎиіҮз”ЈгҒ®е…¬жӯЈдҫЎеҖӨ$62,000 вҲ’ еёіз°ҝдҫЎйЎҚ($80,000 вҲ’ $30,000 = $50,000) = $12,000гҖӮеҸ—е…ҘиЁӯеӮҷгҒҜе…¬жӯЈдҫЎеҖӨ$52,000гҒ§иЁҲдёҠгҒ—гҒҫгҒҷгҖӮ$0гҒҜе•ҶжҘӯзҡ„е®ҹиіӘгӮ’ж¬ гҒҚbootгӮ’еҸ—гҒ‘еҸ–гӮүгҒӘгҒ„е ҙеҗҲгҒ®еҮҰзҗҶгҖҒ$10,000гҒҜеҸ—еҸ–зҸҫйҮ‘гҒЁеҲ©еҫ—гҒ®ж··еҗҢгҖҒ$1,935гҒҜе•ҶжҘӯзҡ„е®ҹиіӘгӮ’ж¬ гҒҸе ҙеҗҲгҒ«гҒ®гҒҝйҒ©з”ЁгҒ•гӮҢгӮӢжҜ”дҫӢиӘҚиӯҳпјҲ$12,000 Г— $10,000/$62,000пјүгҒ®иӘӨйҒ©з”ЁгҒ§гҒҷгҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Exchange Lacking Commercial Substance - Partial Gain on Boot Received
questions.stem: Meridian Co. exchanged equipment with a carrying amount of $40,000 and a fair value of $48,000 for a similar asset with a fair value of $40,000 plus $8,000 in cash. The exchange lacks commercial substance. What amount of gain should Meridian recognize?
questions.explanation: When an exchange lacks commercial substance and boot (monetary consideration) of less than 25% of the total fair value is received, a proportional amount of the gain is recognized: total gain Г— (boot received / total consideration received). Total gain = $48,000 вҲ’ $40,000 = $8,000. Boot ratio = $8,000 / $48,000 = 1/6. Recognized gain = $8,000 Г— 1/6 = $1,333 (rounded). The boot of $8,000 is 16.7% of the $48,000 total, below the 25% threshold, so full fair value accounting does not apply. $8,000 would be the full gain, recognized only if the exchange had commercial substance or boot were 25% or more; $0 ignores the partial recognition required when boot is received; $6,667 is the deferred portion of the gain rather than the recognized portion.
questions.explanationJa: е•ҶжҘӯзҡ„е®ҹиіӘгӮ’ж¬ гҒҸдәӨжҸӣгҒ§з·ҸеҜҫдҫЎгҒ®25%жңӘжәҖгҒ®bootпјҲзҸҫйҮ‘пјүгӮ’еҸ—гҒ‘еҸ–гҒЈгҒҹе ҙеҗҲгҖҒеҲ©еҫ—гҒ®дёҖйғЁгӮ’жҜ”дҫӢзҡ„гҒ«иӘҚиӯҳгҒ—гҒҫгҒҷпјҡз·ҸеҲ©еҫ— Г— (еҸ—еҸ–boot Г· еҸ—еҸ–еҜҫдҫЎз·ҸйЎҚ)гҖӮз·ҸеҲ©еҫ— = $48,000 вҲ’ $40,000 = $8,000гҖӮbootжҜ”зҺҮ = $8,000 Г· $48,000 = 1/6гҖӮиӘҚиӯҳеҲ©еҫ— = $8,000 Г— 1/6 вү’ $1,333гҖӮbootгҒҜз·ҸйЎҚгҒ®16.7%гҒ§25%еҹәжә–жңӘжәҖгҒ®гҒҹгӮҒгҖҒе…ЁйЎҚе…¬жӯЈдҫЎеҖӨеҮҰзҗҶгҒҜйҒ©з”ЁгҒ•гӮҢгҒҫгҒӣгӮ“гҖӮ$8,000гҒҜе•ҶжҘӯзҡ„е®ҹиіӘгҒҢгҒӮгӮӢе ҙеҗҲгҒҫгҒҹгҒҜbootгҒҢ25%д»ҘдёҠгҒ®е ҙеҗҲгҒ®е…ЁйЎҚиӘҚиӯҳгҖҒ$0гҒҜbootеҸ—й ҳжҷӮгҒ«еҝ…иҰҒгҒӘйғЁеҲҶиӘҚиӯҳгҒ®з„ЎиҰ–гҖҒ$6,667гҒҜиӘҚиӯҳйЎҚгҒ§гҒҜгҒӘгҒҸз№°е»¶йғЁеҲҶгҒ§гҒҷгҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Exchange Lacking Commercial Substance - Boot Paid
questions.stem: Larkspur Inc. exchanged a delivery van (original cost $60,000, accumulated depreciation $25,000, fair value $42,000) for a similar van, paying $5,000 cash in addition. The exchange lacks commercial substance. At what amount should Larkspur record the van received?
questions.explanation: When an exchange lacks commercial substance and boot is paid, no gain is recognized; the asset received is recorded at the carrying amount of the asset surrendered plus the cash paid: ($60,000 вҲ’ $25,000) + $5,000 = $35,000 + $5,000 = $40,000. The implied gain of $7,000 ($42,000 fair value вҲ’ $35,000 carrying amount) is deferred and embedded in the lower carrying amount of the new asset. $47,000 ($42,000 + $5,000) applies fair value accounting, which is appropriate only when the exchange has commercial substance; $42,000 is the fair value of the asset given up, not the recorded amount; $35,000 omits the cash paid.
questions.explanationJa: е•ҶжҘӯзҡ„е®ҹиіӘгӮ’ж¬ гҒҸдәӨжҸӣгҒ§bootгӮ’ж”Ҝжү•гҒЈгҒҹе ҙеҗҲгҖҒеҲ©еҫ—гҒҜиӘҚиӯҳгҒӣгҒҡгҖҒеҸ—е…ҘиіҮз”ЈгҒҜеј•жёЎиіҮз”ЈгҒ®еёіз°ҝдҫЎйЎҚгҒ«ж”Ҝжү•зҸҫйҮ‘гӮ’еҠ гҒҲгҒҹйЎҚгҒ§иЁҲдёҠгҒ—гҒҫгҒҷпјҡ($60,000 вҲ’ $25,000) + $5,000 = $40,000гҖӮеҗ«гҒҝзӣҠ$7,000пјҲе…¬жӯЈдҫЎеҖӨ$42,000 вҲ’ еёіз°ҝдҫЎйЎҚ$35,000пјүгҒҜз№°гӮҠе»¶гҒ№гӮүгӮҢгҖҒж–°иіҮз”ЈгҒ®дҪҺгҒ„иЁҲдёҠйЎҚгҒ«з№”гӮҠиҫјгҒҫгӮҢгҒҫгҒҷгҖӮ$47,000гҒҜе•ҶжҘӯзҡ„е®ҹиіӘгҒҢгҒӮгӮӢе ҙеҗҲгҒ®е…¬жӯЈдҫЎеҖӨгғҷгғјгӮ№пјҲ$42,000 + $5,000пјүгҖҒ$42,000гҒҜеј•жёЎиіҮз”ЈгҒ®е…¬жӯЈдҫЎеҖӨгҒқгҒ®гӮӮгҒ®гҖҒ$35,000гҒҜж”Ҝжү•зҸҫйҮ‘гҒ®еҠ з®—жјҸгӮҢгҒ§гҒҷгҖӮ

questions.stem: Crestline Corp. purchased a stamping machine for $310,000 with an estimated salvage value of $10,000. The machine is expected to operate for a total of 60,000 machine hours over its life. During Year 1, the machine operated for 12,000 hours. What is Year 1 depreciation expense under the units-of-production method?
questions.explanation: Under the units-of-production method, the depreciation rate per unit of activity is (cost вҲ’ salvage value) / total estimated activity: ($310,000 вҲ’ $10,000) / 60,000 hours = $5.00 per hour. Year 1 depreciation = $5.00 Г— 12,000 hours = $60,000. $62,000 results from failing to deduct the salvage value ($310,000 / 60,000 Г— 12,000); $50,000 and $75,000 result from using incorrect hour fractions of the depreciable base.
questions.explanationJa: з”ҹз”Јй«ҳжҜ”дҫӢжі•гҒ§гҒҜгҖҒеҚҳдҪҚеҪ“гҒҹгӮҠе„ҹеҚҙзҺҮ = (еҸ–еҫ—еҺҹдҫЎ вҲ’ ж®ӢеӯҳдҫЎйЎҚ) Г· з·ҸиҰӢз©ҚзЁјеғҚйҮҸгҒ§иЁҲз®—гҒ—гҒҫгҒҷпјҡ($310,000 вҲ’ $10,000) Г· 60,000жҷӮй–“ = $5.00/жҷӮй–“гҖӮYear 1гҒ®жёӣдҫЎе„ҹеҚҙиІ» = $5.00 Г— 12,000жҷӮй–“ = $60,000гҖӮ$62,000гҒҜж®ӢеӯҳдҫЎйЎҚгҒ®жҺ§йҷӨжјҸгӮҢпјҲ$310,000 Г· 60,000 Г— 12,000пјүгҖҒ$50,000гҒЁ$75,000гҒҜзЁјеғҚеүІеҗҲгҒ®йҒ©з”ЁиӘӨгӮҠгҒ«гӮҲгӮӢеҖӨгҒ§гҒҷгҖӮ

questions.stem: Bayfield Inc. purchased equipment on January 1, Year 1 for $200,000. The equipment has an estimated salvage value of $20,000 and a useful life of 8 years. Using the double-declining balance method, what is depreciation expense for Year 2?
questions.explanation: The double-declining balance rate is 2 / 8 years = 25%, applied to the beginning carrying amount without deducting salvage value. Year 1: $200,000 Г— 25% = $50,000. Year 2: ($200,000 вҲ’ $50,000) Г— 25% = $150,000 Г— 25% = $37,500. Salvage value is ignored in the annual computation but serves as a floor below which the carrying amount cannot fall. $45,000 incorrectly deducts salvage from the base ($180,000 Г— 25%); $50,000 is the Year 1 amount; $33,750 results from compounding the salvage-adjusted base into the second year ($135,000 Г— 25%).
questions.explanationJa: е®ҡзҺҮжі•пјҲ200%е®ҡзҺҮжі•пјүгҒ®е„ҹеҚҙзҺҮгҒҜ 2 Г· 8е№ҙ = 25%гҒ§гҖҒж®ӢеӯҳдҫЎйЎҚгӮ’жҺ§йҷӨгҒ—гҒӘгҒ„жңҹйҰ–еёіз°ҝдҫЎйЎҚгҒ«йҒ©з”ЁгҒ—гҒҫгҒҷгҖӮYear 1: $200,000 Г— 25% = $50,000гҖӮYear 2: ($200,000 вҲ’ $50,000) Г— 25% = $37,500гҖӮж®ӢеӯҳдҫЎйЎҚгҒҜе№ҙж¬ЎиЁҲз®—гҒ§гҒҜз„ЎиҰ–гҒ•гӮҢгҒҫгҒҷгҒҢгҖҒеёіз°ҝдҫЎйЎҚгҒҢгҒқгӮҢгӮ’дёӢеӣһгӮүгҒӘгҒ„гҒҹгӮҒгҒ®дёӢйҷҗгҒЁгҒ—гҒҰж©ҹиғҪгҒ—гҒҫгҒҷгҖӮ$45,000гҒҜж®ӢеӯҳдҫЎйЎҚгӮ’жҺ§йҷӨгҒ—гҒҹеҹәзӨҺйЎҚгҒ®иӘӨгӮҠпјҲ$180,000 Г— 25%пјүгҖҒ$50,000гҒҜYear 1гҒ®йҮ‘йЎҚгҖҒ$33,750гҒҜж®ӢеӯҳдҫЎйЎҚжҺ§йҷӨеҫҢгҒ®еҹәзӨҺйЎҚгӮ’2е№ҙзӣ®гҒ«еј•гҒҚз¶ҷгҒ„гҒ иӘӨгӮҠгҒ§гҒҷгҖӮ

questions.stem: Oakhurst Co. purchased machinery on January 1, Year 1 for $150,000 with a salvage value of $30,000 and a useful life of 5 years. Using the sum-of-the-years'-digits method, what is depreciation expense for Year 3?
questions.explanation: The sum of the years' digits for a 5-year life is 5 + 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 15. The depreciable base is $150,000 вҲ’ $30,000 = $120,000. Year 3 uses the fraction 3/15 (remaining life at the beginning of Year 3 over the sum): $120,000 Г— 3/15 = $24,000. $40,000 is the Year 1 amount ($120,000 Г— 5/15); $32,000 is the Year 2 amount ($120,000 Г— 4/15); $16,000 is the Year 4 amount ($120,000 Г— 2/15).

questions.subtopic: Change in Depreciation Estimate - Life and Salvage Revised
questions.stem: Glenview Corp. purchased equipment on January 1, Year 1 for $360,000 with no salvage value and an 8-year useful life, depreciated straight-line. At the beginning of Year 4, Glenview determines that the equipment will last only 5 more years from that date and will have a salvage value of $25,000. What is depreciation expense for Year 4?
questions.explanation: A change in estimated useful life or salvage value is a change in accounting estimate, accounted for prospectively. Accumulated depreciation through Year 3 = ($360,000 / 8) Г— 3 = $135,000, leaving a carrying amount of $225,000. The remaining depreciable amount is $225,000 вҲ’ $25,000 new salvage = $200,000, spread over the 5 remaining years: $200,000 / 5 = $40,000. $45,000 ignores the new salvage value ($225,000 / 5); $41,875 spreads the revised depreciable cost over the original 8-year life (($360,000 вҲ’ $25,000) / 8); $25,000 incorrectly divides the remaining base by the original life.

questions.stem: Which of the following expenditures related to existing machinery should be expensed as incurred?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Replacement of a major motor that extends the machine's useful life by five years

choices.label: B
choices.text: Routine annual maintenance and lubrication performed under a service contract

choices.label: C
choices.text: Installation of an attachment that increases the machine's hourly output capacity

choices.label: D
choices.text: Modification of the machine required to produce a newly designed product line

questions.explanation: Routine maintenance merely keeps an asset in its existing operating condition and provides no benefit beyond the current period, so it is expensed as incurred. Expenditures that extend useful life (the motor replacement), increase capacity (the output attachment), or adapt the asset to a new use (the product-line modification) provide future economic benefits and are capitalized, either as an addition to the asset's cost or, for life-extending expenditures, sometimes as a debit to accumulated depreciation.

questions.subtopic: Impairment Loss Measurement - Held and Used
questions.stem: Driftwood Corp. has manufacturing equipment with a carrying amount of $800,000. Due to a significant adverse change in the business climate, Driftwood tests the equipment for impairment. The sum of undiscounted expected future cash flows is $750,000, the present value of those cash flows is $640,000, and the equipment's fair value determined from market prices for similar assets is $620,000. What impairment loss should Driftwood recognize?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 360, the recoverability test compares the carrying amount ($800,000) with undiscounted future cash flows ($750,000); because the carrying amount is not recoverable, an impairment loss is measured as the excess of carrying amount over fair value: $800,000 вҲ’ $620,000 = $180,000. When a market-based fair value is available, it is used rather than the present value of entity-specific cash flows. $50,000 incorrectly measures the loss using undiscounted cash flows; $160,000 uses the present value of cash flows ($640,000) even though an observable fair value exists; $0 would apply only if undiscounted cash flows had exceeded the carrying amount.

questions.stem: Stonebridge Co. owns a production facility with a carrying amount of $900,000. An impairment indicator exists. The undiscounted expected future cash flows from the facility total $950,000, and the facility's fair value is $700,000. What impairment loss, if any, should Stonebridge recognize?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 360, an impairment loss for a held-and-used asset is recognized only if the carrying amount is not recoverableвҖ”that is, only if it exceeds the sum of undiscounted future cash flows. Here, undiscounted cash flows of $950,000 exceed the carrying amount of $900,000, so the asset passes the recoverability test and no impairment loss is recognized, even though fair value ($700,000) is below carrying amount. $200,000 (carrying amount minus fair value) would be recorded only if the recoverability test had failed; $250,000 and $50,000 are differences between other figure pairs that have no significance once the test is passed.

questions.stem: On October 1, Year 1, Fairmont Corp. committed to a plan to sell a warehouse and properly classified it as held for sale. The warehouse has a carrying amount of $420,000, a fair value of $400,000, and estimated costs to sell of $30,000. What loss should Fairmont recognize upon classification as held for sale?
questions.explanation: A long-lived asset classified as held for sale is measured at the lower of its carrying amount or fair value less costs to sell. Fair value less costs to sell = $400,000 вҲ’ $30,000 = $370,000, which is below the $420,000 carrying amount, so a loss of $420,000 вҲ’ $370,000 = $50,000 is recognized. Depreciation also ceases upon held-for-sale classification. $20,000 compares carrying amount only with fair value, ignoring costs to sell; $30,000 is just the costs to sell; $0 ignores the required write-down.

questions.subtopic: Held for Sale - Subsequent Recovery Limitation
questions.stem: In Year 1, Westgate Co. classified a building as held for sale and wrote it down from its carrying amount of $420,000 to its fair value less costs to sell of $370,000. At the end of Year 2, while the building is still held for sale, its fair value less costs to sell has increased to $450,000. What gain should Westgate recognize in Year 2?
questions.explanation: A subsequent increase in fair value less costs to sell of a held-for-sale asset is recognized as a gain, but only up to the cumulative impairment losses previously recognized. The cumulative prior write-down was $50,000 ($420,000 вҲ’ $370,000), so the recovery gain is limited to $50,000, bringing the carrying amount back to $420,000вҖ”not to the $450,000 current fair value less costs to sell. $80,000 would write the asset up above its pre-impairment carrying amount, which is prohibited; $30,000 is the excess above the original carrying amount that cannot be recognized; $0 ignores the permitted recovery for held-for-sale assets.

questions.subtopic: ARO - Total First-Year Expense (Accretion plus Depreciation)
questions.stem: On January 1, Year 1, Cobalt Energy placed a storage facility in service and recognized an asset retirement obligation with a fair value of $200,000, using a credit-adjusted risk-free rate of 8%. The related asset retirement cost is depreciated straight-line over the facility's 10-year life with no salvage value. What total expense related to the ARO and the asset retirement cost should Cobalt recognize in Year 1?
questions.explanation: Recognizing an ARO produces two recurring charges: (1) accretion expense on the liability, $200,000 Г— 8% = $16,000, and (2) depreciation of the capitalized asset retirement cost, $200,000 / 10 years = $20,000. Total Year 1 expense = $16,000 + $20,000 = $36,000. $20,000 includes only the depreciation component; $16,000 includes only the accretion component; $40,000 incorrectly doubles the depreciation rather than adding accretion.
questions.explanationJa: ASC 410-20гҒ§гҒҜгҖҒAROиӘҚиӯҳеҫҢгҒ«жҜҺжңҹ2гҒӨгҒ®иІ»з”ЁгҒҢз”ҹгҒҳгҒҫгҒҷпјҡ(1) иІ еӮөгҒ®жҷӮгҒ®зөҢйҒҺгҒ«гӮҲгӮӢеў—еҠ йЎҚпјҲaccretion expenseпјү= $200,000 Г— 8% = $16,000гҖҒ(2) иіҮз”ЈиЁҲдёҠгҒ—гҒҹйҷӨеҺ»иІ»з”ЁпјҲasset retirement costпјүгҒ®жёӣдҫЎе„ҹеҚҙиІ» = $200,000 Г· 10е№ҙ = $20,000гҖӮYear 1гҒ®еҗҲиЁҲиІ»з”Ё = $16,000 + $20,000 = $36,000гҖӮ$20,000гҒҜжёӣдҫЎе„ҹеҚҙгҒ®гҒҝгҖҒ$16,000гҒҜaccretionгҒ®гҒҝгҒ§гҖҒгҒ„гҒҡгӮҢгӮӮзүҮж–№гҒ®ж§ӢжҲҗиҰҒзҙ гҒ®иҰӢиҗҪгҒЁгҒ—гҒ§гҒҷгҖӮ$40,000гҒҜжёӣдҫЎе„ҹеҚҙгҒ®дәҢйҮҚиЁҲдёҠгҒ«гӮҲгӮӢиӘӨгӮҠгҒ§гҒҷгҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Depletion - Cost of Goods Sold vs Inventory
questions.stem: Galena Mining acquired a mineral property for $7,200,000 and incurred $800,000 of development costs. Galena expects to incur restoration costs of $500,000 (recognized as part of the depletion base) and estimates the land's residual value after restoration at $500,000. Estimated recoverable reserves are 4,000,000 tons. During Year 1, Galena extracted 600,000 tons and sold 500,000 tons. What amount of depletion should be included in Year 1 cost of goods sold?
questions.explanation: The depletion base = acquisition cost $7,200,000 + development $800,000 + restoration $500,000 вҲ’ residual value $500,000 = $8,000,000. The depletion rate = $8,000,000 / 4,000,000 tons = $2.00 per ton. Total depletion for extraction = 600,000 Г— $2.00 = $1,200,000, but only the portion related to tons sold flows to cost of goods sold: 500,000 Г— $2.00 = $1,000,000. The remaining $200,000 (100,000 unsold tons) stays in inventory. $1,200,000 is total depletion capitalized to extracted units, not COGS; $1,062,500 results from omitting the residual value from the computation ($8,500,000 / 4,000,000 Г— 500,000); $900,000 results from an incorrect rate.
questions.explanationJa: жёӣиҖ—е„ҹеҚҙеҹәзӨҺйЎҚ = еҸ–еҫ—еҺҹдҫЎ$7,200,000 + й–ӢзҷәиІ»$800,000 + еҺҹзҠ¶еӣһеҫ©иІ»$500,000 вҲ’ еңҹең°гҒ®ж®ӢеӯҳдҫЎеҖӨ$500,000 = $8,000,000гҖӮжёӣиҖ—зҺҮ = $8,000,000 Г· 4,000,000гғҲгғі = $2.00/гғҲгғігҖӮжҺЎжҺҳеҲҶгҒ®з·ҸжёӣиҖ—йЎҚгҒҜ600,000гғҲгғі Г— $2.00 = $1,200,000гҒ§гҒҷгҒҢгҖҒеЈІдёҠеҺҹдҫЎгҒ«гҒҜиІ©еЈІеҲҶгҒ®гҒҝгҒҢиЁҲдёҠгҒ•гӮҢгҒҫгҒҷпјҡ500,000гғҲгғі Г— $2.00 = $1,000,000гҖӮжңӘиІ©еЈІгҒ®100,000гғҲгғіеҲҶ$200,000гҒҜжЈҡеҚёиіҮз”ЈгҒ«ж®ӢгӮҠгҒҫгҒҷгҖӮ$1,200,000гҒҜжҺЎжҺҳеҲҶе…ЁйЎҚгӮ’COGSгҒЁгҒ—гҒҹиӘӨгӮҠгҖҒ$1,062,500гҒҜж®ӢеӯҳдҫЎеҖӨгҒ®жҺ§йҷӨжјҸгӮҢгҖҒ$900,000гҒҜзҺҮгҒ®иЁҲз®—иӘӨгӮҠгҒ§гҒҷгҖӮ

questions.subtopic: Goodwill Impairment - Loss Less Than Goodwill Balance
questions.stem: Brookfield Corp.'s consumer products reporting unit has a carrying amount of $4,500,000, including goodwill of $800,000. The reporting unit's fair value is determined to be $4,000,000. Under ASC 350 (post-ASU 2017-04), what goodwill impairment loss should Brookfield recognize?
questions.explanation: Under the one-step goodwill impairment test, the loss equals the excess of the reporting unit's carrying amount over its fair value, limited to the carrying amount of goodwill: $4,500,000 вҲ’ $4,000,000 = $500,000, which is less than the $800,000 goodwill balance, so the full $500,000 is recognized. $800,000 would write off all goodwill even though the shortfall is smaller; $0 would apply only if fair value equaled or exceeded the carrying amount; $300,000 is the residual goodwill after impairment, not the loss itself.

questions.subtopic: Goodwill Impairment - Loss Capped at Goodwill Balance
questions.stem: Lakeshore Inc.'s industrial reporting unit has a carrying amount of $6,000,000, including goodwill of $700,000. A quantitative impairment test determines the reporting unit's fair value to be $5,000,000. What goodwill impairment loss should Lakeshore recognize under ASC 350?
questions.explanation: The goodwill impairment loss is the amount by which the reporting unit's carrying amount exceeds its fair value ($6,000,000 вҲ’ $5,000,000 = $1,000,000), but the loss cannot exceed the carrying amount of goodwill allocated to the reporting unit. Because goodwill is only $700,000, the loss is capped at $700,000. The remaining $300,000 shortfall is not recognized against other assets under the goodwill test (other long-lived assets are tested separately under ASC 360 before the goodwill test). $1,000,000 ignores the goodwill cap; $0 ignores the failed test; $300,000 is the uncapped excess over the goodwill balance.

questions.subtopic: Indefinite-Life Intangible Impairment - No Recoverability Test
questions.stem: Summit Media holds an indefinite-life broadcast license with a carrying amount of $900,000. At the annual impairment test date, the license's fair value is $760,000, and the undiscounted future cash flows expected from the license total $1,000,000. What impairment loss should Summit recognize?
questions.explanation: Indefinite-life intangible assets (other than goodwill) are tested for impairment under ASC 350-30 by comparing carrying amount directly with fair valueвҖ”there is no undiscounted cash flow recoverability test. Because fair value ($760,000) is below carrying amount ($900,000), an impairment loss of $140,000 is recognized, even though undiscounted cash flows exceed the carrying amount. $0 incorrectly applies the ASC 360 recoverability screen, which is available only for assets being amortized or depreciated (finite-life or long-lived assets held and used); $100,000 and $240,000 compare the wrong figure pairs.

questions.stem: During Year 1, Novatech Corp. incurred the following costs: laboratory research aimed at discovering new technology, $120,000; design, construction, and testing of a preproduction prototype, $90,000; quality control testing during commercial production, $50,000; legal fees to obtain a patent on the new technology, $30,000; and market research for the new product, $25,000. What amount should Novatech report as research and development expense for Year 1?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 730, R&D includes laboratory research aimed at new knowledge ($120,000) and the design, construction, and testing of preproduction prototypes ($90,000), for a total of $210,000. Quality control during commercial production is a period production cost, not R&D; legal fees to obtain a patent are capitalized as part of the patent's cost; and market research is a selling-related expense excluded from R&D. $315,000 includes all five items; $240,000 incorrectly adds the patent legal fees; $260,000 incorrectly adds quality control costs.

questions.subtopic: R&D Equipment with Alternative Future Uses
questions.stem: On January 1, Year 1, Helix Labs purchased testing equipment for $400,000 to be used in a current research project. The equipment has a 5-year useful life, no salvage value, and alternative future uses in other research projects and in production. The equipment was used exclusively on the research project during Year 1. What amount related to this equipment should Helix include in research and development expense for Year 1?
questions.explanation: Equipment acquired for R&D activities that has alternative future uses is capitalized and depreciated; the depreciation for the periods the equipment is used in R&D is classified as R&D expense. Year 1 depreciation = $400,000 / 5 = $80,000, charged to R&D expense. $400,000 would be correct only if the equipment had no alternative future uses, in which case the entire cost is expensed immediately; $0 ignores that the depreciation must be classified as R&D while the asset is used in research; $320,000 is the year-end carrying amount, not an expense.

questions.subtopic: Software to Be Sold - Costs Capitalized After Technological Feasibility
questions.stem: Quantum Soft is developing a software product to be sold to customers. During Year 1, it incurred $300,000 of costs before establishing technological feasibility, $500,000 of coding and testing costs after technological feasibility was established but before the product was available for general release, and $100,000 of customer support and maintenance costs after general release. Under ASC 985-20, what amount should Quantum capitalize as software development costs?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 985-20, costs incurred before technological feasibility is established are expensed as R&D ($300,000). Costs incurred after technological feasibility but before general releaseвҖ”coding, testing, and producing product mastersвҖ”are capitalized ($500,000). Costs after general release, such as maintenance and customer support, are expensed ($100,000). Therefore, only $500,000 is capitalized. $800,000 incorrectly capitalizes the pre-feasibility R&D costs; $900,000 capitalizes everything; $600,000 incorrectly capitalizes the post-release support costs.

questions.stem: Under ASC 350-40, amortization of capitalized internal-use software costs should begin when:

choices.label: A
choices.text: The application development stage begins

choices.label: B
choices.text: The software is ready for its intended use, regardless of whether it has been placed in service

choices.label: C
choices.text: Management commits funding to the software project

choices.label: D
choices.text: The post-implementation/operation stage costs are first incurred

questions.explanation: ASC 350-40-35-6 requires amortization of internal-use software to begin when the software is ready for its intended useвҖ”that is, after all substantial testing is completedвҖ”regardless of whether the software will be placed in service in planned stages that may extend beyond a reporting period. The start of the application development stage marks when capitalization begins, not amortization; management commitment of funding occurs in the preliminary project stage; and the post-implementation stage relates to training and maintenance costs, which are expensed.

questions.subtopic: Franchise - Initial Fee Amortization and Continuing Fees
questions.stem: On January 1, Year 1, Brava Foods paid an initial franchise fee of $240,000 for a franchise with a 10-year useful life. The franchise agreement also requires Brava to pay a continuing franchise fee equal to 5% of annual franchise revenue. Franchise revenue for Year 1 was $600,000. What total franchise-related expense should Brava report for Year 1?
questions.explanation: The initial franchise fee is capitalized as an intangible asset and amortized over its useful life: $240,000 / 10 years = $24,000 per year. Continuing franchise fees are operating expenses of the period in which the related revenue is earned: $600,000 Г— 5% = $30,000. Total Year 1 franchise expense = $24,000 + $30,000 = $54,000. $24,000 omits the continuing fee; $30,000 omits the amortization; $270,000 incorrectly expenses the entire initial fee immediately plus the continuing fee.

questions.stem: At the beginning of Year 5, Vexel Corp.'s patent had a carrying amount of $180,000 with a remaining useful life of 6 years. On January 2, Year 5, Vexel paid $60,000 in legal fees to successfully defend the patent against an infringement suit. What amount of patent amortization expense should Vexel report for Year 5?
questions.explanation: Legal fees incurred in successfully defending a patent are capitalized because they establish the holder's legal rights and benefit future periods. The new carrying amount of $180,000 + $60,000 = $240,000 is amortized over the remaining 6-year useful life: $240,000 / 6 = $40,000 per year. $30,000 amortizes only the pre-defense carrying amount ($180,000 / 6); $90,000 expenses the legal fees immediately and adds the $30,000 amortization, which is the treatment for an unsuccessful defense; $60,000 has no valid computational basis here.

questions.subtopic: Trademark - Useful Life Shorter Than Legal Life
questions.stem: Halcyon Brands purchased a registered trademark for $96,000. The trademark's registration has a remaining legal life of 10 years and is renewable indefinitely at minimal cost. However, management plans to phase out the branded product line in 8 years and will not renew the trademark thereafter. What annual amortization expense should Halcyon record?
questions.explanation: A recognized intangible asset is amortized over its useful life to the reporting entity, which reflects the period over which the asset is expected to contribute to cash flows. Although the trademark is legally renewable indefinitely, management's plan to discontinue the product in 8 years limits the useful life to 8 years: $96,000 / 8 = $12,000 per year. $9,600 uses the 10-year legal life; $0 treats the trademark as indefinite-life, which is inappropriate because the entity-specific useful life is limited; $4,800 has no valid basis.

questions.stem: Axion Corp. acquired in-process research and development (IPR&D) in a business combination and recognized it as an indefinite-life intangible asset at its acquisition-date fair value of $3,000,000. Two years later, the R&D project is successfully completed and the resulting technology is placed in service. How should Axion account for the IPR&D asset upon completion of the project?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Write off the entire $3,000,000 to expense because R&D costs must be expensed

choices.label: B
choices.text: Assign the asset a finite useful life and begin amortizing it over that life

choices.label: C
choices.text: Continue carrying it as an indefinite-life asset subject only to annual impairment testing

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reclassify the amount to goodwill

questions.explanation: IPR&D acquired in a business combination is capitalized at fair value and treated as an indefinite-life intangible asset during the development period, tested for impairment annually. When the project is completed, the asset's useful life becomes determinable: the entity assigns a finite useful life and amortizes the asset over the period it is expected to contribute to cash flows. Writing it off to expense applies only to internally incurred R&D costs under ASC 730 (or if the acquired project is abandoned); continuing indefinite-life treatment is inappropriate once the development is complete; reclassification to goodwill is never appropriate for a separately recognized identifiable asset.

questions.stem: Juniper Corp. incurred the following costs for a new corporate site: land purchase price $600,000; perimeter fencing $40,000; paved parking lots $70,000; permanent landscaping (earth-moving and contouring with indefinite life) $20,000; and outdoor lighting systems $15,000. What total amount should Juniper classify as depreciable land improvements?
questions.explanation: Land improvements with limited useful lives are depreciable: fencing $40,000 + parking lots $70,000 + outdoor lighting $15,000 = $125,000. Permanent landscaping with an indefinite life (earth-moving, contouring) is added to the non-depreciable land account, as is the $600,000 purchase price. $145,000 incorrectly includes the permanent landscaping; $110,000 omits the lighting; $85,000 omits the parking lots.

questions.subtopic: Capitalized Interest - Conditions to Begin Capitalization
questions.stem: Under ASC 835-20, which of the following is NOT a condition required for interest capitalization on a qualifying asset to begin?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Expenditures for the asset have been made

choices.label: B
choices.text: Activities necessary to get the asset ready for its intended use are in progress

choices.label: C
choices.text: A borrowing specifically designated for the construction of the asset has been arranged

choices.label: D
choices.text: Interest cost is being incurred

questions.explanation: Interest capitalization begins when three conditions are all met: (1) expenditures for the asset have been made, (2) activities necessary to get the asset ready for its intended use are in progress, and (3) interest cost is being incurred. A specific construction borrowing is not requiredвҖ”interest on general borrowings is capitalizable using the weighted-average rate, so an entity with any outstanding debt can capitalize interest even without project-specific financing.

questions.subtopic: Nonreciprocal Transfer - Donation of Asset to Charity
questions.stem: Cypress Corp. donated a parcel of land to a charitable organization. The land had a carrying amount of $100,000 and a fair value of $150,000 at the date of donation. What gain, if any, should Cypress recognize on the donation?
questions.explanation: A nonreciprocal transfer of a nonmonetary asset to another entity is recorded at the fair value of the asset given up, with a gain or loss recognized for the difference between fair value and carrying amount. Cypress records contribution expense of $150,000 and recognizes a gain of $150,000 вҲ’ $100,000 = $50,000. $150,000 is the contribution expense, not the gain; $100,000 is the carrying amount removed from the books; $0 incorrectly applies carrying-amount accounting, which does not apply to nonreciprocal transfers measured at fair value.

questions.stem: Falcon Corp. acquired 100% of Target Inc. in a business combination. Falcon paid $5,000,000 cash to Target's shareholders and also paid $200,000 in legal and investment-banking fees directly related to the acquisition. The fair value of Target's identifiable net assets was $4,400,000. What amount of goodwill should Falcon recognize?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 805, acquisition-related costs such as legal, accounting, and investment-banking fees are expensed as incurred and are not part of the consideration transferred. Goodwill = consideration transferred $5,000,000 вҲ’ fair value of identifiable net assets $4,400,000 = $600,000. The $200,000 of fees is charged to expense in the period of the acquisition. $800,000 incorrectly adds the acquisition costs to consideration; $400,000 incorrectly subtracts them; $0 ignores the excess of consideration over net assets.

questions.stem: Pemberton Corp. owns a factory containing asbestos that, under current law, must be removed and disposed of in a special manner whenever the factory undergoes major renovation or is demolished. Pemberton has no current plans to renovate or demolish the factory, but the building will not last forever. Under ASC 410-20, how should Pemberton account for the asbestos removal obligation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recognize no liability until renovation or demolition activities actually begin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Disclose the obligation but recognize no liability because the timing of settlement is uncertain

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recognize a liability only if removal is expected within the next 12 months

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recognize a liability at fair value currently if the fair value can be reasonably estimated, because the obligation to perform is unconditional

questions.explanation: ASC 410-20 addresses conditional asset retirement obligations: even though the timing and method of settlement are conditional on a future event (renovation or demolition), the legal obligation to perform the asbestos removal is unconditional and exists today. The entity must recognize a liability at fair value when incurred if fair value can be reasonably estimated, incorporating the uncertainty about timing and method into the measurement (for example, through expected present value techniques). Deferring recognition until activity begins, merely disclosing, or applying a 12-month threshold all contradict the recognition requirements for conditional AROs.

questions.stem: Tarragon Corp. purchased a production machine and incurred the following costs: invoice price $150,000; freight-in $6,000; insurance during transit $2,400; installation $10,000; test runs prior to placing the machine in service $3,600; and training of machine operators $8,000. What amount should Tarragon capitalize as the cost of the machine?
questions.explanation: All costs necessary to acquire the asset and bring it to the condition and location for its intended use are capitalized: invoice price $150,000 + freight-in $6,000 + transit insurance $2,400 + installation $10,000 + test runs $3,600 = $172,000. Employee training costs of $8,000 are a period expense because they relate to the employees, not to readying the asset itself. The $180,000 figure incorrectly includes training; $168,400 incorrectly excludes the test-run costs, which are part of readying the asset; $160,000 omits freight, insurance, and test runs.

questions.subtopic: Lump-Sum Purchase - Allocation and First-Year Depreciation
questions.stem: On January 1, Year 1, Verdant Co. acquired land, a building, and equipment in a single transaction for $900,000. Appraised fair values were: land $250,000, building $500,000, equipment $250,000. The building has a 30-year useful life and the equipment a 5-year useful life, both with no salvage value, depreciated straight-line. What is Verdant's total depreciation expense for Year 1?
questions.explanation: Allocate the $900,000 price by relative fair values (total appraised $1,000,000): land $900,000 Г— 25% = $225,000; building $900,000 Г— 50% = $450,000; equipment $900,000 Г— 25% = $225,000. Depreciation: building $450,000 / 30 = $15,000; equipment $225,000 / 5 = $45,000; total $60,000. Land is not depreciated. The $66,667 figure incorrectly depreciates the unallocated appraised values ($500,000/30 + $250,000/5); $67,500 incorrectly adds depreciation on the allocated land cost; $45,000 omits the building depreciation.

questions.subtopic: Capitalized Interest - Limited to Actual Interest Incurred
questions.stem: Wexford Corp. is constructing a qualifying asset. Weighted-average accumulated expenditures for the year were $3,000,000, and the appropriate capitalization rate is 10%. Total interest actually incurred on all borrowings during the year was $240,000. What amount of interest should Wexford capitalize for the year?
questions.explanation: Avoidable interest is $3,000,000 Г— 10% = $300,000, but under ASC 835-20 the amount capitalized cannot exceed total interest actually incurred during the period. Because actual interest was only $240,000, capitalized interest is limited to $240,000. The $300,000 amount ignores the actual-interest ceiling; $60,000 is merely the excess of avoidable over actual interest; capitalizing nothing is wrong because the asset qualifies and interest was incurred.

questions.stem: Brindle Corp. is constructing a warehouse that is a qualifying asset under ASC 835-20. Midway through the year, Brindle intentionally suspended substantially all construction activities for four months while management re-evaluated the project's design, although interest on the construction loan continued to accrue. How should Brindle treat the interest incurred during the four-month suspension?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Capitalize it, because interest capitalization continues as long as the loan is outstanding

choices.label: B
choices.text: Capitalize it, because the suspension was shorter than one year

choices.label: C
choices.text: Expense it, because interest capitalization ceases when activities necessary to ready the asset are intentionally suspended for an extended period

choices.label: D
choices.text: Defer it as a separate asset and amortize it after construction resumes

questions.explanation: Interest is capitalized only while activities necessary to get the asset ready for its intended use are in progress. When an entity intentionally suspends substantially all construction activities for an extended period, capitalization ceases and interest incurred during the suspension is expensed. Brief, normal interruptions (such as weather delays) do not stop capitalization, but a deliberate four-month halt does. The notion that capitalization continues whenever the loan is outstanding confuses incurring interest with qualifying for capitalization, and there is no one-year grace period or separate deferral asset for such interest.

questions.stem: Calloway Inc. purchased equipment on October 1, Year 1 for $120,000. The equipment has an estimated salvage value of $10,000 and a 5-year useful life. Calloway uses the double-declining balance method. What is depreciation expense for Year 2?
questions.explanation: The DDB rate is 2/5 = 40%, applied to beginning book value (salvage is ignored in the computation but acts as a floor). Year 1 (3 months): $120,000 Г— 40% Г— 3/12 = $12,000. Year 2: ($120,000 вҲ’ $12,000) Г— 40% = $43,200. The $48,000 figure applies 40% to the original cost without reducing it for Year 1 depreciation; $28,800 incorrectly assumes a full year of Year 1 depreciation ($48,000) before computing Year 2; $39,600 incorrectly deducts salvage value from the depreciation base.

questions.stem: An entity acquires a machine at the beginning of the year and expects to use it evenly over its useful life. Assuming the same cost, salvage value, and useful life under each method, which depreciation method will generally produce the highest depreciation expense in the first year?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Straight-line

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sum-of-the-years'-digits

choices.label: C
choices.text: Units-of-production, assuming even usage over the asset's life

choices.label: D
choices.text: Double-declining balance

questions.explanation: Double-declining balance applies twice the straight-line rate to the full cost (salvage is ignored in the base), so its first-year charge exceeds the other methods. Sum-of-the-years'-digits is also accelerated but applies its largest fraction to cost less salvage, producing a smaller first-year amount than DDB. Straight-line spreads cost evenly, and units-of-production with even usage approximates straight-line, so neither front-loads expense.

questions.subtopic: Change in Depreciation Method - Accounting Treatment
questions.stem: At the beginning of Year 4, Mercer Co. changed its depreciation method for a class of equipment from double-declining balance to straight-line because the new method better reflects the expected pattern of consumption of benefits. How should Mercer account for this change?

choices.label: A
choices.text: As a change in accounting estimate effected by a change in accounting principle, applied prospectively

choices.label: B
choices.text: As a change in accounting principle, applied retrospectively to all prior periods presented

choices.label: C
choices.text: As a correction of an error, with a prior-period adjustment to beginning retained earnings

choices.label: D
choices.text: By recognizing the cumulative effect of the change in current-period net income

questions.explanation: Under ASC 250, a change in depreciation method is a change in accounting estimate effected by a change in accounting principle. It is accounted for prospectively: the remaining book value is depreciated under the new method over the remaining useful life. Retrospective restatement applies to pure changes in principle, not to this hybrid; treating it as an error correction is wrong because no error occurred; and the cumulative-effect-in-income approach was eliminated by current GAAP.

questions.subtopic: Depreciation After Impairment - New Cost Basis
questions.stem: At the end of Year 3, Dorset Corp. recognized an impairment loss on equipment held for use, reducing its carrying amount from $480,000 to its fair value of $300,000. At that date, the equipment had a remaining useful life of 6 years and no salvage value. Dorset uses straight-line depreciation. What is depreciation expense for Year 4?
questions.explanation: After an impairment of an asset held for use, the reduced carrying amount becomes the asset's new cost basis, and restoration of the loss is prohibited. Depreciation is computed on the new basis: $300,000 / 6 years = $50,000. The $80,000 figure incorrectly depreciates the pre-impairment carrying amount ($480,000 / 6); $30,000 incorrectly depreciates only the impairment loss ($180,000 / 6); $96,000 spreads the old basis over 5 years instead of the remaining 6.

questions.subtopic: Held for Sale - Cessation of Depreciation
questions.stem: Larkin Co. owns a building with annual straight-line depreciation of $48,000. On April 1, Year 5, the building met all criteria for classification as held for sale and was so classified. The building remained unsold at December 31, Year 5. What amount of depreciation expense should Larkin recognize on the building for Year 5?
questions.explanation: A long-lived asset is depreciated while held and used, but depreciation ceases once the asset is classified as held for sale. Larkin depreciates the building only for January through March: $48,000 Г— 3/12 = $12,000. Recognizing nothing ignores the three months before classification; $36,000 incorrectly depreciates the nine months after classification instead of the three months before; a full year of $48,000 ignores the held-for-sale classification entirely.

questions.stem: Under ASC 845, a nonmonetary exchange has commercial substance when:

choices.label: A
choices.text: The entity's future cash flows are expected to change significantly as a result of the exchange

choices.label: B
choices.text: The fair values of the assets exchanged are approximately equal

choices.label: C
choices.text: Monetary consideration (boot) of at least 25% of the fair value of the exchange is included

choices.label: D
choices.text: The assets exchanged are of a similar nature and used in the same line of business

questions.explanation: An exchange has commercial substance if the entity's future cash flows are expected to change significantly вҖ” that is, the configuration (risk, timing, or amount) of the cash flows of the asset received differs significantly from that of the asset given up, or the entity-specific value of the affected operations changes significantly. Approximate equality of fair values says nothing about cash-flow configuration. Boot of 25% or more causes the entire transaction to be treated as monetary, which is a separate rule, not the definition of commercial substance. Similarity of the assets actually suggests the exchange may lack commercial substance.

questions.subtopic: Nonmonetary Exchange - Fair Value of Asset Received More Clearly Evident
questions.stem: Pemrose Co. exchanged a custom-built piece of equipment for a machine in a transaction with commercial substance. The fair value of the equipment given up could not be reliably determined, but the machine received has a readily determinable fair value of $62,000. The equipment given up had a carrying amount of $50,000. At what amount should Pemrose record the machine received?
questions.explanation: In an exchange with commercial substance, the asset received is recorded at the fair value of the asset given up unless the fair value of the asset received is more clearly evident.
