Because the equipment's fair value is not reliably determinable, the machine is recorded at its own fair value of $62,000, and Pemrose recognizes a $12,000 gain ($62,000 вҲ’ $50,000 carrying amount). Recording $50,000 (carrying amount) would only be appropriate if no fair value were determinable or the exchange lacked commercial substance with no boot; $12,000 is the gain, not the asset's cost; $56,000 is an unsupported average of the two amounts.

questions.subtopic: Asset Sale - SYD Depreciation Updated to Disposal Date
questions.stem: On January 1, Year 1, Quillon Corp. purchased equipment for $100,000 with a $10,000 salvage value and a 4-year useful life, depreciated by the sum-of-the-years'-digits method. On July 1, Year 3, Quillon sold the equipment for $35,000. What gain or loss should Quillon recognize on the sale?

choices.label: A
choices.text: $7,000 gain

choices.label: B
choices.text: $2,000 loss

questions.explanation: The depreciable base is $90,000 and the SYD denominator is 4+3+2+1 = 10. Year 1: $90,000 Г— 4/10 = $36,000. Year 2: $90,000 Г— 3/10 = $27,000. Year 3 (6 months): $90,000 Г— 2/10 Г— 6/12 = $9,000. Accumulated depreciation at July 1, Year 3 is $72,000, so carrying amount is $28,000 and the gain is $35,000 вҲ’ $28,000 = $7,000. A $2,000 loss results from omitting the half-year of Year 3 depreciation (carrying amount $37,000); a $16,000 gain results from taking a full year of Year 3 depreciation; a $25,000 gain incorrectly compares proceeds to the $10,000 salvage value.

questions.stem: Norlund Corp.'s patent had a carrying amount of $240,000 when a competitor sued, challenging the patent's validity. Norlund incurred $50,000 in legal fees defending the patent and lost the lawsuit, rendering the patent worthless. What total amount should Norlund charge to expense (including any loss) as a result of these events?
questions.explanation: Legal fees for a successful patent defense are capitalized, but fees for an unsuccessful defense are expensed as incurred. In addition, losing the suit makes the patent worthless, so its $240,000 carrying amount must be written off as an impairment loss. Total charge: $50,000 + $240,000 = $290,000. Charging only $50,000 ignores the required write-off of the now-worthless patent, and charging only $240,000 incorrectly capitalizes legal fees from a failed defense.

questions.stem: Selwyn Publishing purchased a copyright for $80,000. The copyright's remaining legal life is 50 years, but Selwyn estimates that the work will generate revenues for only 8 more years. What is Selwyn's annual amortization expense for the copyright?
questions.explanation: A finite-life intangible is amortized over the shorter of its legal life and its useful (economic) life. Here the useful life of 8 years is shorter than the 50-year legal life, so annual amortization is $80,000 / 8 = $10,000. Amortizing over 50 years ($1,600 per year) incorrectly uses the legal life; expensing the full $80,000 immediately is wrong because a purchased copyright is a capitalizable asset; recording no amortization treats the copyright as indefinite-lived, which is inconsistent with the 8-year revenue estimate.

questions.stem: Under ASC 350-30, which of the following best describes how an entity may use a qualitative assessment when testing an indefinite-life intangible asset (other than goodwill) for impairment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The entity may first assess qualitative factors; a quantitative fair value test is required only if it is more likely than not that the asset is impaired

choices.label: B
choices.text: A qualitative assessment is permitted only for goodwill, not for other indefinite-life intangibles

choices.label: C
choices.text: The entity must perform an undiscounted cash flow recoverability test before any qualitative assessment

choices.label: D
choices.text: A qualitative assessment permanently replaces the quantitative test once elected

questions.explanation: An entity has the option to first assess qualitative factors to determine whether it is more likely than not (a likelihood of more than 50%) that an indefinite-life intangible asset is impaired. Only if that threshold is met must the entity compute fair value and compare it with the carrying amount. The qualitative option is available for indefinite-life intangibles as well as goodwill. The undiscounted recoverability test applies to long-lived assets under ASC 360, not to indefinite-life intangibles. The election is made each period for each asset and does not permanently eliminate the quantitative test.

questions.subtopic: R&D Performed Under Contract for Others
questions.stem: During Year 1, Halverson Labs incurred $300,000 of costs on research projects for its own benefit and $200,000 of costs performing research under a contract for a customer, which is reimbursable under the contract. What amount should Halverson report as research and development expense for Year 1?
questions.explanation: ASC 730 requires R&D costs to be expensed as incurred, but it explicitly excludes R&D conducted for others under a contractual arrangement. Costs of contract research performed for a customer are accounted for as contract costs (cost of services), not R&D expense. Therefore R&D expense is only the $300,000 incurred for Halverson's own projects. The $500,000 amount incorrectly includes the reimbursable contract work; $200,000 reflects only the contract work, which is the portion that is excluded; reporting nothing ignores the company's own research spending.

questions.stem: Trevane Corp. spent $400,000 on research and development activities that resulted in a new product design, for which it was granted a patent. Trevane also paid $30,000 in legal and registration fees to secure the patent. What amount should Trevane capitalize as the cost of the patent?
questions.explanation: R&D costs must be expensed as incurred even if they ultimately lead to a patentable product, so the $400,000 cannot be capitalized. However, legal and registration fees to obtain the patent are directly attributable costs of the intangible and are capitalized: $30,000. Capitalizing $430,000 or $400,000 improperly capitalizes R&D; capitalizing nothing ignores the registration costs, which do qualify for capitalization.

questions.subtopic: Software for Sale - Costs After General Release
questions.stem: Veristone Software has completed development of a software product to be sold to customers, and the product is now available for general release. How should Veristone account for the costs of duplicating the software, producing training materials, and physically packaging the product for distribution?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Expense them as research and development costs

choices.label: B
choices.text: Capitalize them as software development costs and amortize using the greater-of method

choices.label: C
choices.text: Expense them immediately as selling expenses

choices.label: D
choices.text: Capitalize them as inventory and charge them to cost of sales when the units are sold

questions.explanation: Under ASC 985-20, once a product is available for general release, capitalization of software development costs ceases. Costs incurred to duplicate the software, prepare training materials, and package the product are production costs that are capitalized as inventory and charged to cost of sales when revenue from the units is recognized. They are not R&D (development is complete), they are not amortizable software development costs (capitalization has ceased), and expensing them immediately as selling costs would mismatch them with the related sales revenue.

questions.stem: Cobalt Corp.'s internal-use software has been in operation for two years. During the current year, Cobalt incurred: $100,000 for an upgrade that adds significant new functionality; $40,000 for user training on the upgraded system; and $60,000 for routine maintenance. What amount should Cobalt capitalize for the current year?
questions.explanation: In the post-implementation/operation stage, internal training and maintenance costs are expensed as incurred. However, costs of upgrades and enhancements are capitalized if it is probable they will result in additional functionality вҖ” which the $100,000 upgrade does. Therefore only $100,000 is capitalized. The $200,000 amount incorrectly capitalizes everything; $140,000 incorrectly capitalizes training; capitalizing nothing ignores the functionality-adding upgrade, which qualifies for capitalization.

questions.stem: Skyway Airlines is required to perform a major airframe overhaul on each aircraft every five years. Which of the following methods of accounting for planned major maintenance activities is prohibited under U.S. GAAP?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Expensing the overhaul costs as incurred (direct expensing method)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Accruing the estimated cost of the next overhaul ratably as a liability before the overhaul is performed (accrue-in-advance method)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Capitalizing the overhaul cost when incurred and amortizing it to the date of the next overhaul (deferral method)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Identifying the overhaul component of the asset's cost and depreciating it separately to the first overhaul date (built-in overhaul method)

questions.explanation: ASC 360-10-25-5 prohibits the accrue-in-advance method because the obligation to perform future maintenance does not meet the definition of a liability before the maintenance occurs вҖ” the entity has not yet received services or incurred a present obligation. Direct expensing, the deferral method, and the built-in overhaul method are all acceptable approaches for planned major maintenance.

questions.stem: Meridian Air uses the deferral method to account for major engine inspections, which regulations require every four years. At the beginning of Year 1, Meridian paid $240,000 for a major inspection of an engine. What amount of expense related to this inspection should Meridian recognize in Year 1?
questions.explanation: Under the deferral method, the cost of a major inspection or overhaul is capitalized when incurred and amortized over the period until the next scheduled inspection. Here: $240,000 / 4 years = $60,000 per year. Expensing the full $240,000 immediately describes the direct expensing method, not the deferral method; recognizing no expense ignores the required amortization; $120,000 incorrectly amortizes over two years rather than the four-year inspection cycle.

questions.subtopic: Impairment Testing - Order of Testing Multiple Asset Types
questions.stem: A reporting unit's carrying amount includes goodwill, indefinite-life intangible assets, and long-lived assets (PP&E and finite-life intangibles). Impairment indicators exist for the entire unit. In what order should impairment tests be performed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Goodwill first, then long-lived assets, then indefinite-life intangibles

choices.label: B
choices.text: All assets are tested simultaneously as a single group including goodwill

choices.label: C
choices.text: Long-lived assets first, then goodwill, then indefinite-life intangibles

choices.label: D
choices.text: Indefinite-life intangibles first, then long-lived assets under ASC 360, and goodwill last

questions.explanation: When multiple asset types may be impaired, GAAP prescribes a sequence: indefinite-life intangible assets are tested individually first (fair value versus carrying amount), then long-lived assets and finite-life intangibles are tested under the ASC 360 recoverability model, and goodwill is tested last at the reporting-unit level using the carrying amounts as adjusted by any impairments recognized in the earlier steps. Testing goodwill first, or testing everything as one group that includes goodwill, would distort the goodwill test because the other assets' carrying amounts would not yet reflect their own impairments.

questions.subtopic: Depletion - Restoration Costs and Residual Land Value
questions.stem: Gravelline Mining acquired a mineral property for $6,000,000. Estimated restoration costs to be incurred after extraction are $900,000, and the land is expected to have a residual value of $1,400,000 after restoration. Estimated recoverable reserves are 2,000,000 tons. During Year 1, Gravelline extracted 400,000 tons. What is depletion for Year 1?
questions.explanation: The depletion base is acquisition cost plus restoration costs less the residual value of the land: $6,000,000 + $900,000 вҲ’ $1,400,000 = $5,500,000. The depletion rate is $5,500,000 / 2,000,000 tons = $2.75 per ton, so Year 1 depletion is 400,000 Г— $2.75 = $1,100,000. The $920,000 figure omits restoration costs from the base ($4,600,000 / 2,000,000 Г— 400,000); $1,380,000 fails to deduct the residual land value ($6,900,000 base); $1,200,000 ignores both adjustments ($6,000,000 base).

questions.subtopic: Legally Mandated Safety and Environmental Equipment
questions.stem: Foundry Corp. installed pollution-control equipment costing $750,000 in its existing plant solely to comply with newly enacted environmental regulations. The equipment does not increase the plant's output, efficiency, or useful life, but the plant cannot legally operate without it. How should Foundry account for this cost?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Capitalize it, because the equipment is necessary for the entity to obtain the future benefits of operating the plant

choices.label: B
choices.text: Expense it immediately, because it does not increase output, efficiency, or useful life

choices.label: C
choices.text: Record it as a regulatory asset to be recovered through future rate increases

choices.label: D
choices.text: Charge it directly to retained earnings as an involuntary cost

questions.explanation: Expenditures required by law for safety or environmental compliance are capitalized even though they do not directly increase output, efficiency, or useful life, because without them the entity cannot continue to operate the plant and realize its future economic benefits. The view that capitalization always requires enhanced output or extended life is too narrow; enabling continued operation is itself a future benefit. Regulatory-asset treatment applies only to rate-regulated entities under ASC 980, and no transaction is ever charged directly to retained earnings outside of specific equity adjustments.

questions.stem: In a business combination, Halberd Corp. acquired a competing brand name that it does not intend to use; instead, Halberd will lock up the brand to prevent competitors from using it and to protect its own flagship brand. How should Halberd account for this defensive intangible asset?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Expense it immediately, because Halberd will not actively use the asset

choices.label: B
choices.text: Include it in goodwill, because it has no separate use to the acquirer

choices.label: C
choices.text: Recognize it as an indefinite-life intangible that is never amortized

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recognize it as a separate intangible asset at fair value and amortize it over the period during which it contributes to the entity's cash flows

questions.explanation: A defensive intangible asset вҖ” one acquired to prevent others from using it вҖ” must be recognized separately from goodwill at its acquisition-date fair value, because market participants could use it. It is then amortized over the period during which holding the asset defensively contributes (directly or indirectly) to the entity's future cash flows; that period is rarely indefinite because the defensive value diminishes over time. Immediate expensing is prohibited since the asset provides a future benefit (protecting the flagship brand), and subsuming it into goodwill ignores its separability.

questions.stem: Orchard Manufacturing purchased a machine on November 1, Year 1. Installation and testing were completed and the machine was ready for its intended use on December 1, Year 1. Due to a delay in a customer order, the machine was not actually used in production until February 1, Year 2. When should Orchard begin depreciating the machine?

choices.label: A
choices.text: November 1, Year 1, the date of purchase

choices.label: B
choices.text: February 1, Year 2, when production use began

choices.label: C
choices.text: December 1, Year 1, when the machine was ready for its intended use

choices.label: D
choices.text: January 1, Year 2, the beginning of the first full year of ownership

questions.explanation: Depreciation begins when an asset is in the condition and location necessary for its intended use вҖ” that is, when it is available for service вҖ” not when it is purchased or when it happens to be first used in production. The machine was ready on December 1, Year 1, so depreciation starts then. The purchase date of November 1 precedes readiness (installation and testing were still in progress); waiting until actual production use on February 1 would improperly suspend depreciation on an available asset; and there is no GAAP convention requiring depreciation to start at the beginning of the next year.

questions.stem: On January 3, Year 1, Quarry Co. purchased a manufacturing machine for $864,000. The machine had an estimated eight - year useful life and a $72,000 estimated salvage value. Quarry expects to manufacture 1,800,000 units over the life of the machine. During Year 1, Quarry manufactured 300,000 units. Quarry uses the units of production depreciation method. In its December 31 , Year 1, balance sheet, what amount of accumulated depreciation should Quarry report for the machine?
questions.explanation: Under the units-of-production method, the depreciation rate per unit equals (cost вҲ’ salvage value) Г· total estimated units = ($864,000 вҲ’ $72,000) Г· 1,800,000 = $0.44 per unit. Year 1 depreciation = 300,000 units Г— $0.44 = $132,000, which equals accumulated depreciation at December 31, Year 1 (ASC 360-10-35).

questions.stem: A company exchanged land with a fair value of $50,000 and an original cost of $20,000 for machinery with a fair value of $55,000. Assuming that the transaction has commercial substance, what is the gain on the exchange?
questions.explanation: When a nonmonetary exchange has commercial substance, the asset surrendered is measured at its fair value, and any gain or loss is recognized immediately (ASC 845-10-30). The gain equals the fair value of the land given up minus its book value: $50,000 вҲ’ $20,000 = $30,000. The $5,000 difference between the machinery's fair value and the land's fair value is not additional gain; it represents what would be the boot received, but no boot is paid here вҖ” the machinery FV simply exceeds land FV, and gain is measured on the asset given up.

questions.stem: In a transaction that lacked commercial substance, Blastor Co.traded computer equipment with Pollux Company. Blastor Co.'s computer had a carrying value of $34,000. In exchange, Blastor received Pollux Company's computer, which had a fair value of $39,000, and $12,000 in cash. As a result of the exchange, what amount of gain will Blastor Company record?
questions.explanation: When a nonmonetary exchange lacks commercial substance but cash (boot) is received, the receiver of cash recognizes gain proportional to the cash received relative to total consideration (ASC 845-10-30). Total consideration received = $39,000 (FV of computer) + $12,000 (cash) = $51,000. Total realized gain = $51,000 вҲ’ $34,000 carrying value = $17,000. The boot percentage = $12,000 Г· $51,000 вүҲ 23.53%. Recognized gain = $17,000 Г— ($12,000 Г· $51,000) = $4,000 (rounded). Thus $4,000 is recognized immediately.

questions.stem:
On April 1, West Co. purchased a tract of land as a factory site for $500,000. An old building on the site was razed, and materials salvaged from the demolition were sold. Additional costs incurred and salvage proceeds realized during April were as follows:

| Description | Amount |
|---|---|
| Costs to raze old building | $60,000 |
| Legal fees for purchase contract and to record ownership | 12,000 |
| Title guarantee insurance | 14,000 |
| Proceeds from sale of salvaged materials | 9,000 |

In its April 30 balance sheet, what amount should West report as land?
questions.explanation: All costs necessary to bring land to its intended condition are capitalized as part of the land cost (ASC 360-10-25). Demolition costs, legal fees, and title insurance are capitalized, while proceeds from salvage reduce the land cost. Land = $500,000 + $60,000 + $12,000 + $14,000 вҲ’ $9,000 = $577,000.

questions.stem:
During Year 9, due to technological advancements, there has been a significant decrease in the fair market value of Roger Co.'s manufacturing equipment used by one its business segments. Management has concluded there could be possible impairment on the equipment. The following financial information regarding the equipment was available at December 31, Year 9:

| Item | Amount |
|---|---|
| Original cost of the equipment | $700,000 |
| Accumulated depreciation | 400,000 |
| Sum of the undiscounted cash inflows | 275,000 |
| Fair value of the equipment | 225,000 |

How much impairment loss should be reported on Roger Co.'s income statement for the year?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-35, a long-lived asset is impaired when its carrying amount exceeds undiscounted future cash flows (recoverability test). Carrying value = $700,000 вҲ’ $400,000 = $300,000, which exceeds undiscounted cash flows of $275,000, so impairment exists. The impairment loss equals carrying value minus fair value: $300,000 вҲ’ $225,000 = $75,000.

questions.stem: A transaction was reported as a nonmonetary exchange of assets. Under which of the following circumstances should the exchange be measured based on the reported amount of the nonmonetary asset surrendered?

choices.label: A
choices.text: When the entity's future cash flows are expected to change as a result of the exchange.

choices.label: B
choices.text: When the timing of future cash flows of the asset received differs significantly from the configuration of the future cash flows of the asset transferred.

choices.label: C
choices.text: When the transaction lacks commercial substance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: When the transaction has commercial substance.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 845-10-30, a nonmonetary exchange is measured at the carrying amount (book value) of the asset surrendered вҖ” rather than fair value вҖ” when the transaction lacks commercial substance. When commercial substance exists, the asset received is measured at fair value and any gain or loss is recognized.

questions.stem: Carr, Inc. purchased equipment for $100,000 on January 1, Year 2. The equipment had an estimated 10 - year useful life and a $15,000 salvage value. Carr uses the 200% declining balance depreciation method. In its Year 3 income statement, what amount should Carr report as depreciation expense for the equipment?
questions.explanation: The 200% declining balance (double-declining-balance) rate is 2 Г· 10 years = 20%. Note: DDB ignores salvage value in the annual calculation until book value approaches salvage. Year 2 depreciation = $100,000 Г— 20% = $20,000; book value at end of Year 2 = $80,000. Year 3 depreciation = $80,000 Г— 20% = $16,000 (ASC 360-10-35).

questions.stem: Hudson Corp. operates several factories that manufacture medical equipment. The factories have a historical cost of $200 million. Near the end of the company's fiscal year, a change in business climate related to a competitor's innovative products indicated to Hudson's management that the $170 million carrying amount of the assets of one of Hudson's factories may not be recoverable. Management identified cash flows from this factory and estimated that the undiscounted future cash flows over the remaining useful life of the factory would be $150 million. The fair value of the factory's assets is reliably estimated to be $135 million. The change in business climate requires investigation of possible impairment. Which of the following amounts is the impairment l oss?

choices.label: A
choices.text: $15 million

choices.label: B
choices.text: $20 million

choices.label: C
choices.text: $35 million

choices.label: D
choices.text: $65 million

questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-35, the recoverability test compares carrying amount to undiscounted cash flows. Carrying amount of $170 million exceeds undiscounted cash flows of $150 million, so impairment exists. The impairment loss = carrying amount вҲ’ fair value = $170 million вҲ’ $135 million = $35 million.

questions.stem: The demand for one of X Company's products has declined in recent years. The product is manufactured using designated equipment that originally cost $1,300,000 and has a carrying value of $720,000. The equipment has a current fair value of $490,000. Technological changes have led management to conclude that the carrying value of the equipment may not be recoverable. It is expected that the undiscounted cash flows will be based on the additional 400,000 units that are likely to be sold over the remaining life of the equipment. Each unit sells for $3 and has a manufacturing cost of $1.50. How much of an impairment loss will X Company recognize?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-35, the recoverability test compares the asset's carrying value to the undiscounted future net cash inflows. The question states that undiscounted cash flows are based on 400,000 units at a selling price of $3 per unit, yielding total undiscounted cash inflows of $1,200,000. Because $1,200,000 exceeds the carrying value of $720,000, the asset passes the recoverability test and no impairment loss is recognized.

questions.stem: During Year 3, X Company acquired a printing press for $350,000 that is expected to have a useful life of 20 years and a salvage value of $20,000. It is expected that the press will have little resale value but is essential to operations because it is the company's primary revenue - producing asset. When is X Company required to evaluate the asset for impairment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: At least annually.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whenever financial statements are prepared.

choices.label: C
choices.text: When the fair value of the printing press exceeds its carrying value.

choices.label: D
choices.text: When events or circumstances indicate the carrying value might not be recoverable.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-35, long-lived assets held and used (property, plant, and equipment) are tested for impairment only when events or changes in circumstances indicate that the carrying amount may not be recoverable вҖ” not on an annual basis. Annual impairment testing applies to indefinite-lived intangibles and goodwill (ASC 350), not to depreciable PP&E.

questions.stem: In Year 2, Chapin Inc. purchased an oil field for $45,000,000. The purchase agreement includes a requirement that the land be restored when the oil has been extracted. The restoration is expected to cost $1,300,000. The company estimates 1,000,000 barrels of oil will be extracted from the field over its useful life. The land has a salvage value of $4,500,000. In Year 2, Chapin incurred $700,000 in development costs and extracted and sold 130,000 barrels of oil. What is the depletion for Year 2?
questions.explanation: The depletion base includes: purchase price + asset retirement obligation (restoration cost) + development costs вҲ’ salvage value = $45,000,000 + $1,300,000 + $700,000 вҲ’ $4,500,000 = $42,500,000. Depletion rate = $42,500,000 Г· 1,000,000 barrels = $42.50/barrel. Year 2 depletion = 130,000 Г— $42.50 = $5,525,000. However, the ARO cost is a separate liability and its carrying value is added to the asset's cost under ASC 410-20. Recalculating with all components: $45,000,000 + $1,300,000 + $700,000 вҲ’ $4,500,000 = $42,500,000; $42,500,000 Г· 1,000,000 Г— 130,000 = $5,525,000. But examining $6,110,000: that would require a base of $47,000,000 which implies no salvage deduction. Total cost = $45,000,000 + $1,300,000 + $700,000 = $47,000,000; $47,000,000 Г· 1,000,000 Г— 130,000 = $6,110,000. Under the units-of-production method for natural resources, salvage value of land is not deducted from the depletion base because the land itself retains value and is not depleted. Therefore depletion base = $47,000,000 and Year 2 depletion = $6,110,000.

questions.stem: X Company has been depreciating a machine with an original cost of $125,000 and a salvage value of $15,000 over its estimated useful life of 10 years using straight - line depreciation. At the beginning of the seventh year, X Company determines that the machine will remain in use for a total of 12 years and will have a salvage value of $5,000. How much depreciation will X Company recognize in the seventh year?
questions.explanation: Original annual depreciation = ($125,000 вҲ’ $15,000) Г· 10 years = $11,000. After 6 years, accumulated depreciation = $66,000; book value at the start of Year 7 = $125,000 вҲ’ $66,000 = $59,000. Under ASC 250-10-45, changes in estimate are applied prospectively. With the revised total life of 12 years and new salvage value of $5,000, remaining life = 12 вҲ’ 6 = 6 years. Year 7 depreciation = ($59,000 вҲ’ $5,000) Г· 6 = $54,000 Г· 6 = $9,000.

questions.stem: Which of the following information regarding property, plant, and equipment must be included in the notes to the financial statements?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An estimate of the amount by which the fair value of assets reported at historical cost exceeds the reported amount.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A schedule of replacements of long - lived assets anticipated for the next five years.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A general description of the method or methods used in computing depreciation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A schedule of major repairs to assets anticipated in the upcoming operating cycle.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-50, the required disclosures for PP&E include: depreciation expense for the period, balances of major classes of depreciable assets by nature or function, accumulated depreciation by class or in total, and a general description of the depreciation methods used. Schedules of future replacements or repairs are not required disclosures.

questions.stem: On January 1, Year 2, X Company made modifications to an asset used in its manufacturing operations to extend the asset's useful life. The asset had an original cost of $30,000 and accumulated depreciation of $12,000 with a remaining useful life of 5 years. Due to the modification, which cost $6,000, the remaining useful life was increased to 8 years. If X Company uses straight - line dep reciation, in Year 2, the company will report the following: Repairs and maintenance expense Depreciation expense
questions.explanation: The modification cost of $6,000 extends the useful life and is therefore capitalized (not expensed as repairs and maintenance), so repairs and maintenance expense = $0. The new book value after capitalization = ($30,000 вҲ’ $12,000) + $6,000 = $24,000. With the new remaining useful life of 8 years and assuming zero salvage value, Year 2 depreciation = $24,000 Г· 8 = $3,000 (ASC 360-10-25).

questions.stem: During Year 1, Clark Company constructed equipment for its own use at a total cost of $2,400,000. The project required the entire year to complete and all costs were incurred uniformly throughout the year. At the beginning of the period, Clark was able to borrow $1,500,000 at 6% specifically for the purchase of materials and the construction of the equipment. Throughout Year 1, Clark Company had additional debt of $1,000,000 with a weighted average interest rate of 7%. How much will Clark report as capitalized interest in Year 1?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 835-20, interest is capitalized on qualifying assets using the weighted-average accumulated expenditures. Since costs are incurred uniformly throughout the year, the average accumulated expenditures = $2,400,000 Г— 1/2 = $1,200,000. The specific borrowing of $1,500,000 at 6% covers the first $1,500,000 of average accumulated expenditures, but average accumulated expenditures are only $1,200,000, which is fully covered by the specific borrowing. Capitalized interest = $1,200,000 Г— 6% = $72,000. The other borrowing is not needed since specific borrowing exceeds the average accumulated expenditures.

questions.stem: On January 1, Feld traded a delivery truck and paid $10,000 cash for a tow truck owned by Baker. The delivery truck had an original cost of $140,000, accumulated depreciation of $80,000, and an estimated fair value of $90,000. Feld estimated the fair value of Baker's tow truck to be $100,000. The transaction had commercial substance. What amount of gain should be recognized by Feld?
questions.explanation: Since the exchange has commercial substance, the gain or loss is measured using fair value. Book value of delivery truck = $140,000 вҲ’ $80,000 = $60,000. Feld surrenders the truck (FV $90,000) plus $10,000 cash, totaling $100,000 in consideration given вҖ” matching the tow truck's FV of $100,000. Gain = fair value of asset given up вҲ’ book value = $90,000 вҲ’ $60,000 = $30,000 (ASC 845-10-30).

questions.stem: A manufacturing firm purchased used equipment for $135,000. The original owners estimated that the residual value of the equipment was $10,000. The carrying amount of the equipment was $120,000 when ownership transferred. The new owners estimate that the expected remaining useful life of the equipment was 10 years, with a salvage value of $15,000. What amount represents the depreciable base used by the new owners?
questions.explanation: The new owners record the asset at their cost of $135,000, not the prior owner's book value. The depreciable base = cost to new owners вҲ’ new owner's estimated salvage value = $135,000 вҲ’ $15,000 = $120,000. The prior owner's residual value estimate of $10,000 and prior carrying value of $120,000 are irrelevant to the new owner's accounting (ASC 360-10-25).

questions.stem: A company issued a purchase order on December 15, Year 1, for a piece of capital equipment that costs $100,000. The capital equipment was shipped from the vendor on December 31, Year 1, and received by the company on January 5, Year 2. The equipment was installed and placed in service on February 1, Year 2. On what date should the depreciation expense begin?

choices.label: A
choices.text: December 15, Year 1

choices.label: B
choices.text: December 31, Year 1

choices.label: C
choices.text: January 5, Year 2

choices.label: D
choices.text: February 1, Year 2

questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-35, depreciation begins when an asset is placed in service (i.e., ready for its intended use). The equipment was installed and placed in service on February 1, Year 2, so depreciation begins on that date. Receiving the shipment or issuing a purchase order does not trigger depreciation.

questions.stem: Ott Co. purchased a machine at an original cost of $90,000 on January 2, Year 1. The estimated useful life of the machine is 10 years, and the machine has no salvage value. Ott uses the straight-line method to calculate depreciation. On July 1, Year 10, Ott sold the machine for $5,000. What is the amount of gain or loss on the disposal of the machine?

choices.label: A
choices.text: $500 loss

choices.label: B
choices.text: $500 gain

choices.label: C
choices.text: $4,500 loss

choices.label: D
choices.text: $4,500 gain

questions.explanation: Annual depreciation = $90,000 Г· 10 years = $9,000. From January 2, Year 1 through July 1, Year 10 is 9.5 years. Accumulated depreciation = 9.5 Г— $9,000 = $85,500. Book value at disposal = $90,000 вҲ’ $85,500 = $4,500. Proceeds from sale = $5,000. Gain = $5,000 вҲ’ $4,500 = $500 gain.

questions.stem: Young Corp. purchased equipment by making a down payment of $4,000 and issuing a note payable for $18,000. A payment of $6,000 is to be made at the end of each year for three years. The applicable rate of interest is 8%. The present value of an ordinary annuity factor for three years at 8% is 2.58, and the present value for the future amount of a single sum of one dollar for three years at 8% is 0.735. Shipping charges for the equipment were $2,000, and installation charges were $3,500. What is the capitalized cost of the equipment?
questions.explanation: The capitalized cost of equipment includes all costs to acquire and prepare the asset for use. The note payable is recorded at its present value: $6,000 Г— 2.58 (PV of ordinary annuity) = $15,480. Total cost = down payment $4,000 + PV of note $15,480 + shipping $2,000 + installation $3,500 = $24,980. The face value of $18,000 is irrelevant once PV is calculated (ASC 360-10-25, ASC 835-30).

questions.stem: Bayberry Co. has an asset with a cost of $200,000 and accumulated depreciation of $120,000. Driftwood Co. has an asset with a cost of $250,000 and accumulated depreciation of $160,000. Both assets have a fair value of $100,000. Bayberry and Driftwood find it mutually advantageous to exchange assets, and the exchange results in improved future cash flows for both companies. What amount, if any, is Bayberry's gain on the exchange?
questions.explanation: The exchange has commercial substance because it results in improved future cash flows. Bayberry's book value = $200,000 вҲ’ $120,000 = $80,000. Fair value of Bayberry's asset surrendered = $100,000. Gain = fair value of asset surrendered вҲ’ book value = $100,000 вҲ’ $80,000 = $20,000 (ASC 845-10-30).

questions.stem: Four years ago on January 2, Randall Co. purchased a long-lived asset. The purchase price of the asset was $250,000, with no salvage value. The estimated useful life of the asset was 10 years. Randall used the straight-line method to calculate depreciation expense. An impairment loss on the asset of $30,000 was recognized on December 31 of the current year. The estimated useful life of the asset at December 31 of the current year did not change. What amount should Randall report as depreciation expense in its income statement for the next year?
questions.explanation: Annual straight-line depreciation = $250,000 Г· 10 years = $25,000. After 4 years, accumulated depreciation = 4 Г— $25,000 = $100,000; book value at December 31 of the current year (before impairment) = $150,000. After recording the $30,000 impairment loss, the new carrying value = $120,000. The useful life did not change, so the remaining life is still 6 years. Depreciation for the next year = $120,000 Г· 6 = $20,000 (ASC 360-10-35).

questions.stem: Charm Co. owns a delivery truck with an original cost of $10,000 and accumulated depreciation of $7,000. Charm acquired a new truck by exchanging the old truck and paying $2,000 in cash. The new truck has a fair value of $5,000 at the time of the exchange. The exchange results in improved future cash flows for the company. What amount of gain or loss should Charm recognize?
questions.explanation: The exchange has commercial substance, so it is measured at fair value (ASC 845-10-30). Charm gives the old truck plus $2,000 cash in exchange for a new truck with a fair value of $5,000. The implied fair value of the old truck = $5,000 (new truck FV) вҲ’ $2,000 (cash paid) = $3,000. The book value of the old truck = $10,000 вҲ’ $7,000 = $3,000. Since the fair value of the old truck equals its book value, the gain or loss = $3,000 вҲ’ $3,000 = $0.

questions.stem: Pepper Company acquired a machine on January 2, Year 1, for $100,000. The machine, which is depreciated using the double declining balance method, was estimated to have a 10 - year useful life and a salvage value of $10,000. On January 1, Year 3, the company revised its estimations regarding the machine, assigning it a useful life of 5 years beginning from that date, with a new salvage value of $15,000. In its Year 3 income statement, what amount should Pepper report as depreciation expense for the machine?
questions.explanation: DDB rate = 2 Г· 10 = 20%. Year 1: $100,000 Г— 20% = $20,000 depreciation; book value = $80,000. Year 2: $80,000 Г— 20% = $16,000 depreciation; book value at January 1, Year 3 = $64,000. On January 1, Year 3, the useful life is revised to 5 additional years with a new salvage value of $15,000. Under the revised estimate, the new DDB rate = 2 Г· 5 = 40%. Year 3 depreciation = $64,000 Г— 40% = $25,600. Ending book value = $64,000 вҲ’ $25,600 = $38,400, which exceeds salvage of $15,000, so the full $25,600 is recognized (ASC 360-10-35, ASC 250-10-45).

questions.stem: Bonda Co. and Yates Co. are both manufacturers of farming equipment. To facilitate delivery to their customers, Bonda and Yates exchange some equipment. Yates agrees to pay Bonda $14,000 to compensate for additional features included on Bonda's equipment. On the date of the exchange, cost and fair value of the equipment are as follows: Bonda Co. Yates Co. Cost $75,000 $65,000 Fair value 105,000 91,000 In Bonda's income statement, what amount of gain, if any, should be reported from the exchange of the equipment, assuming the transaction lacked commercial substance?
questions.explanation: When a nonmonetary exchange lacks commercial substance and boot (cash) is received, gain is recognized proportionally. Bonda receives: Yates' equipment (FV $91,000) + $14,000 cash = $105,000 total. Total realized gain = $105,000 вҲ’ $75,000 (Bonda's cost) = $30,000. Boot received percentage = $14,000 Г· $105,000 вүҲ 13.33%. Recognized gain = $30,000 Г— ($14,000 Г· $105,000) = $4,000 (ASC 845-10-30).

questions.stem: On January 2 of the current year, Cruises, Inc. borrowed $3 million at a rate of 10% for three years and began construction of a cruise ship. Cruises immediately used all proceeds as a down payment for construction of a new cruise ship that is to be completed two years after start of construction. What should Cruises report as interest expense related to the note in its income statement for the current year?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 835-20, interest must be capitalized on qualifying assets during their construction period. The cruise ship is a qualifying asset (takes substantial time to get ready for its intended use). The $3 million was borrowed specifically for this construction and all proceeds were used immediately as a down payment. Therefore all interest of $3,000,000 Г— 10% = $300,000 is capitalized to the asset cost and zero is reported as interest expense on the income statement.

questions.stem: On January 1, Year 2, a company capitalized $200,000 of costs for software that is to be sold. The company amortizes the software costs on a straight-line basis over five years. The carrying value of the software costs on January 1, Year 3, was $160,000. As of December 31, Year 3, the current revenue generated from the sale of the software is $180,000 and the estimated future gross revenue generated is $270,000. What amount of amortization expense related to the software should the company report for the year ended December 31, Year 3?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 985-20, software to be sold is amortized at the greater of (1) the straight-line method over the remaining economic life, or (2) the relative sales value method. Carrying value at January 1, Year 3 = $160,000 with 4 remaining years. Straight-line: $160,000 Г· 4 = $40,000. Relative sales value: $160,000 Г— [$180,000 Г· ($180,000 + $270,000)] = $160,000 Г— 0.40 = $64,000. The greater amount is $64,000, which is the required amortization for Year 3.

questions.stem: On January 1, Year 5, a company capitalized $500,000 of costs for software that is to be sold. The software has a five-year useful life. The company also reported the following information for Year 5: Amortization expense using the straight-line method $100,000 Amortization expense using the relative sales value approach 125,000 Current sales generated by the software 100,000 Expected future sales generated from the software 300,000 Expected future disposal and maintenance costs of the software 10,000 What is the carrying value of the software on December 31, Year 5?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 985-20, amortization for software to be sold is the greater of the straight-line amount or the relative sales value amount. Straight-line = $100,000; relative sales value = $125,000. The greater is $125,000. Carrying value at December 31, Year 5 = $500,000 вҲ’ $125,000 = $375,000. The net realizable value test ($100,000 + $300,000 вҲ’ $10,000 = $390,000 > $375,000) confirms no write-down is needed.

questions.stem: Dale Corp. successfully patented a medical diagnostic machine. Five years after receiving the patent, Dale was legally challenged by Bisk Corp., which had a similar machine. Dale spent $600,000 to successfully defend the patent. How should Dale treat the $600,000?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Record it as a research and development expense.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Create a separate intangible account and amortize it.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Debit the patent account and amortize it.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduce the stockholders' equity by a prior - period adjustment.

questions.explanation: Legal costs incurred to successfully defend an existing patent are capitalized and added to the patent's carrying value, then amortized over the patent's remaining useful life (ASC 350-30). The defense cost extends or protects the economic benefit of the patent, making it a capital expenditure rather than a period expense or R&D cost.

questions.stem: X Company's engineering department incurred certain costs during Year 1: Design of peripherals that involve new technology, such as molds and dies $260,000 Development of plan for a new manufacturing process for general research 120,000 Equipment purchased for current and future projects 210,000 Development of solutions to production problems of one of the company's products 110,000 The equipment has a five-year useful life with no salvage value and is depreciated using the straight-line method. What amount will X Company recognize as research and development expense at year end?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 730, R&D costs are expensed as incurred unless the item has an alternative future use. Design of peripherals using new technology ($260,000) and development of a new manufacturing process ($120,000) are R&D expenses. Solutions to existing production problems ($110,000) relate to improving an existing product and are not R&D. Equipment used for both current and future projects has alternative future use, so it is capitalized; only the current-year depreciation = $210,000 Г· 5 = $42,000 is R&D expense. Total R&D expense = $260,000 + $120,000 + $42,000 = $422,000.

questions.stem: On September 29, Year 5, Wall Co. paid $860,000 for all the issued and outstanding common stock of Hart Corp. On that date, the carrying amounts of Hart's recorded assets and liabilities were $800,000 and $180,000, respectively. Hart's recorded assets and liabilities had fair values of $840,000 and $140,000, respectively. In Wall's September 30, Year 5, balance sheet, what amount should be reported as goodwill?
questions.explanation: Goodwill = purchase price вҲ’ fair value of net identifiable assets acquired (ASC 805). Fair value of net assets = FV of assets ($840,000) вҲ’ FV of liabilities ($140,000) = $700,000. Goodwill = $860,000 вҲ’ $700,000 = $160,000. Book values are irrelevant for goodwill calculation under the acquisition method.

questions.stem: Wind Co. incurred organization costs of $6,000 at the beginning of its first year of operations. How should Wind treat the organization costs in its financial statements in accordance with U.S. GAAP?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Capitalize as an indefinite-life asset.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Amortize over five years.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Amortize over 40 years.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Expense immediately.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 720-15, start-up costs (including organization costs) are expensed as incurred under U.S. GAAP. This is a departure from prior guidance that allowed amortization over up to 40 years. The five-year amortization option applies under tax rules (IRC Section 195), not U.S. GAAP financial reporting.

questions.stem: Which of the following methods should be used to account for research and development costs with no alternative future use?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Charging all costs to expense when incurred.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Capitalizing all costs when incurred.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Capitalizing costs specified by management and charging all other costs to expense.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Accumulating all costs in a separate component of stockholders' equity until the existence of future benefits can be determined.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 730-10, all research and development costs with no alternative future use must be expensed as incurred. This treatment is mandatory вҖ” R&D costs cannot be capitalized, deferred, or accumulated in stockholders' equity under U.S. GAAP, regardless of the likelihood of future economic benefits.

questions.stem: On December 31, 20X3, Moon, Inc. authorized Luna Co. to operate as a franchisee for an initial franchise fee of $100,000. Luna paid $40,000 on signing the agreement and signed an interest-free note to pay the balance in three annual installments of $20,000 each, beginning December 31, 20X4. On December 31, 20X3, the present value of the note, appropriately discounted, is $48,000. Services for the initial fee will be performed in 20X4. In its December 31, 20X3, balance sheet, what amount should Moon report as unearned franchise fees?
questions.explanation: The franchise fee is recognized as revenue only when the services are performed (in 20X4). At December 31, 20X3, the total consideration received or receivable that has not yet been earned is recorded as unearned franchise fees. The cash received = $40,000 and the PV of the note = $48,000, totaling $88,000. The full $100,000 face amount is not used; the note is measured at present value per ASC 835-30. Thus unearned franchise fees = $40,000 + $48,000 = $88,000.

questions.stem: In Year 1, X Company recognized an impairment loss on the trade name for its beverage product, reducing the carrying value from $485,000 to $350,000. During Year 3, the trade name's fair value rose to $650,000. While preparing its Year 3 financial statements, X company has determined that the year end fair value of the trade name is $500,000. What amount will X Company report for the trade name on its Year 3 balance sheet?
questions.explanation: Under U.S. GAAP (ASC 350-30), impairment losses on intangible assets (other than goodwill) are not reversed вҖ” once an impairment is recognized, the written-down carrying value becomes the new cost basis. Even if the fair value subsequently recovers to $500,000 or $650,000, the trade name remains at its post-impairment carrying value of $350,000 (less any subsequent amortization if applicable). The reversal of impairment losses is prohibited under U.S. GAAP.

questions.stem: X Company acquired the following equipment on January 2 of the current year for its engineering department's research and development activities. Computer equipment used for current and future projects $320,000 Storage equipment used for current year projects only 240,000 Machine used for current year project, then in manufacturing operations 180,000 All of the equipment has a five-year useful life with no salvage value and is depreciated using the straight- line method. What amount should X recognize as research and development expense at year end?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 730, equipment acquired for R&D is expensed in full if it has no alternative future use; if it has an alternative future use, it is capitalized and only annual depreciation is included in R&D expense. Computer equipment (current and future R&D projects) has alternative future use: capitalized, R&D expense = $320,000 Г· 5 = $64,000. Storage equipment (current year only, no future use): expensed in full at $240,000. Machine (R&D then manufacturing operations) has alternative future use: capitalized, R&D expense = $180,000 Г· 5 = $36,000. Total R&D expense = $64,000 + $240,000 + $36,000 = $340,000.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an intangible asset that is subject to the recoverability test?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A patent.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Goodwill.

choices.label: C
choices.text: R&D costs for a patent.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A trademark with indefinite useful life.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-35, the recoverability test (comparing carrying value to undiscounted future cash flows) applies to long-lived assets held and used with finite useful lives, such as patents. Goodwill and indefinite-lived intangibles (trademarks with indefinite life) are tested for impairment under ASC 350 using a fair-value-based test вҖ” not the recoverability test. R&D costs are expensed as incurred and are not capitalized assets subject to impairment testing.

questions.stem: Which of the following expenditures qualifies for asset capitalization?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cost of materials used in prototype testing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Costs of testing a prototype and modifying its design.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Salaries of engineering staff developing a new product.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Legal costs associated with obtaining a patent on a new product.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 730, R&D costs вҖ” including materials used in prototypes, prototype testing, design modifications, and engineering salaries related to new product development вҖ” are expensed as incurred. However, legal costs directly associated with obtaining a patent (filing fees, attorney fees) are capitalized as an intangible asset (ASC 350-30) because they represent the cost to acquire a legal right.

questions.stem: Northstar Co. acquired a registered trademark for $600,000. The trademark has a remaining legal life of five years but can be renewed every 10 years for a nominal fee. Northstar expects to renew the trademark indefinitely. What amount of amortization expense should Northstar record for the trademark in the current year?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-30, intangible assets with indefinite useful lives are not amortized. Since Northstar intends to renew the trademark indefinitely at a nominal cost, the trademark has an indefinite useful life and is not subject to amortization. Instead, it is tested for impairment annually (ASC 350-30-35). The legal life of five years is irrelevant when renewal is expected indefinitely.

questions.stem: A company began developing computer software to be sold as a separate product on January 1, Year 1. During the planning, coding, and testing phases, the company incurred $1,300,000 of costs. On June 30, Year 1, the product was determined to be technologically feasible. The company began producing product masters of the software and incurred an additional $750,000 of costs from July 1, Year 1, through September 30, Year 1. After the software was available for release on October 1, Year 1, the company incurred an additional $275,000 of costs relating to maintenance and customer support. What amount of software-related costs should be capitalized?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 985-20 (software to be sold), costs incurred prior to establishing technological feasibility ($1,300,000) are expensed as R&D. Costs incurred from technological feasibility to the product being available for release ($750,000) are capitalized. Post-release costs for maintenance and customer support ($275,000) are expensed when incurred. Therefore only $750,000 is capitalized.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements is correct concerning start-up costs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Costs of start-up activities, including organization costs, should be expensed as incurred.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Costs of start-up activities, including organization costs, should be capitalized and expensed only if an impairment exists.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Costs of start-up activities, including organization costs, should be capitalized and amortized on a straight-line basis over the lesser of the estimated economic life of the company or 60 months.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Costs of start-up activities should be capitalized and amortized on a straight-line basis over the lesser of the estimated economic life of the company or 60 months, while organization costs should be expensed as incurred.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 720-15, all start-up costs вҖ” including organization costs вҖ” must be expensed as incurred under U.S. GAAP. There is no option to capitalize, defer, or amortize start-up costs. The 60-month amortization described in some choices reflects outdated guidance or tax accounting rules, not current U.S. GAAP.

questions.stem: Tech Co. bought a trademark on January 2, Year 2. The intangible was being amortized over its useful life of 40 years. The carrying value at the beginning of Year 2 was $38,000. It was determined during the current year that the cash flow from the trademark will be generated indefinitely at the current level. What amount should Tech report as amortization expense on its December 31, Year 4, income statement?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-30, when a company determines that an intangible asset previously assigned a finite useful life now has an indefinite useful life, amortization ceases and the asset is reclassified as an indefinite-lived intangible. The question states this determination was made 'during the current year' (Year 4). Going forward (including Year 4's income statement), no amortization is recorded. The asset is instead tested annually for impairment.

questions.stem: Crestman Co. exchanged inventory with Dandy Co. in a transaction that lacks commercial substance. Crestman's inventory had a cost of $50,000 and a fair value of $80,000. Crestman received inventory with a fair value of $56,000 plus $24,000 cash. What amount of gain should Crestman recognize on this exchange?
questions.explanation: When an exchange lacks commercial substance, gains are generally deferred; however, when monetary consideration (cash) is received, a proportional gain is recognized. Total gain = fair value received ($80,000) вҲ’ book value given ($50,000) = $30,000. Proportion of cash received = $24,000 Г· $80,000 = 30%. Recognized gain = 30% Г— $30,000 = $9,000.

questions.stem: A company moved its operations to a new building. The old building was actively marketed for sale and is expected to be sold within 4 months. Each of the following statements is correct regarding the old building, EXCEPT:

choices.label: A
choices.text: It will be reclassified as an asset held for sale.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It will be classified as a current asset.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It will no longer be depreciated.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It will be valued at historical cost.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-45, when a long-lived asset meets held-for-sale criteria (actively marketed, expected sale within 12 months), it is reclassified as an asset held for sale, classified as a current asset, and depreciation ceases. However, the asset is NOT valued at historical cost; it is measured at the lower of its carrying amount or fair value less costs to sell. Valuing at historical cost is therefore incorrect.

questions.stem: Cole Co. is constructing a building. During construction, interest on specific construction debt amounted to $50,000. Interest on other borrowings amounted to $20,000. The interest computed on the weighted-average accumulated expenditures was $40,000. How much interest should Cole capitalize?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 835-20, the amount of interest to capitalize equals the lesser of actual interest incurred or avoidable interest. Actual total interest = $50,000 (specific debt) + $20,000 (other borrowings) = $70,000. Avoidable interest, computed on the weighted-average accumulated expenditures, is $40,000. Since $40,000 < $70,000, Cole capitalizes $40,000.

questions.stem: Ocean Corp. carried comprehensive insurance on its waterfront warehouse. The warehouse was destroyed by a storm. The insurance policy had a deductible of $50,000. Dismantling cost was $20,000. The replacement cost of the warehouse was $1,100,000 and its carrying amount was $300,000. What amount should Ocean report as a gain, presented as a separate component of income from continuing operations?
questions.explanation: The insurance proceeds equal the replacement cost less the deductible: $1,100,000 вҲ’ $50,000 = $1,050,000. The gain on involuntary conversion = insurance proceeds вҲ’ carrying amount вҲ’ dismantling costs = $1,050,000 вҲ’ $300,000 вҲ’ $20,000 = $730,000. This gain is reported as a separate component of income from continuing operations.

questions.stem: A company's fiscal year ends June 30. Construction began April 1. Construction-in-progress balances were: April 30 = $500,000; May 31 = $800,000; June 30 = $1,500,000. The applicable interest rate is 5% for the AprilвҖ“June period. What amount of interest should be capitalized at June 30?
questions.explanation: The weighted-average accumulated expenditures are approximated using the average of the three month-end CIP balances: ($500,000 + $800,000 + $1,500,000) Г· 3 = $933,333. Capitalized interest for the three-month period (AprilвҖ“June) at 5% = $933,333 Г— 5% Г— 3/12 = $11,667 (rounded to $11,666).

questions.stem:
Quick Co. acquired several assets from a competitor for a lump sum of $200,000. The fair values of the individual assets were:

| Asset | Fair Value |
|---|---:|
| Inventory | $50,000 |
| Land | $50,000 |
| Building | $150,000 |

What amount should Quick record as the cost of the building?
questions.explanation: When multiple assets are acquired for a lump-sum price, the cost is allocated based on relative fair values. Total fair value = $50,000 + $50,000 + $150,000 = $250,000. Building's proportion = $150,000 Г· $250,000 = 60%. Cost allocated to building = 60% Г— $200,000 = $120,000.

questions.stem: Finch Co. had an asset retirement obligation (ARO) balance at the beginning of the year of $257,000. During the year, Finch acquired new obligations with a discounted value of $68,000, paid $87,000 toward settlement of existing obligations, and recorded accretion expense of $26,000. What is Finch's ARO balance at year end?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 410-20, the asset retirement obligation balance changes as follows: beginning balance $257,000, plus new obligations at fair value (discounted) $68,000, minus settlements paid $87,000, plus accretion expense $26,000 = $257,000 + $68,000 вҲ’ $87,000 + $26,000 = $264,000.

questions.stem: Sen Inc. incurred the following costs related to land: Purchase price $600,000 (includes existing building valued at 10% of purchase price); Demolition of existing building $15,000; Debris removal and grading $8,000; Fine for failure to obtain demolition permit $2,000; Surveying $12,000; Excavation for basement of new building $20,000; Building permits $10,000. What amount should Sen capitalize to land?
questions.explanation: Land costs include the purchase price ($600,000), demolition of the existing structure ($15,000), debris removal and grading ($8,000), and surveying ($12,000), totaling $635,000. The fine for failure to obtain a demolition permit ($2,000) is not capitalizable because it provides no future economic benefit. Excavation for the new building's basement ($20,000) and building permits ($10,000) are costs of the new building, not the land.

questions.stem: Both an asset group and goodwill assigned to a reporting unit need impairment testing. Which of the following statements is correct regarding the order and treatment of impairment testing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The other asset group should be tested for an impairment loss before goodwill is tested.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Impairment testing may be conducted concurrently.

choices.label: C
choices.text: If the other asset group is impaired, the loss should not be recognized prior to goodwill being tested.

choices.label: D
choices.text: If goodwill is impaired, the loss should be recognized prior to testing the other assets.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-20-35, when both other long-lived asset groups and goodwill in a reporting unit require impairment testing, the other asset group must be tested and any impairment loss recognized first. This is because reducing the asset group's carrying amount affects the overall net assets of the reporting unit, which then feeds into the goodwill impairment test.

questions.stem: Newt Co. sold a warehouse that had been used in its operations and used the proceeds to purchase a new warehouse. The excess of the proceeds over the carrying amount of the old warehouse should be reported as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reduction of the cost of the new warehouse.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gain from discontinued operations, net of income taxes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Part of continuing operations.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Part of other comprehensive income.

questions.explanation: The gain on the sale of a warehouse used in operations is recognized in income from continuing operations in the period of sale. There is no basis under U.S. GAAP to net the gain against the cost of the replacement asset, report it as discontinued operations (which requires disposal of a component of an entity), or include it in other comprehensive income.

questions.stem:
Are restorations of carrying value permitted for long-lived assets if fair value increases after an impairment loss has been recognized?

                Held-for-use    Held-for-disposal

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes               No

choices.label: B
choices.text: No                Yes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes               Yes

choices.label: D
choices.text: No                No

questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-35, impairment losses on long-lived assets held for use are not reversed вҖ” no restoration of carrying value is permitted. For long-lived assets classified as held for sale (held for disposal), subsequent increases in fair value less costs to sell are recognized as a gain, but only up to the amount of the previously recognized impairment loss. Therefore, restoration is not permitted for held-for-use assets but is permitted for held-for-disposal assets.

questions.stem: A company received donated nonmonetary assets in a nonreciprocal transfer. Which of the following statements is correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The donated assets are reported as part of operating income.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The donated assets are recorded at the historical cost of the asset received.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The donated assets are recorded at the fair value of the asset received.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The donated assets result in recognition of a gain or loss.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 845-10-30, nonmonetary assets received in a nonreciprocal transfer (donation) are recorded at the fair value of the asset received at the date of the donation. They are not recorded at the donor's historical cost, and the credit is to revenue or contribution income rather than a gain or loss.

questions.stem: A company borrowed $300,000 at 10% on January 1, Year 1, specifically to finance construction of an office building. On January 1, the company paid $120,000 for land on which to build. On September 1, a progress payment of $150,000 was made to the contractor. What amount of interest should be capitalized for Year 1?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 835-20, interest is capitalized on weighted-average accumulated expenditures for qualifying assets. The land ($120,000 on January 1) is outstanding the full 12 months; the September 1 progress payment ($150,000) is outstanding 4 months. Weighted-average accumulated expenditures = $120,000 Г— 12/12 + $150,000 Г— 4/12 = $120,000 + $50,000 = $170,000. Capitalized interest = $170,000 Г— 10% = $17,000, which is less than actual interest of $30,000.

questions.stem: A company placed an offshore oil platform into service. The platform cost $400 million and must be dismantled in 10 years at an estimated cost of $20 million. The discounted present value of the dismantling obligation at the credit-adjusted risk-free rate is $9 million. What liability and what expense should be recorded when the platform is placed in service?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Liability $0 / Expense $0

choices.label: B
choices.text: Liability $9,000,000 / Expense $0

choices.label: C
choices.text: Liability $9,000,000 / Expense $9,000,000

choices.label: D
choices.text: Liability $20,000,000 / Expense $20,000,000

questions.explanation: Under ASC 410-20, an asset retirement obligation (ARO) is initially measured at fair value вҖ” the discounted present value of $9,000,000 вҖ” and recorded as a liability. The corresponding debit increases the carrying amount of the asset (not an expense). Therefore, at initial recognition, a liability of $9,000,000 is recorded and no expense is recognized; the $9,000,000 is capitalized into the asset and will be depreciated over the asset's useful life.

questions.stem: At the beginning of the year, an asset retirement obligation had a fair value of $100,000. The risk-free rate is 5% and the credit-adjusted risk-free rate is 10%. What is the accretion expense for the year?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 410-20-35, accretion expense is calculated by applying the credit-adjusted risk-free interest rate to the beginning balance of the asset retirement obligation. Accretion expense = $100,000 Г— 10% = $10,000. The risk-free rate (5%) is not used; the rate locked in at initial recognition вҖ” the credit-adjusted risk-free rate of 10% вҖ” is applied.

questions.stem: Which of the following is LEAST likely to be an indicator of potential impairment of a long-lived asset?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An operating segment is projecting continuing losses.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Independently verified fair value of similar assets is comparable to the asset's carrying value.

choices.label: C
choices.text: New laws are causing the company to dispose of the asset earlier than expected.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Competitive pressures are causing technological obsolescence of the asset.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-35, indicators of potential impairment include significant declines in market value, adverse changes in the business or legal environment, current or projected operating losses, and expected early disposal. When independently verified fair value of similar assets is comparable to the asset's carrying value, there is no gap between fair value and book value вҖ” this is the least likely indicator of impairment among the choices.

questions.stem: Sylvester Co. borrowed $900,000 at 12% on December 3, Year 4 specifically to construct a building. At the beginning of Year 5, the construction-in-progress balance was $536,000. During Year 5, additional costs were incurred: $240,000 on March 1 and $100,000 on December 31. Actual interest paid during Year 5 was $108,000. How much interest should Sylvester capitalize for Year 5?
questions.explanation: Weighted-average accumulated expenditures: $536,000 beginning balance Г— 12/12 = $536,000; March 1 addition $240,000 Г— 10/12 = $200,000; December 31 addition $100,000 Г— 0/12 = $0. Total WAAE = $736,000. Avoidable interest = $736,000 Г— 12% = $88,320. Since avoidable interest ($88,320) is less than actual interest paid ($108,000), Sylvester capitalizes $88,320.

questions.stem: Lyle Co. purchased a manufacturing facility on January 1. After remodeling, the facility was ready for use on March 1, and production began April 1. Interest costs were as follows: January 1вҖ“March 1: building $10,000, remodeling $2,000. March 1вҖ“April 1: building $5,000, remodeling $3,000. How much interest should Lyle capitalize?
questions.explanation: Interest capitalization continues until the asset is substantially complete and ready for its intended use, which occurred on March 1 when remodeling was completed. Building interest from January 1 to March 1 = $10,000; remodeling interest from January 1 to March 1 = $2,000; total capitalized = $12,000. The $5,000 building interest and $3,000 remodeling interest incurred after March 1 are expensed because the facility was already ready for use.

questions.stem: A corporation purchased 10 acres of land. The following costs were incurred: purchase price $1,000,000; real estate taxes in arrears $15,000; debt issuance costs $2,000; attorney fee for title search $5,000. At what amount should the corporation record the land?
questions.explanation: Land costs include the purchase price ($1,000,000), real estate taxes in arrears assumed by the buyer ($15,000 вҖ” a cost of acquiring clear title), and attorney fees for the title search ($5,000), totaling $1,020,000. Debt issuance costs ($2,000) are costs associated with obtaining financing, not acquiring the land, and are capitalized separately as a contra-liability and amortized under ASC 835-30.

questions.stem: Last year, Katt Co. reduced the carrying value of its long-lived assets from $120,000 to $100,000 due to an impairment loss. In the current year, the fair value of those assets increased to $130,000. What amount should Katt record as a restoration of the previously recognized impairment loss?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 360-10-35, impairment losses recognized for long-lived assets held for use are not reversed if the asset's fair value subsequently increases. Katt's assets are held for use in operations, so even though fair value increased to $130,000, no restoration of the previously recognized $20,000 impairment loss is permitted. The carrying amount remains at $100,000.

questions.subtopic: Capitalized Interest вҖ“ Specific Borrowing with Offsetting Investment Income
questions.stem: Herr Inc. has a fiscal year ending April 30. On May 1, of the previous year, Herr borrowed $10,000,000 at 15% to finance construction of its own building. Repayments of the loan are to commence the month following completion of the building. During the current year ended April 30, expenditures for the partially completed structure totaled $6,000,000. These expenditures were incurred evenly throughout the year. Interest earned on the unexpended portion of the loan amounted to $400,000 for the year. How much should be shown as capitalized interest on Herr ' s financial statements at April 30?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 835-20 (formerly SFAS 34), interest cost to be capitalized equals the avoidable interest on the average accumulated expenditures. Since the $6,000,000 was incurred evenly throughout the year, the average accumulated expenditures = $6,000,000 Г· 2 = $3,000,000. Interest to capitalize = $3,000,000 Г— 15% = $450,000. However, under the specific-borrowing rule, interest earned on the temporarily invested unexpended portion of the borrowing ($400,000) must be offset against the interest cost to be capitalized. Net capitalized interest = $450,000 вҖ“ $400,000 = $50,000.

questions.subtopic: Capitalized Interest вҖ“ Average Accumulated Expenditures Basis
questions.stem: A company is constructing an asset for its own use. Construction began in the previous year. The asset is being financed entirely with a specific new borrowing. Construction expenditures were made last year and this year at the end of each quarter. The total amount of interest cost capitalized in the current year should be determined by applying the interest rate on the specific new borrowing to the

choices.label: A
choices.text: Total accumulated expenditures for the asset in both years

choices.label: B
choices.text: Average accumulated expenditures for the asset in both years

choices.label: C
choices.text: Average expenditures for the asset in the current year

choices.label: D
choices.text: Total expenditures for the asset in the current year

questions.explanation: Under ASC 835-20, the amount of interest to capitalize each year is determined by applying the borrowing rate to the average accumulated expenditures for the qualifying asset during the capitalization period. Average accumulated expenditures include both prior-year and current-year expenditures weighted by the fraction of the year they were outstanding during the current period. Because expenditures were made at the end of each quarter in both years, the current-year calculation must incorporate the weighted-average of all accumulated expenditures in both years, not just the current year's expenditures.

questions.subtopic: Nonmonetary Exchange вҖ“ Commercial Substance, Fair Value Recording
questions.stem: In an exchange with commercial substance, Vey Co. traded equipment with an original cost of $100,000 and accumulated depreciation of $40,000 for similar productive equipment with a fair value of $120,000. In addition, Vey received $30,000 cash in connection with this exchange. What should be Vey ' s carrying amount for the new equipment received on the day of exchange?
questions.explanation: In an exchange with commercial substance, both gains and losses are recognized in full, and the new asset is recorded at its fair value under ASC 360. The fair value of the old equipment surrendered = fair value of assets received = fair value of new equipment ($120,000) + cash received ($30,000) = $150,000. Book value of old equipment = $100,000 вҖ“ $40,000 = $60,000. Gain = $150,000 вҖ“ $60,000 = $90,000 (recognized in full). The new equipment is recorded at its fair value of $120,000.

questions.stem: Spiro Corp. uses the sum-of-the-years ' -digits method to depreciate equipment purchased in January of the current year for $20,000. The estimated salvage value of the equipment is $2,000 and the estimated useful life is four years. What should Spiro report as the asset ' s carrying amount as of December 31 in the third year?
questions.explanation: Sum-of-the-years'-digits for a 4-year life: SYD = 4+3+2+1 = 10. Depreciable amount = $20,000 вҖ“ $2,000 = $18,000. Year 1 depreciation = $18,000 Г— 4/10 = $7,200. Year 2 depreciation = $18,000 Г— 3/10 = $5,400. Year 3 depreciation = $18,000 Г— 2/10 = $3,600. Accumulated depreciation at end of Year 3 = $7,200 + $5,400 + $3,600 = $16,200. Carrying amount = $20,000 вҖ“ $16,200 = $3,800.

questions.stem: Rye Co. purchased a machine with a four-year estimated useful life and an estimated 10% salvage value for $80,000 on January 1 of the current year. In its income statement for the third year, what would Rye report as the depreciation expense using the double-declining-balance method?
questions.explanation: Double-declining-balance (DDB) rate = 2 Г· 4 years = 50%. DDB does not deduct salvage value in the annual calculation but stops depreciating when the book value reaches salvage value ($80,000 Г— 10% = $8,000). Year 1: $80,000 Г— 50% = $40,000; book value = $40,000. Year 2: $40,000 Г— 50% = $20,000; book value = $20,000. Year 3: $20,000 Г— 50% = $10,000; however, the resulting book value would be $10,000, which is above the $8,000 salvage floor, so the full $10,000 is deducted. Year 3 depreciation = $10,000.

questions.subtopic: Composite Depreciation вҖ“ Composite Life Calculation
