questions.stem: A schedule of machinery owned by Lester Manufacturing Company is presented below: Total Cost Estimated Salvage Value Estimated Life in Years Machine A $550,000 $50,000 20 Machine B 200,000 20,000 15 Machine C 40,000 в”Җ 5 Lester computes depreciation on the straight-line method. Based upon the information presented, the composite life of these assets (in years) should be
questions.explanation: Composite life = (Total depreciable cost) Г· (Total annual straight-line depreciation). Machine A: ($550,000 вҖ“ $50,000) Г· 20 = $25,000/yr. Machine B: ($200,000 вҖ“ $20,000) Г· 15 = $12,000/yr. Machine C: ($40,000 вҖ“ $0) Г· 5 = $8,000/yr. Total annual SL depreciation = $45,000. Total depreciable cost = $500,000 + $180,000 + $40,000 = $720,000. Composite life = $720,000 Г· $45,000 = 16.0 years.

questions.subtopic: Composite and Group Depreciation вҖ“ Asset Retirement Accounting
questions.stem: When equipment is retired, accumulated depreciation is debited for the original cost less any residual recovery under which of the following depreciation methods? Composite depreciation Group depreciation

choices.label: A
choices.text: No No

choices.label: B
choices.text: No Yes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes No

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes Yes

questions.explanation: Under both composite and group depreciation methods, when an individual asset is retired, no gain or loss is recognized. Instead, the accumulated depreciation account is debited for the difference between the original cost of the asset and any salvage or residual proceeds received. The asset account (cost) is credited, and the proceeds (if any) are debited to cash. This treatment applies equally to composite depreciation (dissimilar assets grouped together) and group depreciation (similar assets grouped together).

questions.subtopic: R&D Costs вҖ“ Equipment with No Alternative Future Use
questions.stem: On January 1, Jambon purchased equipment for use in developing a new product. Jambon uses the straight-line depreciation method. The equipment could provide benefits over a 10-year period. However, the new product development is expected to take five years, and the equipment can be used only for this project. Jambon ' s expense for the year equals

choices.label: A
choices.text: The total cost of the equipment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: One-fifth of the cost of the equipment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: One-tenth of the cost of the equipment.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Zero.

questions.explanation: Under ASC 730-10, equipment acquired solely for use in a specific R&D project with no alternative future use is expensed in full as research and development cost in the period incurred. Because the equipment can only be used for this one product development project and has no alternative future uses, the entire cost must be expensed immediately вҖ” not capitalized and depreciated. The fact that the equipment 'could provide benefits over a 10-year period' is irrelevant when it has no alternative future use beyond this single project.

questions.subtopic: Goodwill вҖ“ Purchased vs. Internally Generated
questions.stem: On June 30, Union Inc. purchased goodwill of $125,000 when it acquired the net assets of Apex Corp. During the year, Union incurred additional costs of developing goodwill, by training Apex employees ($50,000) and hiring additional Apex employees ($25,000). Union ' s December 31 balance sheet should report goodwill of
questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-20, only goodwill acquired in a business combination is recognized and capitalized on the balance sheet. Internally generated goodwill вҖ” including costs to develop, maintain, or restore goodwill вҖ” is expensed as incurred. Training existing employees ($50,000) and hiring additional employees ($25,000) are costs of internally developing goodwill and must be expensed, not capitalized. Therefore, only the $125,000 of purchased goodwill from the acquisition of Apex Corp. is reported on the balance sheet.

questions.subtopic: Software Development Costs вҖ“ Post-Technological-Feasibility Capitalization
questions.stem: In Pitt ' s December 31 balance sheet, what amount should be capitalized as software cost, subject to amortization?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 350-40 and ASC 985-20, software costs incurred after technological feasibility is established (and before the product is available for general release) are capitalized. Costs incurred before technological feasibility вҖ” such as planning, research, and preliminary project activities вҖ” are expensed as incurred. In the standard AICPA version of the Pitt scenario, only $54,000 of costs were incurred after technological feasibility was established, making $54,000 the amount to capitalize and subsequently amortize. Costs totaling more than $54,000 include pre-feasibility amounts that must be expensed.

questions.subtopic: Capitalized Software Costs вҖ“ Amortization and NRV Test
questions.stem: On December 31, year 1, Bit Co. had capitalized costs for a new computer software product with an economic life of five years. Sales for year 2 were 30 percent of expected total sales of the software. At December 31, year 2, the software had a net realizable value equal to 90 percent of the capitalized cost. What percentage of the original capitalized cost should be reported as the net amount on Bit ' s December 31, year 2 balance sheet?
questions.explanation: Under ASC 985-20, capitalized software costs are amortized using the greater of: (1) the ratio of current-year gross revenues to total expected gross revenues over the life of the product, or (2) the straight-line method over the remaining estimated economic life. The ratio method gives 30% amortization (30% of total expected sales). The straight-line method gives 1/5 = 20% per year. The greater amount is 30%, so 30% is amortized in Year 2. Remaining net carrying amount = 100% вҖ“ 30% = 70%. The net realizable value (NRV) test requires that the carrying amount not exceed NRV (90%). Since 70% < 90%, the NRV floor is not breached, and the balance sheet amount is 70% of original capitalized cost.

questions.stem: Oak Co., a newly formed corporation, incurred the following expenditures related to land and building: County assessment for sewer lines $ 2,500 Title search fees 625 Cash paid for land with building to be demolished 135,000 Excavation for construction of basement 21,000 Removal of old building $21,000 less salvage of $5,000 16,000 At what amount should Oak record the land?
questions.explanation: All costs necessary to acquire land and prepare it for its intended use are capitalized as part of the cost of land. The land cost includes: purchase price $135,000 + county sewer assessment $2,500 + title search fees $625 + net cost of demolishing the old building ($21,000 вҲ’ $5,000 salvage) $16,000 = $154,125. The excavation for the basement is a cost of constructing the building, not of the land, and is excluded from the land account (ASC 360-10-25).

questions.stem: Cody Corp. incurred the following costs during the year: Design of tools, jigs, molds, and dies involving new technology $125,000 Modification of the formulation of a process $160,000 Trouble-shooting in connection with breakdowns during commercial production $100,000 Adaptation of an existing capability to a particular customer ' s need as part of a continuing commercial activity $110,000 In its year-end income statement, Cody should report research and development expense of
questions.explanation: Under ASC 730-10, research and development costs must be expensed as incurred. Design of tools, jigs, molds, and dies involving new technology ($125,000) qualifies as R&D because it involves developing new technology. Modification of the formulation of a process ($160,000) is an R&D activity. Together these total $285,000. Trouble-shooting during commercial production ($100,000) is excluded from R&D because it relates to ongoing production operations, not new development. Adaptation of an existing capability for a specific customer as part of continuing commercial activity ($110,000) is also excluded because it is a commercial activity, not research or development (ASC 730-10-25).

questions.stem: During the year, Jase Co. incurred research and development costs of $136,000 in its laboratories relating to a patent that was granted on July 1. Costs of registering the patent equaled $34,000. The patent ' s legal life is 20 years, and its estimated economic life is 10 years. In its December 31 balance sheet, what amount should Jase report as patent, net of accumulated amortization?
questions.explanation: The $136,000 of R&D costs incurred in the laboratory are expensed as incurred under ASC 730-10 and are not capitalized as part of the patent. Only the $34,000 registration cost is capitalized. A patent is amortized over the shorter of its legal life (20 years) and its estimated economic life (10 years), so the amortization period is 10 years. The patent was granted on July 1, so amortization covers 6 months in the current year: $34,000 Г· 10 Г— 6/12 = $1,700. Net patent value at December 31 = $34,000 вҲ’ $1,700 = $32,300 (ASC 350-30-35).

questions.subtopic: Computer Software Costs вҖ“ Inventory Classification
questions.stem: In Pitt ' s December 31 balance sheet, what amount should be reported in inventory?
questions.explanation: For computer software developed for sale, costs after technological feasibility are capitalized, while duplication and packaging costs are classified as inventory. Under ASC 350-40/985-20, once the product master is complete, the costs to duplicate (copy) and package the software units constitute inventory. The duplication costs of $25,000 and packaging costs of $9,000 total $34,000, which should be reported as inventory. The product masters ($15,000) and post-feasibility coding/testing costs are capitalized software assets, not inventory.
